## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — State Machine of TONR

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle: Idle State — EN=0, TT=0, DN=0, ACC holds prior value
    Timing: Timing State — EN=1, TT=1, ACC increments by TB
    Held: Held State — EN=0, TT=0, ACC frozen
    Tripped: Tripped State — DN=1, EN=1, ACC frozen at PRE
    Reset: Reset State — ACC forced to 0

    Idle --> Timing: IN becomes TRUE
    Timing --> Held: IN becomes FALSE
    Held --> Timing: IN becomes TRUE again
    Timing --> Tripped: ACC reaches PRE
    Held --> Tripped: ACC already at PRE and IN returns TRUE
    Tripped --> Reset: RES instruction fires
    Reset --> Idle: RES coil de-energizes
    Timing --> Reset: RES instruction fires
```

## 4.3 Ladder Logic Schematic (Text Representation)

```
   X0 (Start)        T4:1               T4:1.RES
   ─┤ ├──────────────( TONR )───────────┤ ├─────── ( RES )
                       │                  │
                       │ EN              (driven by Y0
                       │ TT               interlock or
                       │ DN               operator PB)
                       │
                     .PRE = 50
                     .TB  = 0.1 s
                     .ACC = retained across scans
```

**Reading the Ladder:**
- Rung 1 (left): When `X0` is TRUE, timer `T4:1` accumulates time.
- Rung 2 (right): The `RES` (reset) coil clears the timer.
- The `DN` bit (`T4:1.DN`) can be used elsewhere as a permissive for downstream logic.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Q1.** Define a Retentive Timer. How does it differ from a Non-Retentive ON-Delay timer?

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 + 2):**

- **Definition (1 Mark):** A Retentive Timer is a PLC timer instruction that **retains its accumulated (ACC) value in memory** even when its enabling input becomes false or when power to the PLC is cycled. It must be explicitly cleared using a **Reset (RES)** instruction.

- **Difference (2 Marks):**
  1. A Non-Retentive timer (TON) **automatically resets its ACC to zero** when the input goes false, whereas a Retentive timer (TONR) **freezes** the ACC value.
  2. A Non-Retentive timer does not require a separate reset coil; a Retentive timer **must be reset** using an explicit `RES` instruction before it can be reused.

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]** **Q2.** List the status bits of a retentive timer and explain the function of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark each):**

| Bit | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **EN** | Enable Bit | TRUE while the timer's input rung condition is TRUE. |
| **TT** | Timer-Timing Bit | TRUE only while the timer is actively counting. |
| **DN** | Done Bit | TRUE when `ACC ≥ PRE`; latches until reset. |

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 1 Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> When drawing timing diagrams, students frequently forget to **label the time axis with units (s or ms)** and omit the **scan-by-scan increments** on the ACC waveform. Always annotate: (i) the `PRE` value as a **horizontal dashed line**, (ii) the `ACC` as a **staircase rising only during ON periods**, and (iii) the `DN` bit **as a step function that latches**, not pulses. Failure to latch `DN` correctly is the single most common mark-deduction point.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style]*

**(a)** With a neat timing diagram, explain the operation of a **Retentive ON-Delay Timer (TONR)**. Clearly label the EN, TT, DN bits and the ACC waveform. Assume `PRE = 5 s` and `TB = 1 s`, with the input pulsed for 2 s ON, 2 s OFF, 2 s ON, 2 s OFF, 2 s ON. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write the ladder logic program for a **motor total-run-hour indicator**. The motor is started by `I:0/0` and stopped by `I:0/1`. A TONR with `TB = 1 s` and `PRE = 3600` counts run hours. When the preset is reached, output `O:0/0` (Maintenance Due lamp) must turn ON. The maintenance personnel press `I:0/2` to acknowledge and reset the timer. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part A(a):**

The timing diagram should show:
- `IN`: square wave with 2 s ON / 2 s OFF / 2 s ON / 2 s OFF / 2 s ON pattern.
- `ACC`: starts at `0`, rises in **1 s steps** during each ON interval, **holds flat** during each OFF interval.
- After the third ON pulse, `ACC` reaches `5 s = PRE`, so `DN` latches to `1`.
- `TT = 1` only during the rising segments; `EN = IN` waveform.

**Scan-by-scan trace (5 marks for diagram + 2 marks for explanation):**

| Time (s) | IN | EN | TT | ACC | DN |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 → 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 → 2 | 0 |
| 2 → 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **2 (held)** | 0 |
| 4 → 6 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 2 → 4 | 0 |
| 6 → 8 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **4 (held)** | 0 |
| 8 → 10 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 4 → 5 | **1 (latched)** |

> **Crucial Behavior:** Notice the ACC value at `t = 2 s` is `2`, at `t = 4 s` it is still `2` (frozen), and at `t = 6 s` it resumes from `2` to `4`. This is the **retentive property**.

#### **Model Solution — Part A(b):**

```
   Rung 1: Motor Seal-In Ladder
   I:0/0        M1 (Motor Seal-in)
   ─┤ ├──────────( )──────┐
                          │
   I:0/1                  │
   ─┤/├─────────────────── ┘
                          │
                          │
   M1            T4:1                T4:1
   ─┤ ├─────────( TONR )────────────( )─────────── O:0/0 (Lamp)
                  PRE = 3600
                  TB  = 1.0 s

   Rung 2: Reset (Acknowledge)
   I:0/2              T4:1.RES
   ─┤ ├────────────────( RES )
```

**Explanation (7 Marks breakdown: 3 for rung 1, 2 for rung 2, 2 for logic):**

1. **Rung 1 (3 Marks):** The motor is started by `I:0/0` and latched via the seal-in contact `M1`. It is unlatched by pressing Stop `I:0/1`. While the motor runs (`M1 = 1`), the TONR `T4:1` accumulates seconds. After 3600 seconds (1 hour) of *cumulative* motor run time, `T4:1.DN = 1`, energizing the maintenance lamp `O:0/0`.

2. **Rung 2 (2 Marks):** The maintenance technician presses `I:0/2` to fire the `RES` coil of timer `T4:1`, clearing the accumulator and turning off the lamp.

3. **Logic Justification (2 Marks):** Because `M1` is the rung condition feeding the TONR, every intermittent motor start (e.g., due to overload recovery) will continue to add to the **total runtime** without resetting—exactly the behavior of a retentive counter.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Style]*

**(a)** Compare **Retentive and Non-Retentive timers** with respect to: (i) memory retention across input cycles, (ii) requirement of a reset instruction, (iii) behavior on power failure, (iv) typical industrial applications. Provide one real-world example for each timer type. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A chemical reactor must be heated for a **total accumulated time of 120 minutes**. The heater is controlled by output `O:0/1`, and a temperature interlock input `I:0/3` goes low momentarily if the heater overheats. Design a PLC program using a TONR such that:
   - The 120-minute countdown **continues from its current value** when the interlock clears.
   - When `ACC = PRE`, output `O:0/2` (Cycle Complete) is latched.
   - A reset button `I:0/4` clears the cycle for the next batch. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part B(a):**

| Criterion | Retentive (TONR) | Non-Retentive (TON) |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Memory across input cycles | **Retains ACC** when IN = 0 | **Resets ACC to 0** when IN = 0 |
| (ii) Reset instruction | **Mandatory** explicit RES | Not required |
| (iii) Power failure | **Retains ACC** (battery/RAM backed) | **Loses ACC** |
| (iv) Application example | Totalizing run-hours of a pump | One-shot startup delay for a conveyor |

> **Industrial Examples (2 Marks):**
> - **Retentive:** Cement kiln total heating time across intermittent power pulses.
> - **Non-Retentive:** Compressor startup delay to prevent simultaneous motor inrush.

#### **Model Solution — Part B(b):**

```
   Rung 1: Heater Control with Interlock
   Start_PB        I:0/3 (Interlock OK)    O:0/1 (Heater)
   ─┤ ├──────────────┤ ├────────────────────( )─────┐
                                                     │
   Stop_PB                                         │
   ─┤/├─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                                     │
                                                     │
                  T4:2
   ──────────────( TONR )
                  PRE = 7200
                  TB  = 1.0 s
                  (7200 × 1 s = 120 minutes)

   Rung 2: Cycle Complete Latch
   T4:2.DN        Cycle_Done (Latch)
   ─┤ ├────────────( L )──────────────┐ O:0/2
                                      │
   ────────────────────────────────────┘

   Rung 3: Manual Reset
   I:0/4                 T4:2.RES        Cycle_Done.U
   ─┤ ├──────────────────( RES )────────( U )
```

**Explanation (7 Marks — 3 + 2 + 2):**

1. **Rung 1 (3 Marks):** The heater `O:0/1` is energized by Start PB **AND** the interlock `I:0/3`. The TONR `T4:2` is wired to run whenever the heater is ON. If `I:0/3` momentarily drops, the heater turns OFF, but the TONR **retains** its accumulated minutes. When `I:0/3` re-asserts, counting resumes.

2. **Rung 2 (2 Marks):** When `T4:2.DN = 1`, the `Cycle_Done` bit latches and energizes `O:0/2` (Cycle Complete indicator).

3. **Rung 3 (2 Marks):** Pressing `I:0/4` resets both the TONR and the cycle-done latch, priming the reactor for the next batch.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error:** Students often connect the TONR directly across `O:0/1`'s rung **before** the interlock contact. This causes the timer to **keep counting during heater-off periods** — defeating the purpose of the interlock. Always place the interlock **upstream** of the timer coil.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorize Before Exam:**

- **Retentive Timer (TONR):** Retains `.ACC` value when input goes false. Requires **explicit `.RES` coil** to zero the accumulator.
- **Non-Retentive Timer (TON):** Automatically resets `.ACC` to `0` whenever the input rung is false. **No reset required**.
- **Siemens Equivalent:** SS = Retentive ON-Delay; SA = Retentive OFF-Delay.
- **Allen-Bradley Equivalent:** `TONR` instruction in RSLogix 500 / Studio 5000.
- **Key Bits:** `EN` (enable), `TT` (timer-timing), `DN` (done-latch).
- **Time Base (TB):** Resolution of each scan increment — typically **1 ms, 10 ms, 100 ms, or 1 s**.
- **Recursion Formula:** $ACC_n = ACC_{n-1} + TB \cdot \mathbb{1}(IN)$ unless RES = 1.
- **Trip Equation:** $T_{trip,ss} = \frac{PRE \cdot T_{base}}{D}$ where $D$ is the duty cycle.
- **DONE Bit:** **Latches** to 1 once `ACC ≥ PRE` — does NOT pulse.
- **Power Loss:** Retentive timers **survive** power cycles (battery/RAM backed); non-retentive ones **lose state**.
- **Applications:** Totalizing run-hours, batch timers, energy meters, fault-accumulation trips.
- **Pitfall to Avoid:** A retentive timer is **never auto-resetting** — always wire a `RES` rung for reusability.
- **Pitfall to Avoid:** Place any interlock **upstream** of the timer coil, not downstream — otherwise the timer counts during fault conditions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programming Counters - Up/Down Counter - Combining Counter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMRT604 - PLC & DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Programming Counters - Up/Down Counter - Combining Counter ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Programming Counters — Formal Definition In a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC), a **Counter** is a fundamental internal instruction that monitors and tallies the number of times an event (an input pulse, a transition, or a condition) occurs within the user program. Unlike physical hardware counters, PLC counters are **software-implemented instructions** that occupy dedicated data-table registers (typically `C5:` or `CT` files depending on the manufacturer — Allen-Bradley SLC 500, Siemens S7, Mitsubishi, etc.). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A counter is a PLC instruction used to count the number of times a rung-condition goes from **false-to-true** (rising edge transitions). The counter maintains a running tally known as the **Accumulated Value (ACC/CV)** which is continuously compared against a user-defined **Preset Value (PRE/PV)**. A counter is composed of four mandatory elements: 1. **Counter Address (Tag)** — A unique identifier in the counter file (e.g., `C5:0`). 2. **Preset Value (PRE / PV)** — The target count the user wants to reach. 3. **Accumulated Value (ACC / CV)** — The current, dynamic tally of pulses received. 4. **Control Inputs** — At minimum one counting input and one reset input. ### 1.2 The Three Classes of PLC Counters > [!IMPORTANT] > Per the KTU Module 1 syllabus, students must master all three counter variants: | Counter Type | Symbol/Instruction | Function | |---|---|---| | Up Counter | **CTU** (Count Up) | Increments ACC on every pulse | | Down Counter | **CTD** (Count Down) | Decrements ACC on every pulse from a preset load value | | Up/Down Counter | **CTUD** | Combines both — increments on one input, decrements on another | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bottling Plant" Intuition Imagine a soft-drink bottling plant where bottles pass under a **photoelectric sensor** on a conveyor belt. - **Up Counter (CTU):** Every time a bottle breaks the light beam, the sensor pulses ON. The counter tallies **bottles produced since the start of the shift**. When the count hits `1000` (preset), the `DN` (Done) flag trips, and a buzzer sounds telling the supervisor: *"Batch complete — change the flavor."* - **Down Counter (CTD):** A truck is to be loaded with `500` bottles. The operator loads the truck, and the counter starts at `500`. Each bottle that passes the sensor **subtracts one**. When `ACC = 0`, the `DN` flag trips: *"Truck full — close the tailgate."* - **Up/Down Counter (CTUD):** The plant has **two sensors** — one at the entrance (counts bottles *entering* the crate-filling station) and one at the exit (counts bottles *leaving* for quality rejection). The counter shows the **net bottles currently inside the station buffer zone**. > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid:** Think **UP** = "adding bottles in", **DOWN** = "taking bottles out". The counter's job is simply to **remember a number** while pulses stream in. ### 1.4 Why Counters Matter in Industrial Automation Counters are indispensable in applications such as: - **Batch counting** (parts produced, items packaged) - **Position tracking** on rotary encoders - **Sequential machine cycles** (Step 1 done → move to Step 2 after N presses) - **Production rate calculation** (counts ÷ time) - **Length measurement** by counting encoder pulses per unit distance > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Trap:** Many students confuse **timers** with **counters**. The key distinction: *Timers measure the passage of TIME (in hundredths/seconds). Counters measure the NUMBER OF EVENTS (pulses).* A counter does NOT need a time-base — it only cares about transitions. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of an Up Counter (CTU) An **Up Counter** is the most fundamental counter in PLC programming. Its rung-condition evaluation follows a strict three-input logic: **Logical Structure:** - **CU (Count-Up Enable):** Each false-to-true transition of this input increments the **ACC** by 1. - **R (Reset):** When this input is TRUE, the **ACC** is forced back to `0`. The `DN` bit clears. - **PRE (Preset):** A constant or variable integer representing the target. - **CV (Current Value):** Output word reflecting the live count. - **DN (Done) Bit:** Boolean output. `DN = TRUE` when `ACC \geq PRE`. **Internal Logic Equations:** $$ \text{ACC}_{n} = \text{ACC}_{n-1} + 1 \quad \text{(on rising edge of CU)} $$ $$ \text{DN} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \text{ACC} \geq \text{PRE} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ $$ \text{ACC} = 0 \quad \text{whenever } R = 1 $$ ### 2.2 Anatomy of a Down Counter (CTD) A **Down Counter** reverses the counting direction. It is used when you know in advance *how many events remain*. **Logical Structure:** - **CD (Count-Down Enable):** Each false-to-true transition **decrements** ACC by 1. - **LD (Load):** When TRUE, the counter **loads PRE into ACC**. This is essentially the "arm" or "initialize" input. - **DN (Done) Bit:** Trips when `ACC` has counted **down past zero** (i.e., from 1 → 0, the `DN` becomes TRUE). **Internal Logic Equations:** $$ \text{ACC}_{n} = \text{ACC}_{n-1} - 1 \quad \text{(on rising edge of CD)} $$ $$ \text{ACC} = \text{PRE} \quad \text{whenever } LD = 1 $$ $$ \text{DN} = 1 \quad \text{whenever } \text{ACC} \leq 0 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Down Counters DO NOT go negative in standard PLCs.** Once `ACC = 0`, further pulses leave `ACC = 0` and keep `DN = 1` (it latches until LD is reapplied). ### 2.3 Anatomy of an Up/Down Counter (CTUD) The **Up/Down Counter** merges both behaviours into a single instruction. This is the most commonly tested variant in KTU exams. **Logical Structure:** - **CU input:** Increments ACC. - **CD input:** Decrements ACC. - **R (Reset):** Forces ACC to 0, clears `DN`. - **LD (Load):** Forces ACC to PRE. - **OV (Overflow) Bit:** TRUE if ACC exceeds the upper word limit (`+32,767` for 16-bit). - **UN (Underflow) Bit:** TRUE if ACC drops below the lower word limit (`-32,768`). - **DN (Done) Bit:** TRUE when `ACC \geq PRE`. **Internal Logic Equations:** $$ \text{ACC}_{n} = \text{ACC}_{n-1} + (\text{CU pulse}) - (\text{CD pulse}) $$ $$ \text{OV} = 1 \iff \text{ACC} > 32{,}767 $$ $$ \text{UN} = 1 \iff \text{ACC} < -32{,}768 $$ $$ \text{DN} = 1 \iff \text{ACC} \geq \text{PRE} $$ ### 2.4 Combining (Cascading) Counters A single 16-bit counter can only count up to `32,767`. Many industrial applications (e.g., counting revolutions over an hour, counting items in a large batch) require counts in the **millions**. To achieve this, **two (or more) counters are cascaded**, where the **Done bit of the inner (fast) counter becomes the Count-Up pulse of the outer (slow) counter**. **Cascade Total Count Formula:** $$ \text{Total Count} = \text{PRE}_{\text{inner}} \times \text{PRE}_{\text{outer}} $$ **Example:** If `CT_Inner.PRE = 1000` and `CT_Outer.PRE = 1000`, the cascade can total up to: $$ 1{,}000 \times 1{,}000 = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ pulses} $$ **Operational Cascade Logic:** 1. Input pulses flow into the **inner (high-speed) counter** `CT_Inner`. 2. Every time `CT_Inner` reaches its preset, the `CT_Inner.DN` bit becomes TRUE. 3. The outer counter `CT_Outer` is wired so that its `CU` input is driven by `CT_Inner.DN`. 4. Each pulse of `CT_Outer.DN` therefore represents `PRE_inner` input pulses. 5. The final `OUT_EN` (system done) flag fires when `CT_Outer.DN = 1`. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Formula / Rule | Units / Range | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | Up Count Update | $\text{ACC}_{n} = \text{ACC}_{n-1} + 1$ | pulses | Tally of incoming events | | Down Count Update | $\text{ACC}_{n} = \text{ACC}_{n-1} - 1$ | pulses | Tally-down from a known limit | | Up/Down Mixed | $\Delta\text{ACC} = \sum \text{CU} - \sum \text{CD}$ | pulses | Net flow measurement | | Done Bit (Up) | $\text{DN} = (\text{ACC} \geq \text{PRE})$ | Boolean | Batch completion flag | | Done Bit (Down) | $\text{DN} = (\text{ACC} \leq 0)$ | Boolean | Underflow / exhaust flag | | Overflow (CTUD) | $\text{OV} = (\text{ACC} > 32{,}767)$ | Boolean | 16-bit signed limit alarm | | Underflow (CTUD) | $\text{UN} = (\text{ACC} < -32{,}768)$ | Boolean | 16-bit signed limit alarm | | Cascade Maximum | $\text{Max} = \text{PRE}_{1} \times \text{PRE}_{2} \times \cdots \times \text{PRE}_{n}$ | pulses | Total range of multi-stage counter | | Count Rate Limit | $f_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{scan}}}$ | Hz | Max pulse frequency the PLC can reliably count | ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Cascaded Counter Total Count **Problem Statement:** A bottling line must count bottles in batches of **exactly 75,000**. The PLC counter has a 16-bit limit of 32,767. Design a 2-stage cascade and prove it can achieve the target. **Step 1 — Identify the constraint.** The single-counter maximum is the 16-bit positive limit: $$ \text{Max}_{\text{single}} = 2^{15} - 1 = 32{,}767 $$ **Step 2 — Choose the inner counter preset.** We need a factor of 75,000 that is less than 32,767. A convenient choice is: $$ \text{PRE}_{\text{inner}} = 1{,}000 $$ **Step 3 — Solve for the outer counter preset.** $$ \text{PRE}_{\text{outer}} = \frac{\text{Total desired}}{\text{PRE}_{\text{inner}}} = \frac{75{,}000}{1{,}000} = 75 $$ **Step 4 — Verify the cascade total.** $$ \text{Total}_{\text{cascade}} = \text{PRE}_{\text{inner}} \times \text{PRE}_{\text{outer}} $$ $$ \text{Total}_{\text{cascade}} = 1{,}000 \times 75 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Total}_{\text{cascade}} = 75{,}000 \text{ pulses}} $$ **Step 5 — Confirm both presets fit within the 16-bit word limit.** $$ \text{PRE}_{\text{inner}} = 1{,}000 < 32{,}767 \quad \checkmark $$ $$ \text{PRE}_{\text{outer}} = 75 < 32{,}767 \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 6 — Operational trace (10,500 pulses into the system).** - Inner counter reaches `DN` after every 1,000 pulses. - After 10,500 input pulses: $$ \text{CT}_{\text{inner}}\text{.ACC} = 10{,}500 \bmod 1{,}000 = 500 $$ $$ \text{CT}_{\text{outer}}\text{.ACC} = \left\lfloor \frac{10{,}500}{1{,}000} \right\rfloor = 10 $$ **Step 7 — When the full 75,000th pulse arrives:** $$ \text{CT}_{\text{inner}}\text{.ACC} = 0 \quad (\text{just rolled over}) $$ $$ \text{CT}_{\text{outer}}\text{.ACC} = 75 $$ Since $75 \geq 75$, the `CT_outer.DN` bit becomes TRUE, triggering the **batch-complete output**. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Count Rate from Scan Time **Problem Statement:** A PLC has a scan time of $T_{\text{scan}} = 10 \text{ ms}$. An encoder produces pulses at $f_{\text{enc}} = 500 \text{ Hz}$. Will the PLC reliably count every pulse? **Step 1 — Convert scan time to frequency.** $$ f_{\text{scan}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{scan}}} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}} = 100 \text{ Hz} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Nyquist-style criterion for reliable counting.** For reliable edge-detection, the pulse frequency must be **less than the scan frequency** (in practice, much less — typically $\leq 0.25 \times f_{\text{scan}}$): $$ f_{\text{enc}} < f_{\text{scan}} $$ $$ 500 \text{ Hz} > 100 \text{ Hz} $$ **Step 3 — Conclusion.** The PLC **cannot reliably count** this encoder signal at full speed. A **high-speed counter (HSC) module** is required. This is a classic real-world engineering pitfall. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Up/Down Counter Net Flow Calculation **Problem Statement:** A parking garage has two infrared sensors: - `S_IN` (CU input): counts cars entering - `S_OUT` (CD input): counts cars leaving In one hour, `S_IN` produces 240 pulses and `S_OUT` produces 185 pulses. Initial `ACC = 50`. Compute final `ACC` and check if `DN` triggers if `PRE = 100`. **Step 1 — Apply the up/down update rule.** $$ \text{ACC}_{\text{final}} = \text{ACC}_{\text{initial}} + \sum \text{CU} - \sum \text{CD} $$ $$ \text{ACC}_{\text{final}} = 50 + 240 - 185 $$ $$ \text{ACC}_{\text{final}} = 105 $$ **Step 2 — Check the Done bit.** $$ \text{ACC}_{\text{final}} = 105 \geq \text{PRE} = 100 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{DN} = 1 \quad (\text{Overflow alarm: parking FULL})} $$ **Step 3 — Engineering insight.** The `DN` flag here signals the **FULL sign** should illuminate and the entry barrier should deny further entry. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: CTU vs CTD vs CTUD | Feature | CTU (Up) | CTD (Down) | CTUD (Up/Down) | |---|---|---|---| | Direction | Increment only | Decrement only | Bi-directional | | Initialize Input | None (starts at 0) | LD (loads PRE) | Both R and LD | | Reset Input | R | None (LD acts as reset-to-PRE) | R | | Done Bit Meaning | $\text{ACC} \geq \text{PRE}$ (target hit) | $\text{ACC} \leq 0$ (exhausted) | $\text{ACC} \geq \text{PRE}$ (target hit) | | Overflow Protection | Optional (CTU with overflow flag) | N/A | Yes (OV and UN flags) | | Typical KTU Use | Counting events forward | Countdown timers, batch subtraction | Net flow, position, inventory | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — CTU Instruction Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Pulse Source
Sensor / Pushbutton] -->|Rung Condition
False to True| B[CTU Instruction Block
Counter Address] B --> C{Check Reset
R is TRUE?} C -->|Yes| D[Force ACC to 0
Clear DN bit] C -->|No| E{ACC reaches PRE?} E -->|No| F[Increment ACC by 1] E -->|Yes| G[Set DN bit TRUE] F --> H[Output DN bit
to next rung] D --> H G --> H H --> I[Actuator / Output
Buzzer / Conveyor Stop] style A fill:#e1f5ff style B fill:#fff4e1 style I fill:#e8f5e9 ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — CTUD Up/Down Counter ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[CU Input
Count Up Pulse] --> MUX{Logic Core} P2[CD Input
Count Down Pulse] --> MUX R[R Input
Reset to 0] --> MUX LD[LD Input
Load PRE into ACC] --> MUX MUX --> ACC[Accumulated Value
ACC Register] PRE[Preset Value
PRE Register] --> COMP ACC --> COMP{Comparator
ACC vs PRE} ACC --> LIM{Overflow Check
ACC vs Limits} LIM -->|Above 32767| OV[OV Flag TRUE] LIM -->|Below -32768| UN[UN Flag TRUE] COMP -->|ACC greater or equal PRE| DN[DN Flag TRUE] COMP -->|ACC less than PRE| DNOFF[DN Flag FALSE] OV --> OUT[Status Outputs] UN --> OUT DN --> OUT DNOFF --> OUT style MUX fill:#fff4e1 style ACC fill:#e1f5ff style COMP fill:#fce4ec ``` ### 4.3 Ladder Logic Diagram — Cascade of Two Counters ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RUNG1["Rung 1 - Inner Counter CT_INNER"] I1[Input I:1/0
Sensor Pulse] --> CTU1[/CTU
C5:0
PRE=1000/] RST1[Reset B3:0/0] --> CTU1 CTU1 -->|DN bit C5:0/DN| OUT1(( )) end subgraph RUNG2["Rung 2 - Outer Counter CT_OUTER"] CTU1 -->|DN bit C5:0/DN| CTU2[/CTU
C5:1
PRE=75/] RST2[Master Reset B3:0/1] --> CTU2 CTU2 -->|DN bit C5:1/DN| OUT2((Batch Complete
O:2/0)) end style CTU1 fill:#fff4e1 style CTU2 fill:#e1f5ff style OUT1 fill:#e8f5e9 style OUT2 fill:#ffebee ``` **Explanation of the Cascade:** - The inner counter `C5:0` (preset = 1000) increments on every sensor pulse. - When `C5:0` reaches 1000, its `DN` bit becomes TRUE — turning ON the `OUT1` indicator AND simultaneously feeding the `CU` input of `C5:1`. - The outer counter `C5:1` increments by 1 every time the inner one "rolls over". - When `C5:1.ACC` reaches `75`, the entire batch of `75,000` is counted, and `OUT2` (Batch Complete) fires. ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Counter Use in a Manufacturing Cell ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT["Input Layer"] S1[Proximity Sensor
Part Present] S2[Pushbutton
Operator Reset] S3[Limit Switch
Conveyor Full] end subgraph PLC["PLC Processor - Ladder Logic"] CTU_Block[Counter Instruction
CTU C5:0] COMP_Block[Comparator
ACC vs PRE] LOGIC_Block[Logic Gates
AND / OR Latch] end subgraph OUTPUT["Output Layer"] M1[Solenoid
Stop Conveyor] L1[Indicator Lamp
Green - Running] L2[Indicator Lamp
Red - Batch Full] BUZ[Buzzer
Audible Alert] end S1 --> CTU_Block S2 --> CTU_Block CTU_Block --> COMP_Block COMP_Block --> LOGIC_Block S3 --> LOGIC_Block LOGIC_Block --> M1 LOGIC_Block --> L1 LOGIC_Block --> L2 LOGIC_Block --> BUZ style PLC fill:#fff8e1 style INPUT fill:#e1f5ff style OUTPUT fill:#e8f5e9 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Define a PLC counter. Differentiate between an Up counter (CTU) and a Down counter (CTD) with suitable examples.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A PLC counter is an internal instruction that tallies the number of false-to-true transitions on its input rung-condition. It maintains an **Accumulated Value (ACC)** that is compared against a **Preset Value (PRE)**. | Aspect | CTU (Up Counter) | CTD (Down Counter) | |---|---|---| | Direction | Increments ACC by 1 per pulse | Decrements ACC by 1 per pulse | | Initial Value | ACC starts at 0 | ACC loaded with PRE on LD input | | Done Bit Sets When | $\text{ACC} \geq \text{PRE}$ (batch complete) | $\text{ACC} \leq 0$ (counter exhausted) | | Reset Method | R input forces ACC to 0 | LD input reloads PRE into ACC | | **Example** | Counting bottles **produced** since shift start | Counting bottles **remaining** to fill a 500-bottle crate | --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Explain the concept of cascading counters with a neat ladder diagram. Why is cascading needed?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Cascading** is the technique of connecting two or more counters in series so that the **Done (DN) bit of the inner counter** drives the **Count-Up input of the outer counter**. This multiplies the effective count range. **Why Cascading is Needed:** A standard 16-bit PLC counter is limited to $2^{15} - 1 = 32{,}767$ pulses. Many industrial applications (e.g., counting items in a 100,000-piece batch, measuring distance with high-resolution encoders) exceed this range. **Formula:** $$ \text{Total Count Range} = \text{PRE}_{\text{inner}} \times \text{PRE}_{\text{outer}} $$ **Ladder Diagram (textual description):** - **Rung 1:** `Input I:1/0 → [CTU C5:0, PRE = 1000] → Output O:2/0` - **Rung 2:** `C5:0/DN → [CTU C5:1, PRE = 100] → Output O:2/1 (Batch Complete)` **Total range achieved:** $1000 \times 100 = 100{,}000$ counts. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) --- ### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Up/Down Counter (CTUD)** instruction in detail. List all its inputs, outputs, and status flags. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A bottling plant uses a PLC Up/Down counter to track bottles in a buffer zone. Sensor `S_IN` (entering) pulses 150 times in one cycle; Sensor `S_OUT` (leaving) pulses 95 times. The initial `ACC = 200` and `PRE = 250`. Determine the final ACC, DN, OV, and UN bit states. Verify whether the DN flag will trigger an alarm. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q3(a) — 7 Marks **Definition (1 Mark):** A CTUD is a bi-directional counter that increments on one input and decrements on another input, allowing it to track **net flow** of items in a process. **Block Diagram / Inputs (3 Marks):** | Input/Output | Symbol | Function | |---|---|---| | Count Up | CU | Increments ACC on rising edge | | Count Down | CD | Decrements ACC on rising edge | | Reset | R | Forces ACC = 0, clears DN | | Load | LD | Forces ACC = PRE | | Preset | PRE | Target value (16-bit word) | | Accumulator | ACC | Current value (16-bit word) | | Done Bit | DN | TRUE when ACC ≥ PRE | | Overflow | OV | TRUE when ACC > +32,767 | | Underflow | UN | TRUE when ACC < -32,768 | **Operating Logic (2 Marks):** $$ \text{ACC}_{n} = \text{ACC}_{n-1} + (\text{CU pulses}) - (\text{CD pulses}) $$ Priority rules: **R > LD > CU/CD** (Reset has highest priority). **Application (1 Mark):** Used in parking garages, inventory bins, position tracking with bidirectional encoders. --- #### Model Solution for Q3(b) — 7 Marks **Given Data (1 Mark):** - $\text{ACC}_{\text{initial}} = 200$ - $\sum \text{CU pulses} = 150$ - $\sum \text{CD pulses} = 95$ - $\text{PRE} = 250$ **Step 1 — Compute final ACC (2 Marks):** $$ \text{ACC}_{\text{final}} = 200 + 150 - 95 $$ $$ \text{ACC}_{\text{final}} = 255 $$ **Step 2 — Evaluate DN bit (1 Mark):** $$ 255 \geq 250 \implies \text{DN} = 1 $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate OV bit (1 Mark):** $$ 255 \not> 32{,}767 \implies \text{OV} = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate UN bit (1 Mark):** $$ 255 \not< -32{,}768 \implies \text{UN} = 0 $$ **Step 5 — Engineering interpretation (1 Mark):** Since `DN = 1`, the PLC will trigger the **"Buffer Zone Full" alarm** and halt the upstream conveyor feeding new bottles into the buffer. The control system must then either divert bottles to an alternate line or pause production. | Flag | Value | Meaning | |---|---|---| | DN | 1 | Buffer full — alarm ON | | OV | 0 | No overflow | | UN | 0 | No underflow | --- ### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q4. [KTU University Exam — June 2023 / Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain **Counter Cascading** with the help of a neat ladder diagram and timing chart. Derive the formula for the maximum count range of an n-stage cascaded counter. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A PLC has a scan time of $15 \text{ ms}$. An encoder produces 400 pulses per revolution and the motor shaft rotates at 1800 RPM. The PLC needs to count every pulse for position feedback. Determine whether the standard CTU counter can reliably count these pulses. If not, suggest an engineering solution. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q4(a) — 7 Marks **Cascading Concept (2 Marks):** Counter cascading connects counters in series such that the `DN` bit of an inner (faster) counter feeds the `CU` input of an outer (slower) counter. Each "tick" of the outer counter represents a full cycle of the inner counter. **Ladder Diagram (Textual) (2 Marks):** ``` Rung 1: |--[ I:1/0 ]--+--[ CTU C5:0, PRE=500 ]--+--( O:2/0 )--| | | Rung 2: +--[ C5:0/DN ]--+--[ CTU C5:1, PRE=200 ]--+--( Batch O:2/1 )--| | Rung 3: +--[ C5:1/DN ]--+--( O:2/2 System Complete )--| ``` **Derivation of Maximum Range (3 Marks):** Let $N_1, N_2, \ldots, N_n$ be the preset values of counters $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$. Each complete cycle of $C_1$ produces 1 pulse to $C_2$. Therefore: $$ \text{Range of } C_2 = N_1 \times N_2 $$ Extending to $n$ stages: $$ \boxed{\text{Total Range} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} N_i = N_1 \times N_2 \times \cdots \times N_n} $$ For 3 counters with presets 100, 100, 100: $$ \text{Range} = 100 \times 100 \times 100 = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ pulses} $$ --- #### Model Solution for Q4(b) — 7 Marks **Given Data (1 Mark):** - $T_{\text{scan}} = 15 \text{ ms}$ - Encoder resolution = 400 pulses/rev - Motor speed $N = 1800 \text{ RPM}$ **Step 1 — Compute pulse frequency (2 Marks):** $$ f_{\text{pulse}} = \text{Resolutions/rev} \times \frac{N}{60} $$ $$ f_{\text{pulse}} = 400 \times \frac{1800}{60} $$ $$ f_{\text{pulse}} = 400 \times 30 = 12{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 12 \text{ kHz} $$ **Step 2 — Compute PLC scan frequency (1 Mark):** $$ f_{\text{scan}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{scan}}} = \frac{1}{15 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 66.67 \text{ Hz} $$ **Step 3 — Compare and conclude (2 Marks):** $$ f_{\text{pulse}} = 12{,}000 \text{ Hz} \gg f_{\text{scan}} = 66.67 \text{ Hz} $$ The PLC will miss pulses. A **standard CTU cannot reliably count** this signal. **Step 4 — Engineering Solution (1 Mark):** Use a **High-Speed Counter (HSC) module** (e.g., Allen-Bradley `1746-HSCE`, Siemens `FM 350-1`). HSC modules count pulses in **hardware** independent of scan time, capable of counting at 50 kHz to 1 MHz depending on the module. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing CTU Done bit logic** — Students often write "DN = 1 when ACC = PRE" but the correct logic is `ACC ≥ PRE`. (Loss: 1 mark) > 2. **Forgetting the Reset (R) input** when drawing the CTU instruction block. (Loss: 0.5 mark) > 3. **Cascade formula miswritten** — It is `PRE_inner × PRE_outer`, NOT `PRE_inner + PRE_outer`. Adding gives wrong range. (Loss: 2 marks) > 4. **Scan-time question overlooked** — Many students compute pulse frequency but forget to compare with `1/T_scan`. Without this comparison, no valid conclusion can be drawn. (Loss: 2 marks) > 5. **Confusing CTUD done bit with overflow** — `DN` and `OV` are distinct flags; `DN` triggers at the preset, while `OV` triggers only at the 16-bit word limit. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:** - **Counter vs Timer:** Counters tally **events/pulses**; timers measure **time duration**. Never interchange. - **CTU Block — Remember "URRPV":** **U**p input, **R**eset, **R**etained value, **P**reset, current value. - **CTU Done bit** fires when `ACC ≥ PRE` (inclusive, not strict equality). - **CTD Done bit** fires when `ACC ≤ 0` (the counter "underflows" to zero). - **CTUD Done bit** behaves like CTU — fires when `ACC ≥ PRE`. - **CTUD Priority Order:** **R**eset > **L**oad > **C**ount pulses. Reset is dominant; it clears the counter regardless of CU/CD activity. - **Cascading Rule:** Outer counter's CU input ← Inner counter's DN bit. Total range multiplies (NOT adds). - **Scan-time rule of thumb:** Pulse frequency should be **less than ~25% of scan frequency** for reliable software-based counting. Otherwise, use an HSC module. - **Overflow limits (16-bit signed):** OV at `+32,767`, UN at `-32,768`. - **Down counter latches** its `DN` bit — it does NOT auto-reset to PRE. To re-arm, pulse the `LD` input. - **Industry-standard presets:** Always choose powers of 10 (100, 1000) or factors that divide your target count evenly. E.g., for 75,000 → use 1000 × 75. - **Common exam traps:** Mixing up `CU` and `CD` labels; forgetting `LD` on CTD; not drawing the `R` (reset) input on CTU block diagrams. - **Mnemonic for CTUD flags:** **"D.O.U.N."** — **D**one, **O**verflow, **U**nderflow, **N**et value.

topics.title: PLC Arithmetic Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT604 - PLC & DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** PLC Arithmetic Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**PLC Arithmetic Functions** are a class of programming instructions within the PLC (Programmable Logic Controller) instruction set that allow the processor to perform basic and advanced mathematical computations on data held in memory locations (registers, word tables, or I/O image tables) during the program execution phase of the scan cycle.

According to the **IEC 61131-3 standard** (which governs PLC programming languages including Ladder Logic, Function Block Diagrams, and Structured Text), arithmetic functions are formally categorized as standard functions in the `STANDARD FUNCTION` library, operating on elementary data types such as `INT`, `DINT`, `REAL`, and `LREAL`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** PLC Arithmetic Functions form part of the basic instruction set of any PLC platform. They are essential for data scaling, PID control loops, signal linearization, and engineering unit conversions within process automation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The PLC as a Pocket Calculator

Imagine your smartphone calculator. You type a number, press a function key (like `+`, `−`, `×`, `÷`, `√`), type another number, press `=`, and the result appears. 

A PLC works exactly the same way, but instead of you typing the numbers, the **inputs come automatically from sensors** (temperature, pressure, flow) wired to the PLC inputs, and the **results get stored in memory registers** that can drive actuators, displays, or HMI screens.

- **The calculator buttons** = Arithmetic Function Blocks (`ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `SQR`)
- **The numbers on screen** = Data stored in `Input Word (IW)` or `Memory Word (MW)` registers
- **The result** = Output stored in `Output Word (QW)` or another `Memory Word (MW)`

For example, a temperature transmitter gives **4–20 mA** corresponding to **0–200°C**. The PLC scales this using the linear equation:

$$T = \left(\frac{I - 4}{20 - 4}\right) \times 200$$

This single line requires **one SUB, one DIV, and one MUL** instruction inside the PLC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in KTU Exams:** You are expected to be able to *trace* through arithmetic instructions, *predict* the result stored in the destination register, and *identify* overflow, underflow, and division-by-zero conditions. This forms a regular 14-mark question in Module 1.

## 1.3 Standard PLC Arithmetic Instruction Set

The complete family of PLC arithmetic functions is summarized below. Note that syntax varies between vendors (Siemens S7, Allen-Bradley, Schneider, Mitsubishi), but the **mathematical behavior is identical**.

| Instruction | Mathematical Operation | Typical Use Case in Industry |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `ADD` | $A + B \rightarrow C$ | Totalizer, setpoint summation |
| `SUB` | $A - B \rightarrow C$ | Error calculation (SP − PV) |
| `MUL` | $A \times B \rightarrow C$ | Area/volume conversion |
| `DIV` | $A \div B \rightarrow C$ | Engineering unit conversion, scaling |
| `SQR` / `SQRT` | $\sqrt{x} \rightarrow C$ | RMS voltage calculation, flow = $\sqrt{\Delta P}$ |
| `SIN`, `COS`, `TAN` | Trigonometric | Servo axis positioning, robotics |
| `LN`, `EXP`, `LOG` | Logarithmic | Exponential decay, dB scale conversions |
| `INC` | $x + 1 \rightarrow C$ | Counter increment, sequence step |
| `DEC` | $x - 1 \rightarrow C$ | Counter decrement, reverse counting |
| `NEG` | $-x \rightarrow C$ | Sign inversion in control algorithm |
| `ABS` | $\vert x \vert \rightarrow C$ | Magnitude extraction, distance calc. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus Areas:** The Module 1 syllabus places highest weight on `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`, and `SQR`, along with the **status flag conditions** (Zero, Carry, Overflow) that result from their execution. Trigonometric and logarithmic functions are typically treated as brief reference material.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 How the PLC Executes an Arithmetic Instruction — Operational Breakdown

Every arithmetic instruction in a PLC undergoes the same **three-stage micro-operation** during the program scan:

1. **Fetch Stage (Source Operand Read):** The CPU retrieves the value stored in the source memory location (for example, `MW10`). This is a *non-destructive read* — the original value remains intact.
2. **Execute Stage (ALU Operation):** The Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) inside the PLC's microprocessor performs the mathematical operation. The ALU is a hardware circuit built from digital logic gates (full adders, shift registers, comparators).
3. **Store Stage (Destination Write):** The result is written into the destination register (for example, `MW20`). Simultaneously, the CPU updates the **Status Word / Flags Register** based on the result.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** A common 3-mark question asks: *"Explain the function of the Status Word in PLC arithmetic."* The Status Word contains bits such as **RLO (Result of Logic Operation), BR (Binary Result), OV (Overflow), OS (Stored Overflow), CC0, CC1 (Condition Codes), and N (Negative flag)**. These are checked by conditional jump instructions immediately after the arithmetic operation.

## 2.2 Data Types and Their Numerical Boundaries

The result of an arithmetic function depends **critically** on the data type. KTU exams frequently test whether the student recognizes when an overflow will occur.

| Data Type | Bits | Min Value | Max Value | Use Case |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| `INT` (Integer) | 16 | $-32{,}768$ | $+32{,}767$ | Counter values, small setpoints |
| `DINT` (Double Integer) | 32 | $-2{,}147{,}483{,}648$ | $+2{,}147{,}483{,}647$ | Product of two INTs, large counts |
| `REAL` (Floating Point) | 32 | $\approx -3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | $\approx +3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | Process variables, scaled values |
| `LREAL` (Long Real) | 64 | $\approx -1.8 \times 10^{308}$ | $\approx +1.8 \times 10^{308}$ | High-precision calculations |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** When multiplying two `INT` values (say, $300 \times 200 = 60{,}000$), the result **exceeds the 16-bit INT range** ($32{,}767$). The PLC will either truncate the result, set the overflow flag, or store it in a 32-bit `DINT` register — depending on the instruction used. This is the single most tested pitfall in KTU arithmetic questions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Formula | Flag Conditions (When They Activate) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Addition | $C = A + B$ | `OV` if signed sum $> +32{,}767$ or $< -32{,}768$; `CC1` set if $C > 0$; `CC0` set if $C = 0$ |
| Subtraction | $C = A - B$ | `OV` if signed diff. $> +32{,}767$ or $< -32{,}768$; `N` flag set if $C < 0$ |
| Multiplication | $C = A \times B$ | `OV` if $\vert C \vert > 32{,}767$ (for `INT`); use `MUL_DI` for full 32-bit product |
| Division | $C = A \div B$ | **Division by zero ($B = 0$) is trapped** → `OV` flag set, CPU may go to STOP |
| Square Root | $C = \sqrt{A}$ | `OV` if $A < 0$ (square root of negative number is undefined in reals) |
| Increment | $C = A + 1$ | `OV` when wrapping from $+32{,}767$ to $-32{,}768$ |
| Decrement | $C = A - 1$ | `OV` when wrapping from $-32{,}768$ to $+32{,}767$ |
| Linear Scaling | $Y = mX + C$ | $m$ = multiplier, $C$ = offset; avoid divide-by-zero in $m$ derivation |
| Engineering Conversion | $PV = \left(\frac{Raw - Raw_{min}}{Raw_{max} - Raw_{min}}\right) \times EU_{span}$ | Guard against $Raw_{min} = Raw_{max}$ (zero denominator) |

**Where:**
- $A$, $B$ = Source operands (memory words or constants)
- $C$ = Destination register
- $m$ = Slope (gain), $C$ = Y-intercept (offset) in linear scaling
- $Raw$ = Raw input from ADC in counts, $EU_{span}$ = Engineering unit span

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This is Important to Engineers:** Arithmetic functions are not academic exercises — they are the *backbone* of every modern control system.

- **PID Control Loops:** The error signal $e(t) = SP - PV$ requires `SUB`; the integral term requires `ADD` over time; the derivative term uses `SUB` on successive samples; output calculation uses `MUL` with $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$.
- **Flow Measurement from Differential Pressure:** Standard orifice-plate flow equation is $Q = K \sqrt{\Delta P}$, requiring `SUB` (to get $P_1 - P_2$), `SQR` (square root extraction), and `MUL` (with flow constant $K$).
- **Thermocouple Linearization:** The NIST polynomial equations (typically 8th-12th order) for converting thermocouple millivolts to temperature are evaluated using `MUL` and `ADD` repeatedly.
- **Tank Volume Calculation:** Cylindrical tank: $V = \pi r^2 h$ requires `MUL`, `MUL`, `MUL` (with $\pi$ as a constant), and `MUL` again.

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind the Overflow Concept

When the ALU adds two 16-bit numbers, the sum might physically require 17 bits to represent. The PLC must do one of three things:
1. **Truncate** the extra bit (loses information — bad practice).
2. **Set the Overflow Flag** and store only the lower 16 bits (most common).
3. **Auto-promote** to a `DINT` register to hold the full result (used by `ADD_DI`, `MUL_DI`).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Answer Tip:** When asked "What happens when an overflow occurs?" your model answer should mention **all three consequences** — the flag is set, the result wraps around (two's complement), and the conditional jump following the instruction behaves incorrectly if the flag is not checked.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Simple Addition with Overflow Detection

**Problem:** A PLC program executes the following ladder rungs:
- `MW10 = 25,000` (decimal integer)
- `MW12 = 20,000` (decimal integer)
- Instruction: `ADD MW10, MW12` → result stored in `MW14`

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Identify the operation} \\
& C = A + B \quad \text{where } A = 25{,}000,\ B = 20{,}000 \\
\\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Compute the mathematical sum} \\
& C_{\text{math}} = 25{,}000 + 20{,}000 = 45{,}000 \\
\\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Compare against the INT data type limit} \\
& \text{INT max} = 32{,}767 \\
& 45{,}000 > 32{,}767 \quad \Rightarrow \text{OVERFLOW} \\
\\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Determine the 16-bit two's complement wrap-around} \\
& \text{Overflow magnitude} = 45{,}000 - 32{,}768 = 12{,}232 \\
& \text{Result wraps to} = 12{,}232 - 32{,}768 = -20{,}536 \\
& \text{Check: } -20{,}536 + 32{,}768 \times 2 = 45{,}000 \ \checkmark \\
\\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Update flag register} \\
& \text{OV flag} = 1,\ \text{OS flag} = 1 \\
& \text{CC1} = 0 \ (\text{since result is negative}),\ \text{CC0} = 0 \\
\\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Conclusion} \\
& MW14 = -20{,}536 \quad \text{(INCORRECT — overflow occurred)} \\
& \text{Programmer should use ADD\_DI to avoid overflow.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Note:** Notice how the 16-bit result becomes a *negative* number even though both inputs were positive. This is the hallmark of integer overflow in two's complement arithmetic, and KTU frequently tests this.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Linear Scaling of a 4-20 mA Signal

**Problem:** A pressure transmitter outputs 4-20 mA, which the PLC's analog input module converts to a raw integer count of 0-27648 (12-bit unipolar). The corresponding pressure range is 0-10 bar. If the raw input register `IW0 = 14,824`, calculate the engineering unit pressure value.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Identify the linear scaling formula} \\
& PV = \left(\frac{Raw - Raw_{min}}{Raw_{max} - Raw_{min}}\right) \times EU_{max} \\
\\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Substitute known values} \\
& Raw = 14{,}824,\ Raw_{min} = 0,\ Raw_{max} = 27{,}648,\ EU_{max} = 10 \\
& PV = \left(\frac{14{,}824 - 0}{27{,}648 - 0}\right) \times 10 \\
\\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Compute the numerator} \\
& 14{,}824 - 0 = 14{,}824 \\
\\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Compute the denominator} \\
& 27{,}648 - 0 = 27{,}648 \\
\\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Perform the division (REAL division to preserve fractional part)} \\
& \frac{14{,}824}{27{,}648} = 0.5361 \\
\\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Multiply by engineering unit span} \\
& PV = 0.5361 \times 10 = 5.361 \text{ bar} \\
\\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \text{State the PLC instruction sequence} \\
& \text{Rung 1: SUB 0, IW0} \rightarrow MW10 \quad (\text{or use constant}) \\
& \text{Rung 2: DIV 27648, MW10} \rightarrow MW12 \\
& \text{Rung 3: MUL 10.0, MW12} \rightarrow MW14 \quad (\text{use REAL constant}) \\
& \text{Rung 4: Store MW14 as the displayed pressure value}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** In real Siemens S7 code, this is often done with the `FC105` "SCALE" function block, which encapsulates all four arithmetic operations in one block call. However, the underlying math is identical.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Square Root Extraction (Orifice Flow)

**Problem:** A differential pressure transmitter measures $\Delta P = 12{,}500$ Pa across an orifice plate. The flow equation is $Q = 0.05 \sqrt{\Delta P}$, where $Q$ is in m³/hr. Calculate $Q$.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{State the flow equation} \\
& Q = K \sqrt{\Delta P} \quad \text{where } K = 0.05,\ \Delta P = 12{,}500 \\
\\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Apply the square root operation} \\
& \sqrt{\Delta P} = \sqrt{12{,}500} \\
& = \sqrt{125 \times 100} = 10 \sqrt{125} \\
& = 10 \times 11.1803 \\
& = 111.803 \\
\\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Multiply by the flow constant} \\
& Q = 0.05 \times 111.803 \\
& Q = 5.590 \text{ m}^3\text{/hr} \\
\\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{State the PLC instruction sequence} \\
& \text{Rung 1: SQR IW0} \rightarrow MW20 \quad (\Delta P \rightarrow \sqrt{\Delta P}) \\
& \text{Rung 2: MUL 0.05, MW20} \rightarrow MW22 \quad (K \times \sqrt{\Delta P} \rightarrow Q) \\
\\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Flag check} \\
& \Delta P = 12{,}500 > 0 \Rightarrow \text{No overflow on SQR} \\
& \text{CC0} = 0,\ \text{CC1} = 1 \ (\text{positive result})
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Division by Zero and CPU Behavior

**Problem:** A PLC program contains `DIV MW100, MW102` where `MW102 = 0`. Describe what happens.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Mathematical perspective} \\
& \frac{MW100}{0} = \text{undefined} \ (\text{or } \pm \infty \text{ in IEEE 754}) \\
\\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Integer division behavior (Siemens S7 perspective)} \\
& \text{The CPU detects } B = 0 \text{ before ALU execution} \\
& \text{OV flag} = 1,\ \text{OS flag} = 1 \\
& \text{Destination register retains its previous value} \\
\\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Optional OB121 (Error Organization Block) is called} \\
& \text{If OB121 is not programmed, the CPU transitions to STOP mode} \\
\\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Real division behavior (REAL data type)} \\
& \text{In IEEE 754 floating point, } x / 0 = \pm \infty \\
& \text{Result is } +\infty \text{ (special bit pattern: 0x7F800000)} \\
& \text{OV flag may or may not be set depending on PLC platform} \\
\\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Best practice — pre-check the divisor} \\
& \text{Rung 1: } \text{IF } MW102 = 0 \text{ THEN jump to error handler} \\
& \text{Rung 2: } \text{IF } MW102 \neq 0 \text{ THEN execute DIV} \\
& \text{This prevents the CPU from entering STOP state}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write that the result of $x/0$ is zero. **This is wrong.** In a PLC, division by zero is either a *trapped exception* (integer math) or produces a *special infinity bit-pattern* (floating-point math). Writing zero will lose you full marks on this question type.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Integer vs Real Arithmetic

| Property | Integer (`INT`/`DINT`) | Real (`REAL`/`LREAL`) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Bit width | 16 / 32 | 32 / 64 |
| Storage format | Two's complement binary | IEEE 754 floating point |
| Range | Limited ($-2^{15}$ to $2^{15}-1$) | Huge ($\pm 3.4 \times 10^{\pm 38}$) |
| Precision | Exact (every integer representable) | Approximate (~7 decimal digits) |
| Execution time | Fast (single CPU cycle for ADD) | Slow (5–10 cycles for FPU) |
| Overflow behavior | Wraps modulo $2^N$ | Sets to $\pm \infty$ or `NaN` |
| Typical instruction | `ADD_I`, `MUL_DI` | `ADD_R`, `MUL_R` |
| Example syntax (Siemens) | `L MW10; L MW12; +I; T MW14` | `L MD10; L MD14; /R; T MD18` |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PLC Arithmetic Instruction Execution Flow

The diagram below traces the path of an arithmetic instruction from its presence in the scan cycle to the final result being stored, including all flag updates.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Scan Cycle Start])
    nodeB[Read Input Image Table IWx]
    nodeC{Logic Solver\nSection Begins}
    nodeD[Fetch Source Operand A\nfrom Memory Word MWn]
    nodeE[Fetch Source Operand B\nfrom Memory Word MWm or Constant]
    nodeF[ALU Performs Arithmetic\nOperation - ADD SUB MUL DIV SQR]
    nodeG[Compute Mathematical Result\nC equals f of A and B]
    nodeH{Overflow or\nDivide by Zero?}
    nodeI[Set OV Flag = 1\nSet OS Flag = 1]
    nodeJ[Result is Valid\nClear OV Flag]
    nodeK[Update Status Word\nCC0 CC1 N Flags]
    nodeL[Write Result C to\nDestination Register MWd]
    nodeM[Copy Output Image to\nPhysical Outputs QWy]
    nodeN([Scan Cycle End\nNext Cycle Begins])

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH -- Yes --> nodeI
    nodeH -- No --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of PLC Arithmetic Unit

This diagram shows the **hardware-level data path** inside the PLC's CPU when an arithmetic instruction is executed. Note the central role of the **ALU** (Arithmetic Logic Unit) and the **Flags Register** (Status Word).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSrc1[Source A\nMemory Word MWn] --> nodeMUX
    nodeSrc2[Source B\nMemory Word MWm] --> nodeMUX
    nodeConst[Constant from\nInstruction Operand] --> nodeMUX
    nodeMUX[Internal Data Bus\nMultiplexer] --> nodeALU
    nodeALU[ALU\nArithmetic Logic Unit]
    nodeALU --> nodeALUop{Operation\nDecoder}
    nodeALUop -- ADD --> nodeALU
    nodeALUop -- SUB --> nodeALU
    nodeALUop -- MUL --> nodeALU
    nodeALUop -- DIV --> nodeALU
    nodeALUop -- SQR --> nodeALU
    nodeALU --> nodeResult[Result Bus]
    nodeResult --> nodeDest[Destination Register\nMWd]
    nodeResult --> nodeFlags[Flags Register\nOV OS CC0 CC1 N]
    nodeFlags --> nodeJump[Conditional Jump\nInstruction uses flags]
    nodeDest --> nodeOutput[Output Image Table\nor HMI Display]
```

## 4.3 Ladder Logic Representation of Arithmetic Block

The diagram below shows a **standard ladder diagram** representation of a typical arithmetic calculation — the kind students will see in KTU practical exams. Note the `EN` (Enable) and `ENO` (Enable Output) contacts that indicate successful execution.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRailP([Positive Rail L1])
    nodeENcontact[EN Contact\nMaster Control Relay]
    nodeBlock[Arithmetic Function Block\nADD SUB MUL DIV SQR]
    nodeENOpin[ENO Output\nSuccess Indicator]
    nodeRailN([Negative Rail N])

    nodeRailP --> nodeENcontact
    nodeENcontact --> nodeBlock
    nodeBlock --> nodeENOpin
    nodeENOpin --> nodeRailN

    nodeIn1[Input Operand A\nMW10] -.-> nodeBlock
    nodeIn2[Input Operand B\nMW12] -.-> nodeBlock
    nodeOut[Output Result C\nMW14] -.-> nodeBlock
    nodeFlagsOut[Status Flags\nto next rung] -.-> nodeBlock
```

## 4.4 Overflow Detection Logic Flow

This schematic shows the **decision tree** the CPU follows after an arithmetic operation to determine whether the result is usable.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Arithmetic Instruction\nCompletes Execution]
    nodeCheck1{Result fits in\ndestination data type?}
    nodePath1[YES - Normal Path]
    nodePath2[NO - Overflow Detected]
    nodeAction1[Store full result in destination\nOV flag = 0\nContinue program]
    nodeAction2[Truncate to data type width\nOV flag = 1\nOS flag = 1]
    nodeNext{Programmer\nchecks OV flag?}
    nodeHandler[Error Handler Routine\nLogs error and resets]
    nodeStop[CPU enters\nSTOP mode]
    nodeContinue[Program continues\nwith corrupted result]

    nodeStart --> nodeCheck1
    nodeCheck1 -- Fits --> nodePath1
    nodeCheck1 -- Overflows --> nodePath2
    nodePath1 --> nodeAction1
    nodePath2 --> nodeAction2
    nodeAction2 --> nodeNext
    nodeNext -- YES --> nodeHandler
    nodeNext -- NO --> nodeStop
    nodeAction1 --> nodeContinue
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
**Define the term "PLC Arithmetic Function" and list any four standard arithmetic instructions used in PLC programming. Give one industrial application for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **PLC Arithmetic Function** is a programming instruction that enables the PLC's CPU to perform mathematical computations on data stored in memory registers during the program scan cycle. These functions form part of the standard instruction set defined by the IEC 61131-3 standard and operate on data types such as INT, DINT, and REAL.

Four standard arithmetic instructions:

1. **ADD (Addition):** $C = A + B$ — Used in totalizers, e.g., summing daily production counts.
2. **SUB (Subtraction):** $C = A - B$ — Used in PID control, e.g., computing error $e = SP - PV$.
3. **MUL (Multiplication):** $C = A \times B$ — Used in scaling, e.g., converting raw counts to engineering units.
4. **DIV (Division):** $C = A \div B$ — Used in linear scaling of analog inputs, e.g., $4$–$20$ mA to $0$–$100\%$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**  
**Explain the concept of "overflow" in the context of PLC integer arithmetic. What flag is set when overflow occurs, and what is the typical CPU response?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Overflow** in PLC integer arithmetic occurs when the result of an arithmetic operation exceeds the maximum (or minimum) value that can be represented by the destination register's data type. For a 16-bit `INT`, this range is $-32{,}768$ to $+32{,}767$. For example, adding $25{,}000 + 20{,}000 = 45{,}000$ exceeds $+32{,}767$ and causes an overflow.

When overflow occurs:
- The **OV (Overflow) flag** is set to 1.
- The **OS (Stored Overflow) flag** is also set, which remains latched until explicitly cleared.
- The result is stored in the destination register, but only the lower 16 bits are retained (two's complement wrap-around), producing a *numerically incorrect* value.
- The CPU does **not** automatically go to STOP mode for overflow (unlike division by zero); instead, the programmer must use conditional jump instructions to check the OV flag and handle the error.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question Choice A

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
**(a)** Explain the different data types supported by a standard PLC for arithmetic operations. Discuss the numerical range and typical use of `INT`, `DINT`, and `REAL` data types. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A temperature sensor produces an output of $4$–$20$ mA corresponding to a temperature range of $-50°C$ to $+150°C$. The PLC's analog input module converts this to a raw integer count of $0$–$27{,}648$. If the raw input value is $18{,}500$, calculate the equivalent temperature in °C using linear scaling. Show all PLC instruction steps. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(a) — Data Types in PLC Arithmetic (7 marks)

PLC arithmetic operations can be performed on different data types, each with distinct properties:

1. **INT (Integer — 16 bits):**  
   - Range: $-32{,}768$ to $+32{,}767$  
   - Storage: 16-bit two's complement binary  
   - Typical use: Counter values, small setpoints, timer preset values.  
   - Limitation: Result of $MUL$ of two `INT` values can easily exceed range.

2. **DINT (Double Integer — 32 bits):**  
   - Range: $-2{,}147{,}483{,}648$ to $+2{,}147{,}483{,}647$  
   - Storage: 32-bit two's complement binary  
   - Typical use: Products of two `INT` values, large counters, totalizers.  
   - Example: `MUL_DI` instruction stores the full 32-bit product in a `DINT` register.

3. **REAL (Floating Point — 32 bits):**  
   - Range: $\approx -3.4 \times 10^{38}$ to $+3.4 \times 10^{38}$  
   - Storage: IEEE 754 single-precision format (1 sign bit, 8 exponent bits, 23 mantissa bits).  
   - Typical use: Scaled process variables, PID calculations, trigonometric functions.  
   - Note: Limited to ~7 significant decimal digits — accumulating errors possible.

4. **LREAL (Long Real — 64 bits):**  
   - Range: $\approx \pm 1.8 \times 10^{308}$  
   - Storage: IEEE 754 double-precision format.  
   - Typical use: High-precision scientific calculations.

5. **BOOL and WORD:**  
   - `BOOL` is a single bit (0 or 1) — not used for arithmetic in the conventional sense.  
   - `WORD` is 16 bits unsigned (0 to 65,535) — used for bitwise logical operations, not arithmetic.

**Conclusion:** The choice of data type directly impacts range, precision, and execution time. Programmers must select appropriately to avoid overflow and maintain calculation accuracy.

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(b) — Temperature Scaling (7 marks)

**Step 1: Identify the linear scaling formula.**

$$
T = T_{min} + \left(\frac{Raw - Raw_{min}}{Raw_{max} - Raw_{min}}\right) \times (T_{max} - T_{min})
$$

**Step 2: List the known values.**

$$
Raw = 18{,}500,\ Raw_{min} = 0,\ Raw_{max} = 27{,}648 \\
T_{min} = -50,\ T_{max} = +150
$$

**Step 3: Compute the numerator.**

$$
Raw - Raw_{min} = 18{,}500 - 0 = 18{,}500
$$

**Step 4: Compute the denominator.**

$$
Raw_{max} - Raw_{min} = 27{,}648 - 0 = 27{,}648
$$

**Step 5: Compute the division (as REAL).**

$$
\frac{18{,}500}{27{,}648} = 0.6691
$$

**Step 6: Compute the temperature span.**

$$
T_{max} - T_{min} = 150 - (-50) = 200
$$

**Step 7: Multiply.**

$$
0.6691 \times 200 = 133.82
$$

**Step 8: Add the offset.**

$$
T = -50 + 133.82 = 83.82°C
$$

**Step 9: State the PLC instruction sequence (Siemens S7 style).**

| Rung | Operation | Mnemonic | Storage |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Load raw value | `L IW0` | Accu1 ← 18,500 |
| 2 | Subtract $Raw_{min}$ | `L 0; -I` | Accu1 ← 18,500 |
| 3 | Convert to REAL | `ITD; DTR` | Accu1 ← 18,500.0 |
| 4 | Divide by span | `L 27648.0; /R` | Accu1 ← 0.6691 |
| 5 | Multiply by temperature span | `L 200.0; *R` | Accu1 ← 133.82 |
| 6 | Add offset | `L -50.0; +R` | Accu1 ← 83.82 |
| 7 | Store result | `T MD50` | MD50 ← 83.82 (REAL) |

**Final Answer:** The temperature corresponding to a raw input of $18{,}500$ is **$83.82°C$**.

---

### Question Choice B

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**  
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of the PLC's Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) and describe how an `ADD` instruction is executed internally. Discuss the role of the Status Word in this process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flow measurement system uses an orifice plate with the equation $Q = K\sqrt{\Delta P}$, where $K = 0.082$ and $\Delta P$ is the differential pressure in kPa. If the PLC receives a raw $\Delta P$ value of $9{,}000$ Pa (i.e., $9$ kPa) from the differential pressure transmitter, calculate the flow rate $Q$. Show all PLC instruction steps. Also explain what happens if the raw $\Delta P$ value becomes negative due to a sensor fault. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q4(a) — ALU Architecture and ADD Instruction (7 marks)

**Block Diagram Description:**

The PLC's Central Processing Unit (CPU) contains an Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) which is the dedicated hardware block for all arithmetic and logical operations. The ALU receives data from the internal data bus, performs the operation, and outputs the result back to the bus.

**Major Sub-blocks:**

1. **Operand Registers (Accumulator 1 and Accumulator 2):** Temporary storage for source operands $A$ and $B$.
2. **ALU Core:** Performs $A + B$, $A - B$, $A \times B$, $A \div B$, and bitwise operations.
3. **Operation Decoder:** Decodes the instruction opcode and configures the ALU accordingly.
4. **Flags/Status Register:** Stores condition codes: OV, OS, CC0, CC1, N (Negative).
5. **Internal Data Bus:** Connects the ALU to memory (RAM) and output image table.

**Execution of an `ADD` Instruction — Step by Step:**

1. The CPU's instruction decoder identifies the `ADD` opcode.
2. The first source operand $A$ is fetched from its memory location and loaded into **Accumulator 1**.
3. The second source operand $B$ is fetched and loaded into **Accumulator 2**.
4. The ALU's internal adder circuit (built from full-adder logic gates) computes the sum $A + B$.
5. The result is loaded back into Accumulator 1, and the previous content of Accu1 is shifted to Accu2.
6. The result is written to the destination memory location specified in the instruction.
7. Simultaneously, the Status Word is updated:
   - **CC0 (Condition Code 0):** Set if result $= 0$.
   - **CC1 (Condition Code 1):** Set if result $> 0$.
   - **OV (Overflow):** Set if signed result exceeds the representable range.
   - **OS (Stored Overflow):** Latched version of OV.
   - **N (Negative):** Set if MSB of result is 1 (negative number).

**Role of the Status Word:** The flags set in the Status Word are used by the next conditional instruction (typically a jump or comparison). For example, after an `ADD` instruction, a `JP` (Jump if Positive) instruction checks CC1 and triggers a branch if positive.

---

#### Model Solution for Q4(b) — Orifice Flow Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1: State the given equation and values.**

$$
Q = K \sqrt{\Delta P}, \quad K = 0.082, \quad \Delta P = 9 \text{ kPa} = 9{,}000 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the square root.**

$$
\sqrt{\Delta P} = \sqrt{9{,}000}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the square root numerically.**

$$
9{,}000 = 900 \times 10 = 30^2 \times 10
$$
$$
\sqrt{9{,}000} = 30 \sqrt{10} = 30 \times 3.1623 = 94.867
$$

**Step 4: Multiply by $K$.**

$$
Q = 0.082 \times 94.867 = 7.779 \text{ m}^3\text{/hr}
$$

**Step 5: State the PLC instruction sequence.**

| Rung | Instruction | Purpose | Result in Memory |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | `L IW0` (load $\Delta P$ raw) | Read input | Accu1 = 9,000 |
| 2 | `ITD` (convert INT to DINT) | Promote data type | Accu1 = 9,000 (DINT) |
| 3 | `DTR` (convert DINT to REAL) | Allow fractional result | Accu1 = 9,000.0 |
| 4 | `SQRT` (square root) | Apply $\sqrt{\Delta P}$ | Accu1 = 94.867 |
| 5 | `L 0.082; *R` (multiply by K) | Apply flow equation | Accu1 = 7.779 |
| 6 | `T MD100` (transfer to memory) | Store result | MD100 = 7.779 m³/hr |

**Step 6: Discuss the negative $\Delta P$ sensor fault scenario.**

If the sensor fails and the raw $\Delta P$ value becomes negative (say, $-9{,}000$ Pa), the following occurs:

- The square root of a negative number is **not defined in real mathematics**. The square root of $-9{,}000 = \sqrt{-1} \times \sqrt{9{,}000} = j \times 94.867$, which is an **imaginary number**.
- In a PLC, the `SQR` (or `SQRT`) instruction detects this condition. The behavior is platform-dependent:
  - In **Siemens S7**, the `SQRT` instruction with a negative operand sets the **OV flag** and returns an undefined result.
  - In **Allen-Bradley**, the square root of a negative number produces a result of $0$ or generates a major error.
- **Best Practice:** The PLC program should include a pre-check that ensures $\Delta P \geq 0$ before executing the square root. If the value is negative, an error-handling routine should clamp the result to zero and raise an alarm.

**Final Answer:** The flow rate $Q$ is **$7.779$ m³/hr**, and a negative $\Delta P$ input must be guarded against before the square root is taken.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on Arithmetic Function Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the data type limit:** Always state whether the operation uses `INT`, `DINT`, or `REAL`. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you just write "add two numbers" without specifying the data type.
> 
> 2. **Confusing CC0 and CC1:** `CC0 = 1` means **result is zero**. `CC1 = 1` means **result is positive**. `CC0 = 0` AND `CC1 = 0` means **result is negative**. This is asked verbatim in past papers.
> 
> 3. **Not showing the flag check after arithmetic:** After an arithmetic instruction, the next instruction in the ladder should be either a comparison or a conditional jump that checks the status flags. Examiners expect to see this in the program listing.
> 
> 4. **Writing $x/0 = 0$:** Division by zero does **not** yield zero. It is either a trapped CPU error (integer) or a special IEEE 754 infinity value (REAL). Writing zero will cost you full marks.
> 
> 5. **Skipping the L→I→T notation in Siemens programs:** KTU examiners expect the **L (Load), I (Integer op), T (Transfer)** mnemonic sequence to be written explicitly. Simply writing "ADD MW10, MW12" without the storage step loses a mark.
> 
> 6. **Mixing signed and unsigned:** A `WORD` (unsigned 16-bit) has range 0 to 65,535, while `INT` (signed 16-bit) has range $-32{,}768$ to $+32{,}767$. Multiplying a `WORD` value of $60{,}000$ by 2 does *not* overflow as an `INT` value of $-7{,}536$ would. Always clarify signed vs unsigned.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — PLC Arithmetic Functions**

- [x] **Definition:** Arithmetic functions are mathematical operations (`ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `SQR`, `SIN`, `COS`, `TAN`, `LN`, `EXP`) executed by the PLC's ALU on data held in memory registers.

- [x] **Three-stage execution:** Fetch → Execute (ALU) → Store + Flag Update. This happens within the program scan phase of the PLC cycle.

- [x] **Data Types and Limits:**
  - `INT` (16-bit signed): $-32{,}768$ to $+32{,}767$
  - `DINT` (32-bit signed): $\pm 2.1 \times 10^9$
  - `REAL` (32-bit IEEE 754): $\pm 3.4 \times 10^{38}$, ~7 decimal digits precision

- [x] **Status Word Flags:** OV (overflow), OS (stored overflow), CC0 (result = 0), CC1 (result > 0), N (negative result).

- [x] **Linear Scaling Formula:** $PV = \left(\dfrac{Raw - Raw_{min}}{Raw_{max} - Raw_{min}}\right) \times EU_{span} + EU_{min}$

- [x] **Overflow Consequences:** The result wraps around (two's complement), the OV flag is set, and the result is numerically wrong. Programmer must check the flag.

- [x] **Division by Zero:** Integer math → CPU traps error → STOP mode (if no OB121). REAL math → result is $\pm \infty$ (special bit pattern). Always pre-check the divisor.

- [x] **Square Root of Negative:** Mathematically undefined (imaginary). PLC sets OV flag. Programmer should pre-check $\Delta P \geq 0$.

- [x] **Multiplication Overflow:** Most common pitfall — multiplying two `INT` values can easily exceed 16 bits. Use `MUL_DI` (32-bit) for safety.

- [x] **Industrial Applications:** PID error calculation, flow from $\Delta P$, thermocouple linearization, tank volume, setpoint ramping, recipe scaling.

- [x] **Vendor-Neutral Concept:** Although Siemens, Allen-Bradley, and Schneider use different mnemonics, the underlying math, flag behavior, and overflow detection logic are **mathematically identical**.

- [x] **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - Linear scaling: $Y = mX + C$
  - Flow: $Q = K\sqrt{\Delta P}$
  - Error: $e = SP - PV$
  - Cylinder volume: $V = \pi r^2 h$
  - Two's complement range: $-2^{N-1}$ to $2^{N-1} - 1$

- [x] **Always remember:** Arithmetic functions are **deterministic and cycle-time bounded** — they execute in microseconds for `INT` math and slightly longer for `REAL` math. In real-time control systems, this deterministic timing is critical.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Master Control Reset - Skip - Jump and Move Instruction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT604 - PLC & DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Master Control Reset - Skip - Jump and Move Instruction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Master Control Reset (MCR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Master Control Reset (MCR)* is a programmable relay instruction used in PLC ladder logic to **selectively disable a contiguous zone (block) of rungs** in the ladder program. When the MCR control line is energized (TRUE), all rungs within the MCR zone execute normally. When the MCR line is de-energized (FALSE), **all non-retentive outputs within the zone are forcibly turned OFF**, regardless of their logical rung conditions.

### Conceptual Analogy

Think of MCR like a **master power switch on a specific floor of a hotel**:
- The MCR instruction is the master switch.
- The "MCR zone" (the block of rungs between MCR and END MCR) is that specific floor.
- When the master switch is ON → all rooms (outputs) operate based on individual switches (rung conditions).
- When the master switch is OFF → **every light, fan, and AC on that floor is forced OFF**, even if someone left the switch ON. However, **memory retentive coils (like latches and timers with retentive bits) still retain their state**, like the hotel's fire-alarm memory that stays on even if power is cut.

> [!NOTE]
> In **Allen-Bradley (Rockwell) PLCs**, the MCR instruction is denoted as `MCR` and is paired with an unconditional rung labeled `MCR` again to terminate the zone. In **Siemens S7**, the equivalent is **Master Control Relay (MCR)** with `MCRA`, `MCR<`, and `MCRD` instructions.

---

## 1.2 Skip Instruction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Skip (SKP)* is a conditional branch-flow instruction that **skips the immediately following instruction/rung** when its input condition evaluates TRUE. It enables conditional bypass of specific operations within a scan cycle without altering the program flow structure.

### Conceptual Analogy

Imagine reading a **recipe book with sticky notes**:
- You see a step that says "*If you don't like spicy food → skip to step 12*".
- The **SKIP instruction is exactly this sticky note**: if the condition is true, the next instruction is *jumped over* (not executed); if false, execution continues normally to the next instruction.

---

## 1.3 Jump Instruction (JMP / JMPN)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Jump (JMP)* transfers program execution **forward or backward to a labeled rung (LBL)** within the same program file. **Jump Non-conditional (JMPN)** performs the jump unconditionally. The jump bypasses all rungs between the JMP and the target LBL, **without forcing their outputs OFF** — outputs retain their last scanned state.

### Conceptual Analogy

Picture a **YouTube video with chapters**:
- You are at the *Settings* chapter.
- You click "*Jump to Credits*" — the video skips over *Q&A*, *Behind the Scenes*, and *Sponsor message*, going directly to *Credits*.
- Crucially, those skipped sections **don't reset** — they simply weren't visited. In PLC terms, the outputs in skipped rungs **do NOT turn off**; they hold their previous state, unlike MCR which forces them OFF.

---

## 1.4 Move Instruction (MOV)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Move (MOV)* is a data-handling instruction that **copies the content of a source word/register into a destination word/register** without modifying the source. It is the most fundamental data transfer instruction in PLC programming, used for setting preset values, transferring analog readings, copying block data, and initializing variables.

### Conceptual Analogy

The Move instruction is like a **photocopier**:
- The **source** is the original document (remains untouched).
- The **destination** is the copy.
- The original (source) is **never destroyed** — only duplicated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common variants:** `MOV` (move), `MVM` (masked move), `MOVP` (pulse-move / one-shot), `BTW` (block transfer write), `BTD` (block transfer discrete) — used across Allen-Bradley SLC/PLC-5/ControlLogix families.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 Master Control Reset — Operational Logic

### How MCR Works (Step-by-Step)

1. **MCR is initiated** by an input/output/branch condition on a rung.
2. **An `MCR` rung with no conditions** marks the **end of the MCR zone**.
3. When MCR input is **TRUE (1/ON)**:
    - All rungs within the zone execute with normal logic.
    - Outputs operate based on their respective rung conditions.
4. When MCR input is **FALSE (0/OFF)**:
    - **All non-retentive outputs are reset to 0 (OFF)**.
    - Timers are reset to **0** (unless retentive timer type `RTO` is used).
    - **Counters retain** their accumulated and preset values (counters are retentive by default).
    - **Latched (O-L) and unlatched (O-U) coils behave normally** — latches retain state.
    - **Retentive timers (RTO)** retain their accumulated values.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The most commonly tested property is **what happens to retentive vs. non-retentive elements**. KTU examiners frequently ask: *"When MCR is OFF, what is the state of: (a) a standard timer TON, (b) an up-counter CTU, (c) an OTL latch?"*

### MCR Ladder Representation

```
         Rung Header (Condition for MCR Zone)
   ───┤ ├─────────────────────────────────( MCR )────
   ───┤ ├─────────────────────────────────(    )────   ← Inside MCR zone
   ───┤ ├─────────────────────────────────( MCR )────   ← End of MCR zone
```

---

## 2.2 Skip Instruction — Operational Logic

1. The `SKP` instruction appears as a conditional output branch.
2. **If SKP's input condition is TRUE** → the **immediately following instruction is bypassed (not executed in that scan)**.
3. **If SKP's input is FALSE** → the next instruction executes normally.
