**Step-by-step explanation:**
- The static pressure at the bottom of a fluid column is directly proportional to the height of the column.
- $\rho$ = 1000 kg/m³ (standard for water at 4 °C).
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s² is the standard gravitational acceleration.
- Therefore, for every **1 metre** of water height, the sensor sees **9810 Pa** = **9.81 kPa**.

If the transmitter is calibrated for **0–5 metres** of water (range 0 to 5 m), then full scale = $9810 \times 5 = 49050$ Pa ≈ **49.05 kPa**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Engineering Unit Scaling from Raw ADC Count

A 16-bit Allen-Bradley MicroLogix AI module returns a raw integer between **0 and 32760**. The level sensor is calibrated for **0 to 10 metres**. The PLC receives a raw count $N = 24570$. Compute the level in metres.

Given:
- $N_{min} = 0$
- $N_{max} = 32760$
- $EU_{min} = 0$ m
- $EU_{max} = 10$ m
- $N = 24570$

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU &= \left(\dfrac{N - N_{min}}{N_{max} - N_{min}}\right)(EU_{max} - EU_{min}) + EU_{min} \\
EU &= \left(\dfrac{24570 - 0}{32760 - 0}\right)(10 - 0) + 0 \\
EU &= \left(\dfrac{24570}{32760}\right) \times 10 \\
EU &= 0.7500 \times 10 \\
EU &= 7.500 \;\;\text{metres}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The raw count occupies 75 % of full scale, so the level occupies 75 % of 10 m, i.e., 7.5 m.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inverse Scaling for Analog Output (Pump Speed Command)

The PID block computes a control effort of **65 %**. We must convert this to a raw count for the AO module (same 16-bit range, 0–32760) to drive a variable-frequency drive (VFD) controlling a pump (4–20 mA output where 4 mA = 0 % and 20 mA = 100 %).

> [!NOTE]
> **Important nuance:** For 4–20 mA outputs, the lower raw count corresponds to 4 mA, NOT 0 mA.

For a 4–20 mA output where raw **0 = 4 mA (0 %)** and raw **32760 = 20 mA (100 %)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{out} &= \left(\dfrac{\%-EU - EU_{min}}{EU_{max} - EU_{min}}\right)(N_{max} - N_{min}) + N_{min} \\
N_{out} &= \left(\dfrac{65 - 0}{100 - 0}\right)(32760 - 0) + 0 \\
N_{out} &= 0.65 \times 32760 \\
N_{out} &= 21294 \;\;\text{counts}
\end{aligned}
$$

Corresponding current: $I = 4 + (0.65 \times 16) = 4 + 10.4 = 14.4$ mA.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — ADC Resolution Calculation

A 12-bit ADC is used with a 0–10 V input signal. Compute the **step size (resolution)** $Q$ and the digital output when the input is **6.4 V**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}-1} \\
Q &= \dfrac{10}{2^{12}-1} = \dfrac{10}{4095} \\
Q &= 0.002442 \;\;\text{V/count} \approx 2.44 \;\text{mV/count}
\end{aligned}
$$

For an input of 6.4 V:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{V_{in}}{Q} = \dfrac{6.4}{0.002442} \\
N &= 2620 \;\;\text{counts (rounded)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — PID Position-Form Output for a Single Step

A PLC PID block is configured with:
- $K_{p} = 2.5$, $K_{i} = 0.8$ /s, $K_{d} = 0.05$ s
- Sample time $T = 0.1$ s
- Setpoint $SP = 5.0$ m
- Previous error $e_{k-1} = 0.4$ m
- Current error $e_{k} = 0.2$ m
- Sum of errors $\Sigma e_{k} = 3.0$ m·s (cumulative integral sum)
- Output limits: 0–100 %

Compute $u_{k}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{k} &= K_{p}\,e_{k} + K_{i}\,T\,\Sigma e_{k} + \dfrac{K_{d}}{T}(e_{k} - e_{k-1}) \\
u_{k} &= (2.5 \times 0.2) + (0.8 \times 0.1 \times 3.0) + \left(\dfrac{0.05}{0.1}\right)(0.2 - 0.4) \\
u_{k} &= 0.50 + 0.24 + (0.5 \times -0.2) \\
u_{k} &= 0.50 + 0.24 - 0.10 \\
u_{k} &= 0.64 \;\;\text{(i.e., 64 \% pump speed)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix — Tank Level Control Components

| Subsystem | Hardware | Signal Type | Range | PLC Interaction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensor** | Hydrostatic Pressure Tx | 4–20 mA current | 0–5 m water column | Wired to AI0 |
| **AI Module** | 1769-IF8 (16-bit) | Differential V | 0–10 V (after shunt) | Slot 1 |
| **CPU** | MicroLogix 1400 | Digital | — | Executes PID every 100 ms |
| **AO Module** | 1769-OF2 | Current output | 4–20 mA | Drives VFD |
| **Actuator** | Variable-Frequency Drive + Pump | 3-phase AC | 0–50 Hz | Maintains level |
| **HMI** | PanelView 800 | Ethernet/IP | — | Operator setpoint entry |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Block Diagram — Tank Level Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP - 5.0 m] --> SUM
    PV[Process Variable PV - Level Transmitter] --> SUM
    SUM((Summing Junction e = SP - PV)) --> CTRL
    CTRL[PID Controller in PLC CPU] --> AO
    AO[Analog Output Module 4-20 mA] --> VFD
    VFD[Variable Frequency Drive] --> PUMP
    PUMP[Centrifugal Pump] --> TANK
    TANK[Tank with Hydrostatic Sensor] --> PV
    TANK -. Level Feedback .-> HMI[Operator HMI Display]
```

## 4.2 Signal Processing Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Physical Variable - Liquid Level h in m] --> B[Hydrostatic Pressure Sensor P = rho g h]
    B --> C[Transmitter Electronics - 4 to 20 mA Loop]
    C --> D[Shunt Resistor 250 ohm - Converts to 1 to 5 V]
    D --> E[Analog Input Module - 16 bit ADC]
    E --> F[Raw Digital Count N - 0 to 32760]
    F --> G[Scaling Function Block in PLC - SCL or SCP instruction]
    G --> H[Engineering Units - Level in metres]
    H --> I[PID Control Block - Compares with SP]
    I --> J[Control Output 0 to 100 percent]
    J --> K[Inverse Scaling - Raw AO Count]
    K --> L[DAC in Analog Output Module]
    L --> M[4 to 20 mA to VFD]
    M --> N[Variable Speed Pump - Final Control Element]
    N -. Feedback to Tank .-> A
```

## 4.3 Ladder Logic Structure for Analog Scaling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 - Read AI Module - MOV instruction] --> S2[Step 2 - Apply SCP Instruction - Scale to EU]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 - Compare with Setpoint - GRT LES]
    S3 --> S4{Decision - High Low or OK}
    S4 -->|Level too high| S5[Increase Pump Speed - AO writes higher mA]
    S4 -->|Level too low| S6[Decrease Pump Speed - AO writes lower mA]
    S4 -->|Within deadband| S7[Hold Previous Output]
    S5 --> S8[Update HMI Register]
    S6 --> S8
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Loop back to S1 every scan cycle]
```

## 4.4 Sensor-to-Pump Signal Flow Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mental Map for Examinations** — Memorise this closed loop:
> `Level → Pressure → 4-20 mA → AI Module → ADC → Raw Count → Scaling → EU → PID → % → Inverse Scaling → AO Module → DAC → 4-20 mA → VFD → Pump → Tank (back to Level)`

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1, Q1a]**
Explain the concept of **Live Zero** in analog current signalling. Why is a 4–20 mA signal preferred over a 0–20 mA signal in industrial applications?

**Model Answer:**

The term **Live Zero** refers to the use of **4 mA** as the lower end of the signal range instead of 0 mA. This means that when the process variable is at its minimum value, the loop still carries **4 mA** of current — the loop is "alive."

**Advantages over 0–20 mA:**
1. **Fault Detection:** If the current drops below 4 mA (e.g., 0 mA), the PLC immediately detects a **broken wire, open loop, or sensor failure**.
2. **Two-wire transmitter powering:** The 4 mA minimum also supplies enough current to power the field transmitter electronics.
3. **Noise immunity:** Current signals are unaffected by cable resistance, ensuring accurate transmission over hundreds of metres.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "4–20 mA is preferred because it is international standard." This is **wrong** as a primary answer. The **Live Zero fault-detection** point is mandatory for full marks.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2, Q1b]**
With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional blocks of an Analog Input Module** in a PLC.

**Model Answer:**

An Analog Input Module consists of the following functional blocks in sequence:

1. **Signal Conditioning Block** — Filters noise, provides surge protection, and converts current to voltage (using a 250 Ω shunt for 4–20 mA).
2. **Multiplexer (MUX)** — Selects one of multiple input channels sequentially to share a single ADC.
3. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit** — Holds the analog voltage steady during the conversion process.
4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — Converts the analog voltage into a binary digital count (typically 12-bit or 16-bit).
5. **Isolation** — Optical or transformer isolation between field side and logic side.
6. **Output Register / Buffer** — Stores the converted digital count for the CPU to read via the backplane.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Drawing a single box labelled "AI Module" without sub-blocks will fetch only partial marks. **Always show the internal functional architecture**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Q2a/2b pair]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe a **closed-loop tank level control system** using a PLC. Clearly identify the role of the **analog input module, PID controller, and analog output module**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hydrostatic pressure transmitter is installed at the bottom of a water tank. The transmitter output is **4–20 mA** corresponding to a level range of **0 to 4 metres**. A 16-bit Analog Input module is used. Calculate:
   (i) The current signal when the level is **2.8 metres**.
   (ii) The raw digital count corresponding to this level.
   (iii) The voltage across the internal 250 Ω shunt resistor at this level. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A closed-loop tank level control system continuously senses the liquid level and adjusts the pump speed to maintain a desired setpoint. The block diagram (already shown in Section 4.1) consists of:

1. **Level Sensor (Hydrostatic Pressure Transmitter):** Mounted at the bottom of the tank, it senses the hydrostatic pressure $P = \rho g h$ and converts it into a proportional **4–20 mA** current signal.
2. **Analog Input Module:** Receives the 4–20 mA signal, converts it to 1–5 V via an internal 250 Ω shunt, and uses a 16-bit ADC to produce a raw count (0 to 32760).
3. **PLC CPU with PID Block:** Reads the scaled engineering-unit value, subtracts it from the operator setpoint to compute error $e(t)$, and executes the **PID algorithm** every scan cycle.
4. **Analog Output Module:** Receives the PID output as a percentage (0–100 %), converts it to a raw count, and uses a **DAC** to produce a **4–20 mA** current signal.
5. **VFD + Pump:** The current drives the Variable Frequency Drive, which adjusts pump motor speed, controlling inflow to the tank.
6. **Feedback Path:** The tank level rises or falls, closing the loop.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Level range: 0 to 4 m
- Current range: 4 to 20 mA
- $n = 16$ bits → $N_{max} = 2^{16} - 16 = 65520$ or simply 32760 (unipolar) depending on module; we use **0 to 32760** as per Allen-Bradley convention.
- Shunt resistor $R = 250\ \Omega$

**(i) Current at 2.8 m level:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Level} &= \dfrac{2.8}{4} \times 100 = 70\% \\
I &= 4 + (0.70 \times 16) \\
I &= 4 + 11.2 = 15.2 \;\;\text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Raw count:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{I - 4}{16} \times 32760 \\
N &= \dfrac{15.2 - 4}{16} \times 32760 \\
N &= \dfrac{11.2}{16} \times 32760 \\
N &= 0.70 \times 32760 \\
N &= 22932 \;\;\text{counts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Voltage across shunt:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= I \times R \\
V &= 15.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 250 \\
V &= 3.80 \;\;\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Q3a/3b pair]**

**(a)** Explain the **scaling** of analog signals in a PLC with reference to a level control application. State the formulas used for both **input scaling** and **output scaling**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A PLC uses a 12-bit ADC for a level sensor with range **0–10 metres** and current signal **0–20 mA** (not 4–20 mA). Compute the **ADC resolution (step size)** and the **digital count** when the level is **3.5 m**. If the shunt resistor is 500 Ω, what is the ADC input voltage? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Scaling** is the mathematical process of converting a raw ADC count into a meaningful engineering unit (e.g., metres, °C, L/s) and vice versa.

**Input Scaling (Raw Count → Engineering Unit):**

$$
EU = EU_{min} + \dfrac{(N - N_{min})\,(EU_{max} - EU_{min})}{N_{max} - N_{min}}
$$

**Output Scaling (Engineering Unit → Raw Count):**

$$
N = N_{min} + \dfrac{(EU - EU_{min})\,(N_{max} - N_{min})}{EU_{max} - EU_{min}}
$$

**Why scaling is needed:** A 16-bit ADC always returns 0 to 65535 regardless of whether the sensor measures 0–10 m or 0–100 °C. The PLC user must therefore tell the CPU how to interpret the count.

**Level control example:** For a 0–5 m level transmitter producing 4–20 mA on a 0–32760 AI module:
- Input scaling: $Level = (N / 32760) \times 5$ metres.
- Output scaling: Pump speed command $N_{AO} = (Speed\% / 100) \times 32760$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $n = 12$ bits → $2^{12} - 1 = 4095$ counts.
- Full-scale voltage $V_{FS} = 10$ V (assumed for the ADC input stage).
- Shunt $R = 500\ \Omega$.
- Level range 0–10 m, signal 0–20 mA.
- Level to compute: $h = 3.5$ m.

**(i) ADC Resolution (Step Size):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{12}-1} = \dfrac{10}{4095} \\
Q &= 0.002442 \;\;\text{V/count} = 2.44 \;\text{mV/count}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Current at 3.5 m level (linear, 0–20 mA):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \dfrac{3.5}{10} \times 20 = 7.0 \;\;\text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Voltage across shunt:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= I \times R = 7.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 500 \\
V &= 3.5 \;\;\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Digital count at 3.5 V:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{V_{in}}{Q} = \dfrac{3.5}{0.002442} \\
N &= 1433 \;\;\text{counts (rounded)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** When the signal is **0–20 mA** (not 4–20 mA), students mistakenly use the **Live Zero offset** (i.e., subtract 4). This is incorrect. Always read the question carefully to identify whether the range is 0–20 mA or 4–20 mA.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Live Zero = 4 mA:** The single most important concept for distinguishing a healthy 4–20 mA loop from a broken one.
- **Resolution rule:** Higher bits = finer resolution. A 16-bit ADC has $2^{16} - 1 = 65535$ steps.
- **Shunt resistor = 250 Ω** is the default that turns 4–20 mA into 1–5 V. Anything else (e.g., 500 Ω) gives 2–10 V.
- **Scaling formula is linear interpolation** between two known endpoints — nothing more, nothing less.
- **PID output is a percentage (0–100 %)**, NOT a current directly. It must be inverse-scaled to a raw AO count.
- **Hydrostatic pressure** $P = \rho g h$ is the physical principle behind submersible and bottom-mounted level sensors.
- **Closed-loop = feedback.** Without the level sensor feeding back to the PLC, the system is open-loop and cannot self-correct.
- **Ladder logic analog functions:** Look for instructions named `SCP`, `SCL`, `MOV`, `PID` in your PLC programming manual (Allen-Bradley / Siemens S7).
- **Tank cross-section area** $A = \pi r^{2}$ is required for **volume** calculations; often used as a follow-up to level measurement.
- **VFD** = Variable Frequency Drive, the standard industrial actuator for variable-speed pump control.
- **HMI** (Human-Machine Interface) is the operator's window — shows real-time level, setpoint, and pump speed.
- **Sampling time** $T$ in discrete PID is typically **100 ms** for tank level — too fast wastes CPU; too slow causes oscillation.
- **Dead-band / hysteresis** prevents the pump from hunting (rapid on/off cycling) when the level hovers near the setpoint.
- **KTU examiner expectation:** Always show formula, substitution, and final answer with units in three separate lines.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bottle Filling System and Sequential Switching of Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT604 - PLC & DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bottle Filling System and Sequential Switching of Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Bottle Filling System (BFS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> A **Bottle Filling System (BFS)** is a classic *sequential PLC-controlled automation process* in which empty bottles carried by a conveyor are detected, positioned under a filling nozzle, filled with a measured quantity of liquid, and then dispatched for capping — all orchestrated through **discrete I/O**, **timers**, and **counters** within the PLC scan cycle.

The system typically integrates **three functional units**:

1. **Conveyor Unit** — moves bottles using a motor (M) controlled via a contactor.
2. **Sensing Unit** — proximity / photoelectric sensor detects bottle arrival; level sensor confirms fill.
3. **Filling Unit** — a solenoid valve opens to dispense liquid into the bottle.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a **tea-vending kiosk** where the vendor waits for a cup, places it under the tap, presses a lever, waits for the cup to fill, then slides the next cup into place. Now imagine this done **100 times an hour** without the vendor — that is exactly what a PLC-driven BFS does. The PLC acts as the "vendor's brain," replacing manual judgment with sensor inputs and timed relay actions.

> [!NOTE]
> Standard BFS Industry Metrics:
> - **Throughput**: 30 – 120 bottles/min
> - **Operating Voltage**: **24 V DC** (sensors) / **230 V AC** (contactors)
> - **Cycle Time (T<sub>cycle</sub>)**: typically **3 – 6 seconds** per bottle
> - **IEC 61131-3 Languages Used**: **Ladder Diagram (LD)** is preferred; **SFC** for complex sequences.

---

### 1.2 Sequential Switching of Motors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> **Sequential Switching of Motors** refers to a PLC-based control strategy in which **two or more motors are switched ON and OFF in a predefined, time-dependent or condition-dependent order** to ensure safe start-up, load sharing, or process staging. Common implementations include *Forward/Reverse (F/R) motor control*, *Star-Delta starting*, and *time-lag sequential ON/OFF* of multiple pump or conveyor motors.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of the **lights in a KTU exam hall** — the invigilator switches on Row 1, then Row 2, then Row 3, with a small gap between each row to avoid an electrical surge (inrush current). That deliberate, time-staggered switching of multiple loads is exactly what **sequential motor switching** automates using a PLC.

**Three canonical variants** the KTU syllabus emphasizes:

| Variant | Purpose | Key Element |
|---|---|---|
| **Forward/Reverse (F/R) Motor** | Reverses rotation direction | Interlock + limit switches |
| **Star-Delta Starter** | Reduces starting current to ~1/3 | Two contactors + timer |
| **Sequential ON/OFF of Multiple Motors** | Staged load activation | Cascaded timers |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard motor control metrics in PLC context:
> - **Motor rated power**: typically **0.5 – 7.5 kW** for lab/small industrial PLC panels.
> - **Inrush current**: **6 × full-load current (FLC)** during direct-on-line (DOL) start.
> - **Star-Delta transition time (T<sub>sd</sub>)**: typically **3 – 10 seconds**.

---

### 1.3 Why These Topics Matter in KTU 2024 Scheme

Both topics are **direct applications of PLC programming in industrial automation** and are routinely examined because they test a student's ability to:

- Translate a **physical process into an I/O allocation table**.
- Design **ladder logic (LAD)** with timers, counters, and latching coils.
- Apply **interlocks** for electrical and mechanical safety.
- Reason about the **PLC scan cycle** order of execution.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bottle Filling System — Operating Logic

The BFS follows a **finite, repeatable sequence** that maps cleanly onto PLC scan logic:

1. **Idle State** — Conveyor is OFF, no bottle present. PLC waits for start pushbutton (PB_START).
2. **Conveyor Energized** — Motor M1 runs, carrying bottles forward.
3. **Bottle Detection** — Proximity sensor (PS) detects bottle at filling station → triggers a *latch*.
4. **Conveyor Stopped** — Motor M1 de-energizes; bottle is held under the nozzle.
5. **Filling Initiated** — Solenoid valve (SV) opens; Timer T1 begins counting fill duration.
6. **Fill Complete** — Timer T1 done (DN) → SV closes; an optional level sensor (LS) provides redundancy.
7. **Cycle Reset** — Conveyor restarts; counter C1 increments by 1 to log bottles filled.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The "fill duration" T1 is derived from:
> $$ T_1 = \frac{V_{bottle}}{\dot{Q}_{valve}} $$
> where **V<sub>bottle</sub>** = bottle volume (mL), **Q̇<sub>valve</sub>** = nozzle flow rate (mL/s).

---

### 2.2 Sequential Switching of Motors — Operating Logic

#### 2.2.1 Forward / Reverse Motor with Interlock

A motor (e.g., 3-phase induction motor) must rotate in **forward (FWD)** or **reverse (REV)** direction, but **never both at the same time** to avoid a phase-to-phase short circuit.

- **Electrical Interlock**: NO contact of REV pushbutton placed in series with FWD branch, and vice versa.
- **Mechanical Interlock**: Contactors physically prevent simultaneous engagement (auxiliary NC contacts).
- **Limit Switches**: LS_FWD and LS_REV stop the motor at end-of-travel positions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 syllabus highlight:** Interlocks are *safety-critical*. Examiners frequently award marks for explicitly stating both **electrical** and **mechanical** interlock strategies.

#### 2.2.2 Star-Delta Starter

Used to limit the **starting inrush current** of large 3-phase induction motors.

- Initially, windings are connected in **Star (Y)** → line voltage across each winding = **V<sub>L</sub>/√3** → current ≈ **1/3 of DOL**.
- After time **T<sub>sd</sub>** (3 – 10 s), PLC switches to **Delta (Δ)** → full torque operation.

#### 2.2.3 Sequential ON/OFF of Multiple Motors

Motors M1, M2, M3 are switched ON at intervals of **Δt seconds**, and switched OFF in reverse (or LIFO) order. This avoids simultaneous inrush on the supply.

$$ T_{ON}(M_i) = (i - 1) \cdot \Delta t, \quad i = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, n $$

$$ T_{OFF}(M_i) = (n - i) \cdot \Delta t $$

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fill Time | $T_1 = V / \dot{Q}$ | seconds (s) | V in mL, Q̇ in mL/s |
| 2 | Production Rate | $R = 60 / T_{cycle}$ | bottles/min | T<sub>cycle</sub> in s |
| 3 | Total Bottles | $N = \sum \text{Counter pulses}$ | dimensionless | Resets on cycle-end |
| 4 | Star-Delta Current Ratio | $I_{star} = I_{DOL} / 3$ | A | Approximately |
| 5 | Star-Delta Voltage Ratio | $V_{phase,star} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$ | V | Per winding |
| 6 | Motor Starting Time | $T_{sd} = 3 \text{ to } 10$ | s | Application-dependent |
| 7 | Stagger Delay | $\Delta t = T_{sd} / n$ | s | Equal spacing |
| 8 | Inrush Multiplier | $I_{inrush} \approx 6 \cdot I_{FLC}$ | A | For DOL start |
| 9 | PLC Scan Time | $T_{scan} = f(\text{program length})$ | ms | Typically 5 – 20 ms |
| 10 | Interlock Constraint | $FWD \cdot \overline{REV} = 1$ | Boolean | Mutual exclusion |

> [!NOTE]
> The **vertical pipe symbol** has been replaced with **\vert** / explicit text to keep the table syntax intact.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bottle Filling Systems** are deployed in pharmaceuticals, beverages, cosmetics, and chemicals — improving **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** by 20–35 %.
- **Sequential Motor Switching** is essential in pumping stations (sewage, water treatment), HVAC chillers, and conveyor lines in mining/manufacturing — preventing **grid brownouts** during simultaneous start-up.
- Both systems are the **standard KTU viva question** for assessing a student's grasp of **PLC scan cycle, I/O mapping, timers, and counters**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 BFS — I/O Allocation Table Derivation

We begin by translating the physical process into a **PLC I/O address table**, which is the *first mandatory step* in every KTU solution.

**Step 1 — Identify Inputs:**
A bottle arrives via conveyor; we need to *detect* it. Two sensor choices:
- **Inductive proximity sensor** (metallic bottles only).
- **Photoelectric (retro-reflective) sensor** (universal — preferred).

Decision → **Photoelectric sensor PS_BOTTLE** chosen.

We also need:
- **Start pushbutton** (PB_START, NO momentary).
- **Stop pushbutton** (PB_STOP, NC momentary).
- **Level sensor LS_FULL** (optional, redundant).

**Step 2 — Identify Outputs:**
- **Conveyor motor M1** → contactor K1.
- **Filling solenoid valve SV** → relay R1.

**Step 3 — Assign Addresses (e.g., Siemens S7-1200 convention):**

| Symbol | Tag | Address | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PB_START | START | I0.0 | Input (NO) | Start pushbutton |
| PB_STOP | STOP | I0.1 | Input (NC) | Stop pushbutton |
| PS_BOTTLE | SENSOR | I0.2 | Input | Bottle detected |
| LS_FULL | LEV_OK | I0.3 | Input | Level sensor |
| K1 (M1) | MOTOR | Q0.0 | Output | Conveyor motor |
| R1 (SV) | VALVE | Q0.1 | Output | Fill solenoid |

**Step 4 — Internal Memory:**
- **T1**: Timer (preset = fill duration).
- **C1**: Counter (total bottles filled).

| Symbol | Tag | Address | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | FILL_TMR | T37 | Timer (TON) | Fill duration timer |
| C1 | BOT_CTR | C0 | Counter (CTU) | Bottle counter |
| M0.0 | RUN_FLAG | M0.0 | Memory bit | System running flag |

---

### 3.2 BFS — Ladder Logic Derivation (Step-by-Step)

We will build the ladder program in **four rungs**, the *exact* structure expected by KTU examiners.

**Rung 1 — System RUN Latch:**
The system must "remember" that START was pressed until STOP is pressed or a fault occurs.

$$ RUN\_FLAG = (START + RUN\_FLAG) \cdot \overline{STOP} $$

Ladder:
```
|   START    RUN_FLAG      STOP     |
|---[ ]-----+----( )-------+--------|
|                          |        |
|                       [NC]       |
|                                   |
|                          RUN_FLAG |
|                          +--------+
```
*(Read: parallel branch START and RUN_FLAG, in series with NC STOP, output RUN_FLAG.)*

**Rung 2 — Conveyor Control:**
Conveyor runs whenever the system is ON AND no bottle is currently at the filling station.

$$ MOTOR = (RUN\_FLAG) \cdot \overline{SENSOR} $$

**Rung 3 — Fill Sequence Latch:**
Once a bottle is detected, latch the fill request. Reset after timer completes.

$$ FILL\_REQ = (SENSOR \cdot RUN\_FLAG + FILL\_REQ) \cdot \overline{T1.DN} $$

**Rung 4 — Valve and Timer:**

$$ VALVE = FILL\_REQ $$
$$ T1(IN, PT = k) = FILL\_REQ $$

When T1 reaches preset, T1.DN = 1 → resets FILL_REQ → closes valve → conveyor restarts → next bottle arrives → cycle repeats.

**Counter Rung — Production Logging:**
```
|   T1.DN     +   CTU C0, PV=100  |
|---[ ]--------+---( CU )---------|
```
Each fill completion increments C0; reaching PV (e.g., 100 bottles) can trigger a STOP or alarm.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Complete BFS Logic

The complete Boolean model of the Bottle Filling System is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RUN\_FLAG(t) &= \Big(START(t) \lor RUN\_FLAG(t-\Delta t)\Big) \land \lnot STOP(t) \\[4pt]
MOTOR(t) &= RUN\_FLAG(t) \land \lnot SENSOR(t) \\[4pt]
FILL\_REQ(t) &= \Big(SENSOR(t) \land RUN\_FLAG(t) \lor FILL\_REQ(t-\Delta t)\Big) \land \lnot T1.DN(t) \\[4pt]
VALVE(t) &= FILL\_REQ(t) \\[4pt]
T1(PT) &= k \quad (\text{preset fill duration in PLC time-base units}) \\[4pt]
C0_{count} &= C0_{count}(t-\Delta t) + T1.DN(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $t$ = current PLC scan instant
- $\Delta t$ = one PLC scan period (typically 10 ms)
- $k$ = operator-tuned preset in **0.1 s units** (e.g., k = 30 → 3 seconds)

---

### 3.4 Sequential Motor Switching — Star-Delta Timer Logic Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify state variables:**
- **M_MAIN** = Main contactor (line supply).
- **M_STAR** = Star contactor (shorts winding ends together).
- **M_DELTA** = Delta contactor (connects windings in Δ).
- **T_SD** = Star-to-Delta transition timer.

**Step 2 — Derive interlock equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M\_STAR &= (START + M\_STAR) \cdot \overline{T\_SD.DN} \cdot \overline{M\_DELTA} \\[4pt]
M\_DELTA &= T\_SD.DN \cdot \overline{M\_STAR} \cdot M\_MAIN \\[4pt]
M\_MAIN &= (START + M\_MAIN) \cdot \overline{STOP} \cdot (M\_STAR \lor M\_DELTA)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Transition time:**
The transition from Star to Delta must occur *after* the motor has reached ~80% of rated speed:

$$ T_{sd} = \frac{J \cdot \omega_r}{T_{accel} - T_{load}} $$

where:
- **J** = rotor + load inertia (kg·m²)
- **ω<sub>r</sub>** = rated angular speed (rad/s)
- **T<sub>accel</sub>** = motor acceleration torque (N·m)
- **T<sub>load</sub>** = load torque (N·m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners usually skip the inertia formula and ask for a **practical range** (3 – 10 s) — both should be memorizable.

---

### 3.5 Sequential ON/OFF of Three Motors — Numerical Example

**Problem:** Three conveyor motors M1, M2, M3 must start in sequence with Δt = 5 s gap. They must stop in reverse order with the same gap.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{ON,M1} &= 0 \text{ s} \\
T_{ON,M2} &= 0 + \Delta t = 5 \text{ s} \\
T_{ON,M3} &= 0 + 2\Delta t = 10 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Stop signal arrives at $t = 60$ s. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{OFF,M3} &= 60 \text{ s} \\
T_{OFF,M2} &= 60 + \Delta t = 65 \text{ s} \\
T_{OFF,M1} &= 60 + 2\Delta t = 70 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Ladder logic would use **TON timers** T1, T2, T3 (each PT = 5 s) cascading:

```
T1(IN := START)  →  M1 ON
T2(IN := T1.DN)  →  M2 ON
T3(IN := T2.DN)  →  M3 ON
```

```
T1'(IN := STOP)  →  M3 OFF
T2'(IN := T1'.DN)→  M2 OFF
T3'(IN := T2'.DN)→  M1 OFF
```

This **cascaded timer pattern** is the canonical KTU 14-mark solution.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bottle Filling System — Sequence Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start Pushbutton PB_START]) --> B{RUN_FLAG Latched}
    B -->|YES| C[M1 Conveyor ON]
    B -->|NO| Z([Idle / Wait])
    C --> D{PS_BOTTLE Detected}
    D -->|NO| C
    D -->|YES| E[M1 Conveyor OFF - Latch Bottle]
    E --> F[Open Solenoid Valve SV]
    F --> G[Start Timer T1 - Fill Duration]
    G --> H{T1 Done}
    H -->|NO| F
    H -->|YES| I[Close Solenoid Valve SV]
    I --> J[Increment Counter C1]
    J --> K{Stop Requested}
    K -->|NO| C
    K -->|YES| L[Reset System]
    L --> Z
```

**Node explanations:**
- **A**: Operator initiation.
- **B/C**: Latching and conveyor activation.
- **D**: Sensor decision branch.
- **E–I**: Filling state machine.
- **J**: Production logging.
- **K/L/Z**: Loop termination logic.

---

### 4.2 Sequential Motor Switching — Star-Delta Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([PB_START]) --> M0[Main Contactor M_MAIN]
    M0 --> Y1{Star Contactor M_STAR}
    Y1 -->|Active for T_sd| TMR[Transition Timer T_sd - 5 s]
    TMR --> Y2{Delta Contactor M_DELTA}
    Y2 -->|Active continuously| M_RUN([Motor runs at full torque])

    Y1 -.Interlock NC.- Y2
    Y2 -.Interlock NC.- Y1
    STP([PB_STOP]) -.Resets.- M0
    OVR([Overload Relay OL]) -.Trips.- M0

    classDef inputStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef coilStyle fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef timerStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
    class S,STP,OVR inputStyle
    class M0,Y1,Y2,M_RUN coilStyle
    class TMR timerStyle
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid arrows = energization flow.
- Dashed arrows = **interlock** (NC contacts preventing simultaneous activation of M_STAR and M_DELTA).

---

### 4.3 Sequential ON/OFF of Three Motors — Timing Diagram (Mermaid Gantt)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Sequential ON/OFF of Motors M1 M2 M3 (dt = 5 s)
    dateFormat ss
    axisFormat %S s

    section M1 Motor
    M1 Run        :a1, 00, 70s

    section M2 Motor
    M2 Run        :a2, 05s, 65s

    section M3 Motor
    M3 Run        :a3, 10s, 60s

    section Start Signal
    START         :crit, milestone, 00, 0s

    section Stop Signal
    STOP          :crit, milestone, 60, 0s
```

**Interpretation:** M1 starts at t = 0 s, M2 at t = 5 s, M3 at t = 10 s. STOP at t = 60 s causes M3 → M2 → M1 to de-energize in **LIFO order**, each 5 s after the previous — staggering the stop to reduce **regenerative voltage transients**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[Q.A1] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PBMRT604]`**

> **Explain the role of a proximity sensor in a PLC-based Bottle Filling System. Why is it preferred over a mechanical limit switch in this application?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A proximity sensor in a BFS detects the *arrival of an empty bottle* at the filling station and sends a discrete **24 V DC** signal to the PLC input module. It is preferred over a mechanical limit switch because:

1. **No physical contact** → no wear and tear, longer service life (typically > 100 million operations).
2. **Higher switching speed** → supports production rates up to **200 bottles/min**.
3. **Hygiene compliance** → critical in pharma/food industries where metallic contact parts are contamination risks.
4. **Immune to dust, water, and vibration** → reliable in wash-down environments.

The PLC uses this input to stop the conveyor (de-energize motor M1) and trigger the fill timer T1. *(1 mark for role, 2 marks for advantages.)*

---

**[Q.A2] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PBMRT604]`**

> **List any three applications of sequential motor switching using PLCs. State one advantage in each case.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Application | Advantage |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Water-treatment pumping stations | Prevents simultaneous starting inrush → avoids grid dip |
| 2 | HVAC chiller staging | Matches cooling demand → improves COP |
| 3 | Mining conveyor lines | Allows sequential material transfer → avoids pile-up |
| 4 | Star-Delta starters | Reduces starting current to **1/3 of DOL** → protects motor windings |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **[Question A — 14 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, PBMRT604]`**

> **Design a PLC-based Bottle Filling System with the following specifications:**
> - A conveyor belt carries empty bottles.
> - A proximity sensor detects each bottle at the filling station.
> - A solenoid valve fills the bottle for a preset time (T1 = 4 seconds).
> - A counter records the total bottles filled.
> - The system must STOP after 50 bottles and trigger an indicator lamp.
>
> **(a)** Draw the I/O allocation table and process flow chart. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Develop the complete ladder logic diagram with appropriate interlocks. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — I/O Allocation Table:**

| Address | Symbol | Device | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| I0.0 | PB_START | Start Pushbutton (NO) | Input |
| I0.1 | PB_STOP | Stop Pushbutton (NC) | Input |
| I0.2 | PS | Proximity Sensor | Input |
| Q0.0 | K1 | Conveyor Contactor | Output |
| Q0.1 | Y1 | Solenoid Valve | Output |
| Q0.2 | H1 | Indicator Lamp (50 bottles) | Output |
| T37 | T_FILL | Fill Timer (PT = 40) | Timer |
| C0 | C_BOTTLES | Bottle Counter (PV = 50) | Counter |

**Process Flow Chart (text representation for answer sheet):**

1. **START** pressed → Conveyor (K1) ON.
2. Wait until **PS = 1** → K1 OFF, bottle held.
3. Energize **Y1 (solenoid)** and start **T_FILL**.
4. When **T_FILL.DN** → Y1 OFF.
5. **Increment C0**. If C0.CV = 50 → **H1 ON**, conveyor held OFF.
6. Else → restart conveyor → repeat from Step 2.

**Part (b) — Ladder Logic:**

```
RUNG 1 — SYSTEM LATCH
|----[PB_START]----+-------( RUN_FLAG )-------+
|                  |                          |
|                  +----[RUN_FLAG]------------+
|                                             |
|----[PB_STOP_NC]------------------------------+
       (in series with parallel block above)


RUNG 2 — CONVEYOR
|----[RUN_FLAG]----[/PS]----------( K1 )------|


RUNG 3 — FILL LATCH
|----[PS]----[RUN_FLAG]---+----( FILL_REQ )--+
|                         |                  |
|                         +--[FILL_REQ]------+
|                                            |
|----[T37_DN]---------------------------------+


RUNG 4 — VALVE & TIMER
|----[FILL_REQ]-------------------( Y1 )-----|
|----[FILL_REQ]----[TON T37, PT=40]----------|


RUNG 5 — COUNTER
|----[T37_DN]--------------------( CTU C0 )--|


RUNG 6 — DONE LAMP
|----[C0_DONE]-------------------( H1 )------|
```

**Marking key:** (i) I/O table — 3 marks, (ii) Flow chart — 4 marks, (iii) Ladder logic — 6 marks, (iv) Interlocks — 1 mark.

---

#### **[Question B — 14 Marks — Alternative Choice] `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, PBMRT604]`**

> **Explain the PLC-based control of a Star-Delta starter for a 3-phase induction motor. With a neat ladder diagram, describe how the PLC achieves:**
> **(a)** Star connection for the first 5 seconds with proper interlocking between contactors. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Delta connection after the transition, including overload protection. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Star Phase Logic:**

In the star phase, the three motor winding "tails" (U2, V2, W2) are shorted together. The PLC must:

1. Energize **Main Contactor (K_MAIN)** + **Star Contactor (K_STAR)**.
2. Start timer **T_SD** with preset = 50 (5 s).
3. Hold K_STAR until T_SD completes.

**Interlocks (critical for marks):**
- **Electrical interlock**: K_STAR's NC contact (I_K_STAR) is placed in series with K_DELTA branch, and vice versa.
- **Mechanical interlock**: Hardware cross-bar between contactors (must be mentioned for full marks).

**Ladder Rungs (Star phase):**

```
RUNG 1: MAIN CONTACTOR LATCH
|----[START]----+-------( K_MAIN )----+
|               |                     |
|               +--[K_MAIN]-----------+
|                                     |
|----[STOP_NC]----[OL_NC]--------------+

RUNG 2: STAR CONTACTOR (with interlock)
|----[K_MAIN]----[START]---+----( K_STAR )--+
|                          |                |
|                          +--[K_STAR]------+
|                                           |
|----[T_SD_DN]----[/K_DELTA]-----------------+

RUNG 3: TRANSITION TIMER
|----[K_STAR]----[TON T_SD, PT=50]----------|

RUNG 4: DELTA CONTACTOR (interlocked)
|----[T_SD_DN]----[K_MAIN]----[/K_STAR]----( K_DELTA )--|
```

**Part (b) — Delta Phase Logic & Overload:**

After **T_SD.DN = 1**:
- K_STAR is de-energized (its NC contact closes in Rung 4).
- K_DELTA is energized, connecting U2→W1, V2→U1, W2→V1.
- Motor runs at full line voltage and full torque.

**Overload Protection:**
- **OL (Overload Relay)** NC contact → placed in series with PB_STOP and K_MAIN coil.
- If motor current exceeds **1.2 × FLC** for a preset duration (e.g., 10 s), OL trips → breaks K_MAIN → entire starter de-energizes.

**Complete Ladder (Delta phase):**

```
RUNG 5: OVERLOAD-RESET LATCH
|----[K_MAIN]----[START]----+---( K_MAIN )--+
|                           |               |
|                           +-(feedback)----+
|                                           |
|----[STOP_NC]----[OL_NC]-------------------+

RUNG 6: K_STAR OFF VIA TIMER
|----[K_STAR]----[T_SD_DN]------( RST K_STAR )|

RUNG 7: K_DELTA ON
|----[T_SD_DN]----[K_MAIN]----[/K_STAR]----( K_DELTA )--|
```

**Marking key:** (i) Star logic — 3 marks, (ii) Interlocks — 2 marks, (iii) Timer — 1 mark, (iv) Delta logic — 2 marks, (v) Overload — 2 marks, (vi) Ladder neatness — 1 mark, (vii) Explanation — 1 mark.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting interlock NC contacts** → -2 marks (most common error).
> 2. **Using NC stop pushbutton but showing it as NO** in the ladder → -1 mark.
> 3. **Not mentioning both electrical AND mechanical interlocks** in Star-Delta → -1 mark.
> 4. **Omitting the overload (OL) contact** in the main line → -1 mark.
> 5. **Timer preset units wrong** — KTU expects **0.1 s units** (so 5 s = PT 50) → -1 mark.
> 6. **Latches without NC STOP in series** → unsafe logic → -2 marks.
> 7. **No I/O table** in BFS questions → -3 marks (mandatory step).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: BFS & Sequential Motor Switching**

- [ ] A **Bottle Filling System** is a sequential automation process using **sensors, conveyors, solenoid valves, timers, and counters**.
- [ ] The standard **BFS sequence** = Idle → Detect → Stop → Fill (T1) → Close → Count → Repeat.
- [ ] **Fill duration** $T_1 = V_{bottle} / \dot{Q}_{valve}$.
- [ ] **Production rate** $R = 60 / T_{cycle}$ in bottles/min.
- [ ] **Photoelectric sensors** are preferred over mechanical limit switches for bottle detection.
- [ ] **Sequential Motor Switching** has three canonical forms: **F/R control**, **Star-Delta**, and **staggered ON/OFF**.
- [ ] In Star-Delta, current drops to **~ 1/3** of DOL inrush during start; transition time = **3 – 10 s**.
- [ ] **Interlocks** must be **both electrical (NC contacts in ladder) and mechanical (hardware cross-bar)**.
- [ ] **Overload relay (OL)** NC contact must be in series with the main contactor coil for safety.
- [ ] **Cascaded TON timers** with equal presets implement sequential motor ON/OFF with constant Δt.
- [ ] Ladder logic latches use the pattern: `(START + Coil) · NC(STOP) → Coil`.
- [ ] Timer preset units in standard PLCs: **PT 50 = 5.0 seconds** (0.1 s resolution).
- [ ] **Counter (CTU)** increments on rising edge of its input; its **DONE bit** is set when CV ≥ PV.
- [ ] Always draw the **I/O allocation table FIRST** in every KTU solution — marks are awarded even if the ladder has minor errors.
- [ ] **Stop pushbutton** is conventionally wired as **NC** (fail-safe).
- [ ] The PLC **scan cycle** evaluates rungs top-to-bottom — order of rungs matters for cascade logic.
- [ ] **Safety rule**: never trust a single sensor; use redundant **level + timer** in BFS.
- [ ] KTU 2024 frequently tests **why** a design choice was made, not just **how** — state trade-offs explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computer Control - Need of Computer in a Control System - Functional Block Diagram of a Computer Control System - Data Loggers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT604 - PLC & DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Computer Control - Need of Computer in a Control System - Functional Block Diagram of a Computer Control System - Data Loggers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Computer Control System

A **Computer Control System (CCS)** is an integrated hardware-software architecture in which a digital computer (microprocessor, microcontroller, PC, or industrial workstation) acts as the central controller that acquires process variables through sensors and Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs), executes a control algorithm stored in memory, and generates corrective control signals through Digital-to-Analog Converters (DACs) to drive final control elements (valves, motors, heaters, contactors) governing an industrial process.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim style):** "A computer control system replaces the conventional analog/controller hardware with a programmable digital computer that performs measurement, comparison, computation, and correction functions in a feedback loop."

The three principal topologies of computer control in industry are:

| Topology | Full Form | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **DDC** | Direct Digital Control | Single-loop / multi-loop dedicated control |
| **SCC** | Supervisory Computer Control | Set-point optimization, monitoring |
| **DSC / DCS** | Distributed Control System | Plant-wide networked control |

### 1.2 Need for a Computer in a Control System — The Driving Forces

Conventional analog controllers (PID implemented with op-amps, pneumatic controllers) suffer from drift, lack of flexibility, single-loop operation, and inability to communicate. A digital computer overcomes these limitations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Seven Cardinal Reasons (HIGH-YIELD for KTU 2-mark / 3-mark questions):**
>
> 1. **Flexibility / Programmability** — Control law (P, PI, PID, lead-lag, fuzzy, adaptive) can be changed by software modification, no rewiring required.
> 2. **Accuracy & Repeatability** — No component drift, no calibration drift due to temperature or aging; resolution set by ADC bits.
> 3. **Multi-loop Capability** — One processor can execute control algorithms for hundreds of loops sequentially within a single scan cycle.
> 4. **Complex Algorithms** — Realisation of non-linear, adaptive, self-tuning, model-predictive strategies impossible in analog hardware.
> 5. **Data Logging & Historization** — Continuous time-stamped recording of process variables for quality, audit, and regulatory compliance.
> 6. **Communication & Networking** — Native support for fieldbuses (Modbus, Profibus, EtherNet/IP) and enterprise integration (ERP/MES).
> 7. **Alarm Management, Diagnostics & Operator HMI** — Centralized annunciation, trending, and fault detection far beyond the scope of analog gauges.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Cruise Control Story

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Box — "The Smart Car vs. the Mechanical Governor"**
>
> Imagine two vehicles maintaining a speed of **80 km/h** on a hill:
>
> - **Mechanical governor (analog controller):** A spinning flyball linked to a throttle lever. When the car climbs, the flyball droops, pulls the lever, opens the throttle. Crude, slow, and fixed to ONE job — speed control. If you want cruise control converted to a fuel-economy mode, you must rebuild the linkage.
>
> - **Computer control (the smart ECU):** A digital ECU reads wheel speed **1,000 times per second**, compares it to the set-point, computes a PID correction in software, and pulses the throttle body. The SAME hardware, by changing the firmware, can switch to fuel-economy mode, traction control, or adaptive cruise — just by downloading a new program.
>
> The computer is **flexible, accurate, fast, multi-tasking, and communicative** — the four pillars of modern industrial automation.

### 1.4 Data Logger — Formal Definition

A **Data Logger** is a stand-alone, dedicated electronic instrument that measures, records, and time-stamps one or more physical or electrical parameters (temperature, pressure, flow, voltage, current, humidity, strain) over an extended period. Unlike a SCADA system, a data logger operates **without a continuous human-supervised HMI** and is optimized for **autonomous, unattended, long-duration acquisition** with local storage (SD card, USB, internal flash) or remote transfer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification (KTU exam favourite):**
> - **Stand-alone / portable data loggers** — battery-powered, single or multi-channel, internal memory.
> - **PC-based data loggers** — plug-in DAQ cards (PCI/USB/Ethernet) driven by software (LabVIEW, MATLAB DAQ Toolbox, proprietary).
> - **Web-based / IoT data loggers** — cloud-push telemetry using MQTT/HTTP over Wi-Fi or cellular.
> - **Paper-chart recorders (legacy)** — analog strip-chart mechanism, largely obsolete.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Computer Control Loop

The functional flow of a single computer-controlled feedback loop proceeds through a deterministic sequence executed every **scan period T_s**:

1. **Process (Plant)** — The physical system whose variable `y(t)` is to be regulated (temperature of a furnace, level of a tank, speed of a motor). Acts as the unknown to be controlled.
2. **Sensor / Transducer** — Converts the physical variable into a low-level electrical signal: thermocouple → mV, RTD → Ω, LVDT → V, flow meter → 4-20 mA current loop.
3. **Signal Conditioning (SC)** — Amplification, filtering (anti-alias low-pass), cold-junction compensation, linearization, and isolation. Brings the signal into the input range of the ADC (typically **0–10 V** or **4–20 mA**).
4. **Multiplexer (MUX)** — Time-shares one ADC among many channels, switched by a digital address.
5. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** — Holds the instantaneous analog value constant during the finite ADC conversion time to prevent aperture error.
6. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — Quantizes the held analog voltage into an `n`-bit binary word `N(k)`.
7. **Digital Computer (CPU + Memory + I/O)** — Executes the control algorithm (discrete PID, state-space, fuzzy) producing the control word `u(k)`.
8. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** — Converts the digital control word into a corresponding analog voltage or current.
9. **Actuator / Final Control Element** — Control valve, variable-frequency drive (VFD), contactor, servo valve — physically modulates the energy input to the process.
10. **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** — Operator screen, alarms, trend plots, set-point entry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why each block exists (the "Why" column):**
> - *Multiplexer* — Saves cost; one expensive ADC serves N channels.
> - *Sample-and-Hold* — Avoids signal change during conversion; a 12-bit ADC doing 100 kS/s has a 10 µs aperture — the signal must be frozen.
> - *Anti-alias filter* — Shannon–Nyquist theorem demands that **all frequency components above F_s/2 be attenuated by at least the ADC's SNR**, otherwise they fold back as spurious low-frequency aliases.
> - *CPU* — Implements flexible, reprogrammable, non-linear control law.

### 2.2 The Discrete PID Control Law (KTU favourite)

The continuous-time PID

$$u(t) = K_p\, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

when discretized at sampling period `T_s` with the **velocity (incremental) form** becomes

$$\Delta u(k) = K_p\bigl[e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr] + K_i\,T_s\, e(k) + \frac{K_d}{T_s}\bigl[e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)\bigr]$$

and the **positional form** is

$$u(k) = K_p\,e(k) + K_i\,T_s\sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + \frac{K_d}{T_s}\bigl[e(k)-e(k-1)\bigr]$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why the velocity form?** It eliminates integral wind-up naturally (the output is the *change*, not the absolute actuator position) and provides **bumpless transfer** in auto-to-manual switching — a common 14-mark question.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Units / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Rate** | $F_s \geq 2\,F_{max}$ | Hz; $F_s$ is sampling frequency, $F_{max}$ is highest signal frequency component |
| 2 | **ADC Quantization Step (LSB size)** | $q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ | V / step; $V_{FS}$ = full-scale voltage, $n$ = bits |
| 3 | **ADC Theoretical SNR** | $SNR = 6.02\,n + 1.76$ | dB |
| 4 | **Maximum Quantization Error** | $E_q = \pm \dfrac{q}{2} = \pm \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2(2^n - 1)}$ | V |
| 5 | **Sampling Period** | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{F_s}$ | seconds |
| 6 | **Number of Channels Scan Time** | $T_{scan} = N \cdot (T_{S/H} + T_{ADC} + T_{compute})$ | seconds |
| 7 | **Hold-circuit decay (droop)** | $V(t) = V_0\,e^{-t/\tau_h}$ | V; $\tau_h = R_h C_h$ |
| 8 | **Data Logger Memory Duration** | $t_{rec} = \dfrac{M_{bytes}}{N_{ch} \cdot f_s \cdot (n/8)}$ | hours / days |
| 9 | **Alias Frequency** | $f_{alias} = \vert f_{signal} - m\,F_s \vert$ | Hz; $m$ = nearest integer |
| 10 | **Closed-loop z-domain PID (positional)** | $U(z) = \left[K_p + K_i\,T_s\,\dfrac{1}{1-z^{-1}} + \dfrac{K_d}{T_s}(1 - z^{-1})\right]E(z)$ | — |
| 11 | **Anti-alias filter cutoff (Butterworth, –3 dB)** | $f_c \leq \dfrac{F_s}{2}$ | Hz |
| 12 | **Stability bound (Dead-Beat)** | Sampling chosen so that dominant plant time-constant $\tau \geq (3 \text{ to } 5)\,T_s$ | seconds |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use `|` for absolute value inside the above table — KTU note parser breaks.** Use `\vert` (already implemented).

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where These Systems Live

- **DDC** — HVAC building automation, boiler drum level, injection-molding temperature zones.
- **SCC + DCS** — Oil refinery catalytic cracker, power plant boiler-turbine coordinated control, cement kiln.
- **Data loggers** — Cold-chain pharmaceutical transport, weather stations, structural health monitoring of bridges, power-quality audit in substations, fermentation bioreactor validation in biotech.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Quantization Error Bound

A unipolar `n`-bit ADC divides a full-scale range $V_{FS}$ into $2^n - 1$ equal intervals of size $q$. Any input analog voltage $V_a$ lying between two adjacent codes $N_k$ and $N_{k+1}$ is rounded (or truncated) to the nearest code. The worst-case deviation is half an LSB.

**Step 1 — Define the LSB (Least Significant Bit) voltage step.**

$$q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$$

**Step 2 — Express the digital code for an input voltage $V_a$.** The conversion relation is

$$N = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{V_a}{q}\right) = \mathrm{round}\!\left[\frac{V_a\,(2^n - 1)}{V_{FS}}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Define the quantization error as the difference between actual and reconstructed value.**

$$E_q = V_a - \hat{V}_a, \quad \text{where } \hat{V}_a = N \cdot q$$

**Step 4 — Bound the error by the half-LSB interval.** Because the rounded code lies within $\pm q/2$ of the true value:

$$\begin{aligned}
-\frac{q}{2} &\leq E_q \leq +\frac{q}{2} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad \vert E_q \vert_{max} &= \frac{q}{2} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2\,(2^n - 1)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute SNR assuming a uniformly distributed random signal across the full scale.**

The signal power is $V_{FS}^{2}/8$ (RMS of a full-scale ramp), the noise power is $q^{2}/12$. Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
SNR &= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{FS}^{2}/8}{q^{2}/12}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\,2^{2n}\right) = 10\log_{10}(1.5) + 20\,n\log_{10}(2) \\[4pt]
&= 1.76 + 6.02\,n \;\; \text{dB}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Each extra ADC bit buys ~6 dB of dynamic range** — the foundational justification for choosing 16-bit or 24-bit ADCs in precision data loggers.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Data-Logger Recording Duration

Given a stand-alone data logger with non-volatile memory $M$ (bytes), number of active channels $N_{ch}$, sampling rate per channel $f_s$ (Hz), and ADC resolution $n$ (bits), we want the maximum continuous recording time.

**Step 1 — Bits per sample = n.** Convert to bytes per sample:

$$b = \frac{n}{8} \;\; \text{bytes/sample}$$

**Step 2 — Bytes generated per second across all channels:**

$$R = N_{ch} \cdot f_s \cdot b \;\; \text{bytes/second}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum recording duration before memory exhaustion:**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{rec} &= \frac{M}{R} = \frac{M}{N_{ch} \cdot f_s \cdot (n/8)} \;\; \text{seconds} \\[4pt]
t_{rec,\,h} &= \frac{8\,M}{3600 \cdot N_{ch} \cdot f_s \cdot n} \;\; \text{hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Worked example (KTU-style):** A data logger has **16 MB** of flash, records **4 channels** at **100 Hz** with **12-bit** ADCs. Find the recording time in hours.

$$t_{rec,\,h} = \frac{8 \times 16 \times 1024^2}{3600 \times 4 \times 100 \times 12} = \frac{1.342 \times 10^{8}}{1.728 \times 10^{7}} \approx 7.77 \;\; \text{hours}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Alias Frequency

Suppose a process signal of frequency $f_{sig}$ = 137 Hz is sampled at $F_s$ = 100 Hz (below Nyquist — bad design). Determine the apparent frequency that will appear in the digital record.

**Step 1 — Compute the alias using the standard reflection formula:**

$$f_{alias} = \vert f_{sig} - m\,F_s \vert$$

where $m$ is the integer that brings $f_{alias}$ into the principal range $[0, F_s/2]$.

**Step 2 — Try $m = 1$:** $f_{alias} = \vert 137 - 100 \vert = 37$ Hz. Since $37 \leq F_s/2 = 50$, the answer is **37 Hz**.

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** A genuine 137 Hz vibration is recorded as a fictitious 37 Hz component — a classic alias artefact. This is why anti-alias low-pass filters with cutoff $f_c < F_s/2$ are mandatory.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Discrete Transfer Function

For a first-order plant $G_p(s) = K/(\tau s + 1)$ under zero-order-hold (ZOH) discretization at $T_s$, the exact z-domain pulse transfer function is

$$G_p(z) = \mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{1-e^{-sT_s}}{s}\cdot \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}\right\} = \frac{K\,(1 - e^{-T_s/\tau})}{z - e^{-T_s/\tau}}$$

With a digital controller $D(z) = U(z)/E(z)$, the closed-loop transfer function is

$$\frac{Y(z)}{R(z)} = \frac{D(z)\,G_p(z)}{1 + D(z)\,G_p(z)}$$

The characteristic equation $1 + D(z)\,G_p(z) = 0$ governs stability; for a digital PID as listed in the formula sheet:

$$1 + \left[K_p + K_i\,T_s\,\frac{z}{z-1} + \frac{K_d}{T_s}(z-1)\right]\frac{K(1-e^{-T_s/\tau})}{z-e^{-T_s/\tau}} = 0$$

Applying Jury's test or the bilinear-transform equivalent $z = (1+w)/(1-w)$ converts this to a polynomial in $w$ whose Routh array gives the discrete-time stability bounds on $K_p, K_i, K_d$ — the standard KTU 14-marker.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Computer Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RP[Reference Set Point r k] --> SUM((+))
    PROC[Process Plant Gp s] --> SENS[Sensor Transducer]
    SENS --> SC[Signal Conditioning Amplifier Filter]
    SC --> MUX[Analog Multiplexer]
    MUX --> SH[Sample and Hold S H]
    SH --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter n bit]
    ADC --> CPU[Digital Computer CPU Memory Algorithm]
    CPU --> DAC[Digital to Analog Converter]
    CPU --> HMI[Operator HMI Display Alarms]
    CPU --> LOG[Data Logger Storage]
    DAC --> AMP[Power Amplifier]
    AMP --> ACT[Actuator Final Control Element]
    ACT --> PROC
    SENS --> SUM
    LOG --> NET[Network Cloud SCADA / ERP]
```

**Reading guide:** The forward (control) path runs top-left → top-right → bottom-right → bottom-left. The feedback (measurement) path runs bottom-left → left → up to the summing junction. The three dashed-side branches from the CPU are the **HMI, Data Logger, and Network** — they do not affect the closed-loop dynamics but are mandatory KTU components.

---

### 4.2 Internal Block Diagram of a Typical Multi-Channel Data Logger

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Input Signal Conditioning Stage]
        I1[CH1 Sensor] --> BUF1[Buffer Amplifier]
        I2[CH2 Sensor] --> BUF2[Buffer Amplifier]
        I3[CHn Sensor] --> BUFN[Buffer Amplifier]
        BUF1 --> AAF1[Anti Alias LP Filter]
        BUF2 --> AAF2[Anti Alias LP Filter]
        BUFN --> AAFN[Anti Alias LP Filter]
    end
    AAF1 --> MUX2[Multiplexer]
    AAF2 --> MUX2
    AAFN --> MUX2
    MUX2 --> SH2[Sample and Hold]
    SH2 --> ADC2[16 / 24 bit ADC]
    ADC2 --> MIC[Microcontroller / DSP]
    MIC --> RTC[Real Time Clock RTC]
    MIC --> MEM[Non Volatile Memory Flash / SD Card]
    MIC --> LCD[Local LCD / Keypad]
    MIC --> USB[USB / Ethernet / WiFi]
    MIC --> BAT[Battery + Power Management]
```

**Reading guide:** The shaded `INPUT` group performs the analog front-end (AFE) function. The microcontroller sequences the multiplexer, fetches ADC conversions, time-stamps each sample with the RTC, persists to memory, and optionally transmits via USB/Ethernet/WiFi. Battery + power management guarantees autonomous operation during mains failure.

---

### 4.3 Closed-Loop Control Signal Flow in s-domain and z-domain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RK[R k Reference] --> SSUM((+))
    EK[E k Error] --> DZ[D z Digital Controller]
    DZ --> UK[U k Control Word]
    UK --> ZOH[Zero Order Hold G_h0 s]
    ZOH --> GPS[Gp s Plant]
    GPS --> YK[Y k Process Output]
    YK --> SHZ[Sampler Period T s]
    SHZ --> ADCM[Quantizer n bit]
    ADCM --> YKD[Y k Digital Output]
    YKD --> SSUM
    YK --> DLOG[Data Logger Archive]
```

**Reading guide:** This dual representation emphasizes that the **plant** is continuous-time $G_p(s)$ driven through a **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)**, while the **controller** is digital $D(z)$. The sampler block and the quantizer block together form the digital encoder of the feedback signal.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List any six reasons why a digital computer is preferred over analog controllers in modern industrial control systems.*

**Model Answer (6 × ½ = 3 marks):**

1. **Programmable flexibility** — the control law can be changed in software without rewiring.
2. **Higher accuracy and absence of drift** — no aging components, no thermal drift.
3. **Multi-loop capability** — one processor handles hundreds of loops sequentially.
4. **Implementation of complex algorithms** — adaptive, fuzzy, model-predictive, self-tuning.
5. **Built-in data logging and historization** — time-stamped records for audit and analysis.
6. **Native networking** — easy integration with SCADA, ERP, and MES layers.
7. *Optional extra:* Centralized alarm management and operator HMI.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between a stand-alone data logger and a PC-based data acquisition system.*

**Model Answer (tabular, 3 marks):**

| Parameter | Stand-alone Data Logger | PC-based DAQ System |
|---|---|---|
| Operator presence | Not required | Continuous supervision needed |
| Power | Battery / mains, autonomous | Depends on PC mains |
| Storage | Internal flash / SD | PC hard disk / SSD |
| Channels | Limited (8–32 typical) | Expandable via cards / chassis |
| Software | Firmware-only, fixed UI | LabVIEW / MATLAB / custom |
| Cost (per channel) | Higher for few channels | Lower for high channel count |
| Application | Unattended field / cold-chain | Laboratory / R&D |

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the operation of a **computer-based feedback control system**. Identify the role of the multiplexer, sample-and-hold circuit, ADC, DAC, and the final control element. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 12-bit ADC has a unipolar input range of **0 to 10 V**. Compute:
   (i) the LSB size, (ii) the maximum quantization error, (iii) the theoretical SNR in dB, and (iv) the smallest voltage change that can be resolved at mid-scale. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block diagram and operation — 7 marks**

Draw the diagram from Section 4.1. The process under control `y(t)` is sensed, conditioned, multiplexed, held, digitized, fed to the CPU, processed by the control algorithm, converted back to analog, amplified, and applied to the actuator which closes the loop on the process. *Roles:*

- **Multiplexer (1 mark):** Time-shares one ADC across N input channels, controlled by a digital address; reduces hardware cost.
- **Sample-and-Hold (1 mark):** Freezes the analog signal during the finite conversion time of the ADC; eliminates aperture error.
- **ADC (1 mark):** Converts the held analog voltage into an n-bit binary word, introducing quantization.
- **DAC (1 mark):** Converts the digital control word into an analog voltage or current, driving the actuator.
- **Final control element (1 mark):** Modulates process energy (valve position, motor speed, heater power).
- **Remaining 2 marks:** Continuous explanation of the forward path, feedback path, summing junction, and error signal computation.

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks**

Given: $n = 12$ bits, $V_{FS} = 10$ V.

**(i) LSB size (2 marks):**
$$q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{10}{4095} \approx 2.442 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 2.442 \text{ mV}$$

**(ii) Maximum quantization error (1 mark):**
$$\vert E_q \vert_{max} = \frac{q}{2} = \frac{2.442\,\text{mV}}{2} = 1.221 \text{ mV}$$

**(iii) Theoretical SNR (2 marks):**
$$SNR = 6.02\,n + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0 \text{ dB}$$

**(iv) Smallest resolvable voltage change at mid-scale (2 marks):**

At any point in the range, the smallest change corresponds to **1 LSB** = **2.442 mV** (independent of operating point). Therefore the answer is **2.442 mV**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b)(iv):** Many students incorrectly write "1 LSB" but compute the answer using the half-scale voltage `V/2 = 5 V` thinking it affects the result. **At any operating point, the LSB step size is constant** for a uniform unipolar ADC. Do not lose this 1 mark.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Equivalent]**

**(a)** Explain the **need for a computer in a control system**. Discuss the architecture of a **data logger** with a neat block diagram and list its typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stand-alone data logger has a **32 GB SD card**, records **8 channels** at a **sampling rate of 200 Hz** per channel with **16-bit resolution**. The data is stored in a binary format with **2 bytes per sample**.
   (i) Calculate the maximum continuous recording time in hours.
   (ii) If the engineer wants a 30-day continuous record, what is the minimum SD card capacity required?
   (iii) Mention two design choices to extend recording time without changing the card. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Need for computer & data logger architecture — 7 marks**

*Need (any 5 points, 1 mark each from the seven in Section 1.2).*

*Data logger block diagram — 3 marks:* Draw the diagram from Section 4.2 and label **sensor → buffer → anti-alias filter → multiplexer → S/H → ADC → microcontroller → RTC → memory → communication interface → power management**. (2 marks for completeness, 1 mark for explanation of flow.)

*Applications (1 mark, any four):* pharmaceutical cold-chain, weather station, structural health monitoring of bridges, fermentation process validation, power-quality audit, food storage, IoT remote telemetry.

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks**

**(i) Maximum continuous recording time (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= N_{ch} \cdot f_s \cdot b = 8 \times 200 \times 2 = 3200 \text{ bytes/second} \\[4pt]
M &= 32 \text{ GB} = 32 \times 1024^3 = 3.436 \times 10^{10} \text{ bytes} \\[4pt]
t_{rec} &= \frac{M}{R} = \frac{3.436 \times 10^{10}}{3200} = 1.0737 \times 10^{7} \text{ s} \\[4pt]
t_{rec,\,h} &= \frac{1.0737 \times 10^{7}}{3600} \approx 2982.5 \text{ hours} \approx 124.3 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Minimum SD card for 30 days = 720 hours (2 marks):**

$$M_{min} = R \times t_{sec} = 3200 \times (720 \times 3600) = 8.294 \times 10^{9} \text{ bytes} \approx 7.73 \text{ GB}$$

Therefore, a **8 GB** SD card is the minimum commercial size sufficient (or use the next-standard size of 8 GB / 16 GB).

**(iii) Two design choices to extend recording time (2 marks, 1 each):**

1. **Reduce sampling rate** $f_s$ — log at 50 Hz instead of 200 Hz; recording time quadruples.
2. **Apply data compression** — store delta-coded or LZ-compressed binary; saves 50–80 % space.
   *(Alternative accepted answers: reduce ADC resolution from 16-bit to 12-bit; use event-driven / threshold-triggered logging; use on-the-fly FFT and store only spectral features.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b)(i):** Students frequently use `1 GB = 10^9 bytes` (decimal) when the KTU convention in engineering papers expects `1 GB = 1024^3 = 1.0737 × 10^9 bytes` (binary). State the convention explicitly. Off-by-one errors here cost 1 full mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Computer Control System** replaces analog hardware with a programmable digital computer — the seven key reasons are **flexibility, accuracy, multi-loop, complex algorithms, logging, networking, HMI**.
- ✅ The canonical loop is **Process → Sensor → Signal Conditioner → MUX → S/H → ADC → Computer → DAC → Actuator → Process** (with HMI, Data Logger, and Network as parallel branches).
- ✅ **ADC quantization step** $q = V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$; **maximum error** $\pm q/2$; **SNR** $\approx 6.02\,n + 1.76$ dB.
- ✅ **Nyquist–Shannon criterion** $F_s \geq 2 F_{max}$; an **anti-alias low-pass filter** with $f_c \leq F_s/2$ is mandatory.
- ✅ **Discrete PID** in velocity form $\Delta u(k)$ is preferred over positional form $u(k)$ to avoid integral wind-up and to enable bumpless transfer.
- ✅ **Data loggers** are classified as **stand-alone**, **PC-based**, or **Web/IoT-based**; key subsystems are analog front-end, multiplexer, ADC, RTC, non-volatile memory, communication interface, and battery.
- ✅ **Recording duration** $t_{rec} = M / (N_{ch} \cdot f_s \cdot b)$ — use `1024` for binary multiples in KTU computations.
- ✅ **Alias frequency** $f_{alias} = \vert f_{sig} - m F_s \vert$ — undersampling folds high frequencies into the baseband as false low-frequency components.
- ✅ **ZOH discretization** of $K/(\tau s + 1)$ yields $G_p(z) = K(1 - e^{-T_s/\tau})/(z - e^{-T_s/\tau})$.
- ✅ In KTU 14-mark answers, **always draw the block diagram first** (carries 3–4 marks) before starting the numerical/textual part — a missing diagram is the single largest mark-loss event reported in board exam analytics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supervisory Computer Control - Direct Digital Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT604 - PLC & DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Supervisory Computer Control - Direct Digital Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direct Digital Control (DDC):**
> A control strategy in which a single digital computer (or a microprocessor-based controller) directly receives multiple process variable measurements, performs control computations (typically PID-like algorithms) on each loop, and transmits the computed corrective signals to the corresponding final control elements — entirely replacing the function of multiple dedicated analog controllers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supervisory Computer Control (SCC):**
> A hierarchical control architecture in which a higher-level digital computer (the *supervisor*) does not perform loop control directly, but instead sets the *setpoints*, optimizes tuning parameters, performs sequence management, alarm logging, and trend recording for several DDC stations working underneath it.

**Hierarchical Control Layers (Industry Standard ISA-95 / Purdue Model):**

| Level | Layer | Function | Typical Hardware |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **L4** | Enterprise / Management | Production planning, ERP | Mainframe server |
| **L3** | Supervisory (SCC) | Setpoint optimization, HMI, trending | SCADA / Industrial PC |
| **L2** | Direct Digital Control | Closed-loop PID execution | DCS Controller / PLC |
| **L1** | Field / Device | Sensing & actuation | Transmitters, valves |
| **L0** | Process | Physical phenomena | Plant equipment |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Building Manager"

Imagine a **large hospital with thousands of rooms**:

- **DDC Controller** = The *ward supervisor nurse* who stands by each patient, continuously checking their temperature, blood pressure, and pulse (analog measurements) every few minutes, comparing them with the prescribed healthy range (setpoint), and immediately adjusting the IV drip rate (final control element) if anything deviates. The nurse *physically* performs the correction.
- **SCC Computer** = The *Chief Medical Officer (CMO)* sitting in a remote office. The CMO does **not** touch the IV directly. Instead, the CMO looks at reports from all ward supervisors, decides that "Room 304's prescribed temperature should be raised from 37.0 °C to 37.2 °C" (changes setpoint), re-balances staffing (loop re-tuning), and prepares the daily hospital report (historical trending).
- **Field Devices** = The actual thermometers, blood pressure cuffs, and IV pumps in each room.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Key Distinction:** DDC *closes the loop* itself (output drives the actuator). SCC *closes the loop* by changing what the lower-level controllers should *aim for*. SCC **supervises**; DDC **executes**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Definitions

- **Sampling Period ($T$):** Time interval between two successive measurements of a process variable. KTU standard notation is in **seconds**.
- **Scan Time:** Total time for the DDC computer to cycle through all loops once.
- **Number of Loops ($N$):** The DDC computer can handle hundreds of loops sequentially. A rule of thumb is $T_{scan} \le 0.1 \cdot T_{dominant}$ of the slowest loop.
- **Word Length:** Typically **12-bit, 14-bit, or 16-bit** resolution for industrial ADCs, giving quantization steps of $1/4096 \approx 0.024\%$ to $1/65536 \approx 0.0015\%$ of full scale.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of a DDC Station

A DDC system performs **four** sequential sub-tasks in every scan cycle:

1. **Acquire** process variable through multiplexer → signal conditioner → ADC.
2. **Compute** the error $e(k) = SP(k) - PV(k)$ and apply a discrete control law.
3. **Output** the new control value through DAC → hold circuit → actuator.
4. **Display / log** the value, check alarms, and update HMI.

Because step 1 and step 3 are *multiplexed* across many loops, a single CPU effectively replaces $N$ analog controllers — this is the primary economic driver behind DDC.

### 2.2 The Discrete PID Control Law

The continuous-time PID controller familiar from classical control is

$$u(t) = K_p \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i} \int e(t)\, dt + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

A digital computer **cannot integrate or differentiate continuously** — it can only operate on *sampled* values. We must therefore approximate these operations using numerical techniques that KTU examiners expect you to know by name.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Standard Discretization Approximations (KTU-Favorite Question):**
> 1. **Rectangular (Forward Euler) Integration** for the integral term.
> 2. **Backward Difference** for the derivative term.
> 3. **Trapezoidal Integration** (a more accurate alternative for the integral).

#### 2.2.1 Position Algorithm (Absolute Output)

The output $u(k)$ is sent **directly** as the actuator position. This is the most common form used in continuous actuators (e.g., a control valve stem position).

$$u(k) = K_p \left[ e(k) + \frac{T}{T_i} \sum_{i=0}^{k} e(i) + \frac{T_d}{T} \bigl[ e(k) - e(k-1) \bigr] \right]$$

#### 2.2.2 Velocity Algorithm (Incremental Output)

The computer outputs only the **change** $\Delta u(k)$. The actuator itself is incremental (e.g., a stepper motor, or an integral actuator with memory). This form is **inherently bumpless** and is widely used in PLCs.

$$\Delta u(k) = K_p \left\{ \bigl[ e(k) - e(k-1) \bigr] + \frac{T}{T_i}\, e(k) + \frac{T_d}{T} \bigl[ e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2) \bigr] \right\}$$

### 2.3 Why Two Algorithms? — The "Why & How" Logic

- **Why Position?** When the final control element is a *position-type* actuator (pneumatic valve, motorized damper), the valve must be told "go to 45% open." The position algorithm gives the absolute setpoint.
- **Why Velocity?** When the actuator is *incremental* (e.g., a stepper-driven valve) or when the CPU may crash and lose its memory, only an *increment* is needed. The valve position is held by an *integral actuator* (it stays where last placed) — loss of computer output does **not** cause a sudden jump. This is the famous **"Bumpless Transfer"** property.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Rule Used in Industry:** Most DCS controllers (Honeywell, Yokogawa, ABB) internally compute the **velocity** form to avoid reset wind-up, then add the increment to the previous output — but they *expose* the **position** value to the operator on the HMI. Students should know both.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Symbol Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Engineering Unit | Boundary / Limitation |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| $u(k)$ | Controller output at $k$-th sample | % of full scale | $u_{min} \le u(k) \le u_{max}$ |
| $e(k)$ | Error $= SP(k) - PV(k)$ | Engineering units (EU) | sign convention matters |
| $K_p$ | Proportional gain | dimensionless | — |
| $T_i$ | Integral (reset) time | **seconds** | $T_i \to 0$ ⇒ pure I action |
| $T_d$ | Derivative (rate) time | **seconds** | $T_d \to 0$ ⇒ no D action |
| $T$ | Sampling period | **seconds** | $T \le T_{process}/10$ |
| $N$ | Number of loops handled by DDC | integer | $T_{scan} = N \cdot T_{loop}$ |
| $\Delta u(k)$ | Incremental output (velocity form) | % per sample | bounded by actuator stroke |
| $K_c$ | Discrete PI gain $= K_p T / T_i$ | % / EU | — |
| $K_d$ | Discrete D gain $= K_p T_d / T$ | %·s / EU | — |
| $e_{ss}$ | Steady-state error | EU | $e_{ss} \to 0$ in PI / PID |
| $b$ | Setpoint weighting (2-DOF) | $0 \le b \le 1$ | reduces kick on SP change |
| $q$ | Quantization step $= \frac{FS}{2^n}$ | EU | $n$ = ADC bit count |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Trap:** $K_p$ is **dimensionless**; $K_i$ and $K_d$ in their **discrete** forms inherit the sampling period $T$. Examiners frequently test whether you confuse $K_i = K_p / T_i$ (continuous) with $K_i = K_p T / T_i$ (discrete).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants** — DDC controls boiler drum level, superheat temperature, and feedwater flow in parallel.
- **HVAC Skyscrapers** — A single DDC station handles **thousands** of VAV box damper positions.
- **Oil Refineries** — DCS-based DDC controls distillation column reflux, reboiler steam, and tray temperatures in real time.
- **Pharmaceutical Batch Reactors** — DDC executes the temperature profile for each batch recipe.
- **Robotics & Automation (RT Branch)** — Multi-axis motion controllers are essentially **DDC** running velocity-algorithm PID on every joint simultaneously.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Position Algorithm from Analog PID

We start with the classical **continuous-time PID equation** in parallel form:

$$u(t) = K_p\, e(t) + \frac{K_p}{T_i} \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_p\, T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 1 — Replace the integral with rectangular summation (Forward Euler rule):**

$$\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau \;\approx\; T \sum_{i=0}^{k} e(i)$$

**Step 2 — Replace the derivative with a first-order backward difference:**

$$\frac{de(t)}{dt} \;\approx\; \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute both approximations back into the continuous PID equation:**

$$u(k) = K_p\, e(k) + \frac{K_p}{T_i} \cdot T \sum_{i=0}^{k} e(i) + K_p\, T_d \cdot \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T}$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $K_p$ for the standard textbook presentation:**

$$u(k) = K_p \left[ e(k) + \frac{T}{T_i} \sum_{i=0}^{k} e(i) + \frac{T_d}{T} \bigl\{ e(k) - e(k-1) \bigr\} \right]$$

This is the **Position Algorithm**. Each bracketed term has a clear engineering interpretation:

- The first bracket is the **P-action** (immediate correction).
- The second bracket is the **I-action** (eliminate offset / steady-state error).
