- The third bracket is the **D-action** (anticipate future error based on rate of change).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Velocity (Incremental) Algorithm

**Step 1 — Write the position algorithm evaluated one sample earlier:**

$$u(k-1) = K_p \left[ e(k-1) + \frac{T}{T_i} \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} e(i) + \frac{T_d}{T} \bigl\{ e(k-1) - e(k-2) \bigr\} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Subtract $u(k-1)$ from $u(k)$.** Because $u(k) - u(k-1) = \Delta u(k)$, the long summation telescopes automatically:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{k} e(i) - \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} e(i) = e(k)$$

This **self-cancellation** of the integral accumulator is precisely why the velocity form is *immune* to reset wind-up — there is no growing sum to overflow.

**Step 3 — Perform the algebra term by term:**

$$\Delta u(k) = K_p \bigl[ e(k) - e(k-1) \bigr] + K_p \frac{T}{T_i} e(k) + K_p \frac{T_d}{T} \bigl[ e(k) - e(k-1) - e(k-1) + e(k-2) \bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Combine the derivative bracket:**

$$e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)$$

**Step 5 — Final compact expression:**

$$\Delta u(k) = K_p \left\{ \bigl[ e(k) - e(k-1) \bigr] + \frac{T}{T_i}\, e(k) + \frac{T_d}{T} \bigl[ e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2) \bigr] \right\}$$

This is the **Velocity Algorithm**. The new actuator command is computed as $u_{new}(k) = u_{old}(k-1) + \Delta u(k)$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem (Board-Style):** A DDC controller uses the velocity algorithm with $K_p = 2.0$, $T_i = 10$ s, $T_d = 2$ s, and sampling period $T = 1$ s. The error sequence (in engineering units) is $e(0) = 0$, $e(1) = 4$, $e(2) = 6$, $e(3) = 5$. If the initial output $u(0) = 20\%$, compute $u(1)$, $u(2)$, and $u(3)$.

**Pre-compute the constant coefficients:**

$$\frac{K_p T}{T_i} = \frac{2.0 \times 1}{10} = 0.2$$

$$\frac{K_p T_d}{T} = \frac{2.0 \times 2}{1} = 4.0$$

**Step A — Compute $\Delta u(1)$ using $e(0), e(1)$ (and assume $e(-1) = 0$):**

$$\Delta u(1) = 2.0(4 - 0) + 0.2(4) + 4.0(4 - 0 + 0)$$

$$\Delta u(1) = 8.0 + 0.8 + 16.0 = 24.8\%$$

$$u(1) = u(0) + \Delta u(1) = 20 + 24.8 = 44.8\%$$

**Step B — Compute $\Delta u(2)$ using $e(0), e(1), e(2)$:**

$$\Delta u(2) = 2.0(6 - 4) + 0.2(6) + 4.0(6 - 8 + 4)$$

$$\Delta u(2) = 4.0 + 1.2 + 4.0(2) = 4.0 + 1.2 + 8.0 = 13.2\%$$

$$u(2) = 44.8 + 13.2 = 58.0\%$$

**Step C — Compute $\Delta u(3)$ using $e(1), e(2), e(3)$:**

$$\Delta u(3) = 2.0(5 - 6) + 0.2(5) + 4.0(5 - 12 + 6)$$

$$\Delta u(3) = -2.0 + 1.0 + 4.0(-1) = -2.0 + 1.0 - 4.0 = -5.0\%$$

$$u(3) = 58.0 + (-5.0) = 53.0\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Insight:** The negative sign in step C shows the controller is **reducing its output** as the error begins shrinking — classic derivative action. The integral term (0.2) is small but ensures the final steady-state error becomes zero.

### 3.4 SCC Setpoint Adjustment Logic (Analytical View)

In SCC mode, the supervisory computer does not compute $u(k)$ directly. Instead, every $T_{sup}$ seconds it executes:

$$SP_{new}(k) = SP_{old}(k) + K_{opt} \left[ Y_{target} - Y_{measured} \right]$$

where $Y_{target}$ is the production target and $Y_{measured}$ is the recent product quality. The DDC station then chases this moving setpoint with its own PID. The effective closed-loop bandwidth is therefore *slower* for the SCC layer, but it optimizes overall plant economics (energy, throughput, yield).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Single-Loop DDC Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    spA[Setpoint SP k] --> errCalcA[Error Calculator]
    pvA[Process Variable PV k] --> adcA[ADC Converter]
    adcA --> errCalcA
    errCalcA --> pidA[Digital PID Algorithm]
    pidA --> limA[Output Limiter]
    limA --> dacA[DAC plus Zero Order Hold]
    dacA --> actA[Final Control Element Valve]
    actA --> procA[Industrial Process]
    procA --> transA[Transmitter Sensor]
    transA --> pvA
    procA -.feedback.-> transA
```

### 4.2 Multi-Loop DDC Station — One CPU Replaces N Analog Controllers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cpu1[Central Digital Computer CPU] --> mux1[Analog Output Multiplexer]
    mux1 --> loop1A[Loop 1 Actuator]
    mux1 --> loop2A[Loop 2 Actuator]
    mux1 --> loop3A[Loop 3 Actuator]
    mux1 --> loop4A[Loop N Actuator]
    sens1[Loop 1 Sensor] --> mxi1[Analog Input Multiplexer]
    sens2[Loop 2 Sensor] --> mxi1
    sens3[Loop 3 Sensor] --> mxi1
    sens4[Loop N Sensor] --> mxi1
    mxi1 --> cpu1
    cpu1 --> hmi1[Operator HMI Display]
    cpu1 --> alm1[Alarm Management Module]
    cpu1 --> trd1[Historical Trend Database]
```

### 4.3 SCC + DDC Hierarchical Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    mgmt1[Level 4 Enterprise Planning] --> scc1[Level 3 Supervisory Computer SCC]
    scc1 --> setpt1[Setpoint and Tuning Download]
    scc1 --> mon1[Performance Monitoring and Logs]
    setpt1 --> ddc1A[Level 2 DDC Station 1]
    setpt1 --> ddc2A[Level 2 DDC Station 2]
    setpt1 --> ddc3A[Level 2 DDC Station N]
    ddc1A --> fld1[Level 1 Field Actuators and Sensors]
    ddc2A --> fld1
    ddc3A --> fld1
    fld1 --> prc1[Level 0 Industrial Process]
    mon1 --> scc1
    prc1 -.process variables.-> fld1
```

### 4.4 DDC Software Algorithm — Step-by-Step Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initA[Initialize All Loop Parameters] --> waitA[Wait for Scan Trigger Timer]
    waitA --> selA[Select Loop i for This Cycle]
    selA --> readA[Read PV through ADC for Loop i]
    readA --> errA[Compute Error e k equals SP k minus PV k]
    errA --> algoA[Apply Position or Velocity PID Algorithm]
    algoA --> limA2[Apply Output Clamp Min and Max Limits]
    limA2 --> outA[Send Output to DAC for Loop i]
    outA --> checkA{All Loops Complete?}
    checkA -- No --> selA
    checkA -- Yes --> alarmA[Update Alarms and Trend Logs]
    alarmA --> waitA
```

### 4.5 DDC Software Structure — Modular View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    schedA[Real Time Scheduler Kernel] --> acquireA[Data Acquisition Module]
    schedA --> controlA[Control Algorithm Module]
    schedA --> displayA[Display and HMI Module]
    schedA --> alarmA2[Alarm Handling Module]
    schedA --> commA[Communication Module to SCC]
    acquireA --> controlA
    controlA --> commA
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Direct Digital Control (DDC). Compare its salient features with conventional analog control.*

**Model Answer:**
DDC is a control technique in which a digital computer replaces multiple conventional analog controllers. It directly receives process variable signals through ADC, computes the control law (typically PID) for each loop, and outputs corrective signals through DAC. Salient comparisons:

| Feature | Analog Control | DDC |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Hardware per loop | One analog controller card | One shared CPU |
| Tuning changes | Manual potentiometer | Software (no wiring change) |
| Flexibility | Fixed hardware function | Easily reconfigured |
| Communication | Point-to-point wiring | Digital bus to SCC |
| Drift | Susceptible to component aging | No drift — purely numerical |
| Cost per loop | High | Low once CPU is shared |

**(3 marks — definition 1 m, any three comparisons 2 m)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between the **Position Algorithm** and **Velocity Algorithm** used in DDC. State one advantage of each.*

**Model Answer:**

- **Position Algorithm** computes the absolute actuator output $u(k)$ directly. It is used when the final control element is a *position-type* device (e.g., pneumatic control valve). **Advantage:** Single computation gives the actual stem position; easier for the operator to understand on the HMI.
- **Velocity Algorithm** computes the change in output $\Delta u(k)$ at each sampling instant. It is used with *incremental* actuators (e.g., stepper-driven valve). **Advantage:** Inherently bumpless transfer — if the computer fails, the actuator holds its last position because it is an integral device; also avoids reset wind-up because there is no growing integral accumulator.

**(3 marks — 1 m for each definition, 1 m advantage each)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write the position algorithm with **proportional band** instead of $K_p$, mix up $T_i$ and $T_d$ units, or substitute numerical values *before* the symbolic derivation. Always derive the symbolic form **first**, then plug numbers. **3 marks are reserved for the symbolic structure alone** in derivation problems.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3, Q.6 Modified]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of a DDC system with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each block.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Derive the **Position Algorithm** of a digital PID controller from the continuous-time PID equation. List any two practical limitations of the rectangular integration method.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DDC Architecture — 7 Marks**

A DDC system consists of the following functional blocks (refer to Section 4.1 diagram for visual):

1. **Process** — The physical plant whose variables (temperature, flow, level, pressure) must be controlled.
2. **Sensors / Transmitters** — Convert process variables to standard electrical signals (e.g., 4–20 mA).
3. **Multiplexer (MUX)** — Time-shares one ADC across many input channels — essential because a single CPU handles all loops.
4. **Signal Conditioner** — Amplifies, filters, and isolates the signal; provides anti-aliasing low-pass filter.
5. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — Quantizes the analog signal into binary words (typically **12 to 16 bits**).
6. **Digital Computer / CPU** — Executes the discrete PID algorithm for each loop sequentially.
7. **Output Latch / DAC with Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** — Holds the output constant between samples; reconstructs analog voltage/current.
8. **De-Multiplexer (DEMUX)** — Routes the computed output to the correct actuator.
9. **Final Control Element (Actuator + Valve)** — Manipulates the process (e.g., control valve on a flow line).
10. **HMI / Display** — Provides operator interface, trends, and alarm visualization.

*(Each block 0.5 mark, neat diagram 2 marks = 7 marks total.)*

**(b) Derivation of Position Algorithm — 7 Marks**

Continuous-time PID (parallel form):

$$u(t) = K_p\, e(t) + \frac{K_p}{T_i} \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_p\, T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Apply **Forward Euler rectangular integration** to the integral term:

$$\int_{0}^{kT} e(\tau)\, d\tau \;\approx\; T \sum_{i=0}^{k} e(i)$$

Apply **backward difference** to the derivative term:

$$\frac{de(t)}{dt} \;\approx\; \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T}$$

Substitute and factor $K_p$:

$$u(k) = K_p \left[ e(k) + \frac{T}{T_i} \sum_{i=0}^{k} e(i) + \frac{T_d}{T} \bigl\{ e(k) - e(k-1) \bigr\} \right]$$

This is the **Position Algorithm**.

**Two practical limitations of rectangular integration:**

1. **Accumulation error / drift:** When $T$ is large or the error is changing rapidly, the rectangular rule over- or under-estimates the true area. A **trapezoidal rule** is more accurate because it uses the average of the leading and trailing edges:

$$\int \approx T \cdot \frac{e(i-1) + e(i)}{2}$$

2. **Reset wind-up susceptibility:** The summation $\sum e(i)$ grows without bound if the actuator saturates and the error persists. Without anti-wind-up clamping, the integral term becomes huge and produces a large overshoot when the error finally reverses sign. The velocity algorithm naturally avoids this issue.

*(Symbolic derivation = 4 marks, limitations = 3 marks = 7 marks total.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 3, Q.7 Modified]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe a **Supervisory Computer Control (SCC)** system and explain how it interacts with DDC stations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Velocity Algorithm** from the position algorithm. A DDC controller uses velocity algorithm with $K_p = 1.5$, $T_i = 8$ s, $T_d = 1$ s, $T = 1$ s. Given the error sequence $e(0)=0$, $e(1)=2$, $e(2)=5$, $e(3)=3$ and initial output $u(0)=15\%$, calculate $u(1)$, $u(2)$, $u(3)$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Supervisory Computer Control — 7 Marks**

A Supervisory Computer Control (SCC) system sits **above** the DDC layer in the automation hierarchy. It does not close control loops directly; instead it supervises multiple DDC stations. (Refer to Section 4.3 diagram.)

**Functions of SCC:**

- **Setpoint generation** — Computes optimal setpoints for each DDC loop based on plant-wide production targets, energy cost, and product quality.
- **Loop tuning** — Downloads updated $K_p$, $T_i$, $T_d$ values to DDC stations when process characteristics change.
- **Alarm management** — Aggregates alarms from all DDC stations, prioritizes, and notifies operators.
- **Data logging and trending** — Maintains historical records for analysis and regulatory compliance.
- **Operator HMI** — Provides graphical mimics, trends, and reports.
- **Coordination and sequencing** — Coordinates start-up, shut-down, and batch transitions.

**Interaction with DDC:** Communication is typically over **serial bus (Modbus, Profibus)** or **Ethernet (Profinet, EtherNet/IP)**. The SCC polls each DDC station every $T_{sup}$ (e.g., 1–5 s) and downloads new setpoints, while the DDC station executes the inner PID loop every $T$ (typically 0.1–1 s). This dual-rate structure is called **hierarchical or cascade-of-rates control**.

*(Diagram = 2 marks, any 5 functions = 3 marks, interaction explanation = 2 marks = 7 marks total.)*

**(b) Derivation of Velocity Algorithm + Numerical — 7 Marks**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Start with the position algorithm at sample $k$ and $k-1$:

$$u(k) = K_p \left[ e(k) + \frac{T}{T_i} \sum_{i=0}^{k} e(i) + \frac{T_d}{T} \bigl\{ e(k) - e(k-1) \bigr\} \right]$$

$$u(k-1) = K_p \left[ e(k-1) + \frac{T}{T_i} \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} e(i) + \frac{T_d}{T} \bigl\{ e(k-1) - e(k-2) \bigr\} \right]$$

Subtracting and using the telescoping property $\sum_{i=0}^{k} e(i) - \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} e(i) = e(k)$:

$$\Delta u(k) = K_p \left\{ \bigl[ e(k) - e(k-1) \bigr] + \frac{T}{T_i}\, e(k) + \frac{T_d}{T} \bigl[ e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2) \bigr] \right\}$$

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**

Coefficients:

$$\frac{K_p T}{T_i} = \frac{1.5 \times 1}{8} = 0.1875, \qquad \frac{K_p T_d}{T} = \frac{1.5 \times 1}{1} = 1.5$$

Assume $e(-1) = 0$ (initial condition) and $e(-2) = 0$.

**Sample 1:**

$$\Delta u(1) = 1.5(2 - 0) + 0.1875(2) + 1.5(2 - 0 + 0)$$

$$\Delta u(1) = 3.0 + 0.375 + 3.0 = 6.375\%$$

$$u(1) = 15 + 6.375 = 21.375\%$$

**Sample 2:**

$$\Delta u(2) = 1.5(5 - 2) + 0.1875(5) + 1.5(5 - 4 + 0)$$

$$\Delta u(2) = 4.5 + 0.9375 + 1.5(1) = 4.5 + 0.9375 + 1.5 = 6.9375\%$$

$$u(2) = 21.375 + 6.9375 = 28.3125\%$$

**Sample 3:**

$$\Delta u(3) = 1.5(3 - 5) + 0.1875(3) + 1.5(3 - 10 + 2)$$

$$\Delta u(3) = -3.0 + 0.5625 + 1.5(-5) = -3.0 + 0.5625 - 7.5 = -9.9375\%$$

$$u(3) = 28.3125 - 9.9375 = 18.375\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often use $e(k-2) = 0$ for **every** sample. This is only true at $k=1$. For $k \ge 2$, you must use the actual previous error values. The first sample calculation is the most error-prone.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam:**

- ✅ DDC = one digital computer replacing **N** analog controllers by **time-sharing** the CPU.
- ✅ SCC = supervisory layer that sets setpoints, tunes loops, and logs data; it does **not** close the loop directly.
- ✅ Hierarchical levels: **L4 Enterprise → L3 SCC → L2 DDC → L1 Field → L0 Process**.
- ✅ Continuous PID uses **integration & differentiation**; discrete PID replaces them with **summation & finite difference**.
- ✅ **Position Algorithm** → output = absolute value of $u(k)$. Used with position-type actuators.
- ✅ **Velocity Algorithm** → output = $\Delta u(k) = u(k) - u(k-1)$. Used with incremental actuators; **bumpless**, **anti wind-up** by construction.
- ✅ The discrete integral coefficient is $K_p T / T_i$ and the discrete derivative coefficient is $K_p T_d / T$.
- ✅ Sampling theorem: choose $T \le T_{dominant} / 10$ to avoid aliasing and preserve closed-loop stability.
- ✅ Velocity form's derivative bracket simplifies to $e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)$ — memorize this pattern.
- ✅ Trapezoidal integration is more accurate than rectangular but requires storing one extra past error.
- ✅ In multi-loop DDC, the **scan time** $= N \times T_{per-loop}$ must be smaller than the fastest loop's response time.
- ✅ DDC advantages: **flexibility, no drift, easy re-tuning, central monitoring, lower per-loop cost.**
- ✅ DDC disadvantages: **single point of failure** (mitigated by redundant CPU), A/D and D/A quantization noise, requires skilled software engineers, computer must be ruggedized for industrial environment.
- ✅ For $k = 1$ in velocity algorithm, **always** assume $e(-1) = e(-2) = 0$ as initial condition.
- ✅ The integer index $i$ in the summation $\sum_{i=0}^{k} e(i)$ telescopes cleanly to $e(k)$ after subtraction — a board-favorite simplification.
- ✅ The output of the DDC algorithm is typically clamped to $0\% \le u(k) \le 100\%$ before being sent to the actuator.
- ✅ SCC + DDC forms the heart of every modern **DCS (Distributed Control System)** like Honeywell Experion, Yokogawa CENTUM, or ABB 800xA.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SCADA - Programming of Robot (Pick and Place)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT604 - PLC & DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SCADA - Programming of Robot (Pick and Place)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SCADA - Programming of Robot (Pick and Place)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** refers to a centralized industrial control architecture that uses computers, networked data communications, and graphical Human-Machine Interfaces (HMI) to remotely monitor, supervise, and control geographically distributed or automated processes.

In the context of **Robotic Pick and Place**, SCADA refers to the supervisory software layer that sits above the PLC and Robot Controller, providing:
1. **Visualization** of the robot's spatial position, status, and I/O.
2. **Command Dispatch** via soft-keys to trigger pre-programmed routines.
3. **Data Logging** of cycle times, fault counts, and production throughput.

A **Pick and Place Robot** is an automated electro-mechanical manipulator programmed to grasp an object from a defined source location (Pick Zone), transport it through a controlled trajectory, and release it at a defined destination (Place Zone).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The integration of **SCADA ↔ PLC ↔ Robot Controller** forms the **3-Tier Automation Hierarchy**. SCADA NEVER directly drives motors; it sends high-level commands to the PLC, which in turn pulses the Robot Controller.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Tower

Imagine an **Air Traffic Control Tower** controlling an aircraft's landing:

| Element | Real-World Analogy | Technical Counterpart |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Air Traffic Controller | Supervisory Operator | **SCADA HMI Software** |
| Pilot executing commands | Robot end-effector | **Robot Manipulator** |
| Radio dispatch | Communication protocol (Modbus/Profibus/Ethernet/IP) | **Industrial Bus** |
| Runway lights & radar | Limit switches, encoders, vision sensors | **Field I/O & Sensors** |
| Aircraft autopilot box | Sequencing logic | **PLC Ladder Program** |

The Tower (SCADA) does **not** physically land the plane. It **commands** the pilot (PLC/Robot) using a standardized vocabulary (Tag Database). Similarly, SCADA tags like `Robot_Home_Pos`, `Gripper_Status`, `Conveyor_Run` form a *digital dictionary* shared between the operator and the machine.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Industrial Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Key industrial parameters used in Pick and Place robotic cell design (must be memorized for KTU):
> - **Payload Capacity:** typically **1 kg – 500 kg** (industrial SCARA/articulated arms)
> - **Repeatability:** $\pm 0.02$ mm to $\pm 0.1$ mm for precision pick operations
> - **Standard Cycle Time:** **4 to 12 seconds** for a single pick-place cycle
> - **Communication Latency Tolerance:** $\leq \mathbf{100 \; ms}$ for real-time supervisory commands
> - **Safety Stop Category:** **Category 0 / Category 1** (per ISO 13850)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The 3-Tier Architecture for Pick and Place

The SCADA-Robot system is decomposed into three logical layers:

### **Tier 1 — Field Level (Sensors & Actuators)**
- **Inputs:** Proximity sensors (object present), photo-electric eyes, vacuum pressure switch, gripper position feedback, emergency stop (E-Stop) NC contact.
- **Outputs:** Solenoid valves for gripper, conveyor motor starters, indicator lamps, servo drive enable signals.

### **Tier 2 — Control Level (PLC + Robot Controller)**
- **PLC** handles the **sequencing logic** (Start, Home, Pick, Move, Place, Return).
- **Robot Controller** handles the **trajectory interpolation** (linear, joint, circular motion) and **kinematics**.
- Communication via **Digital I/O** (simple bit-level handshaking) or **Fieldbus** (EtherCAT, Profinet for richer data).

### **Tier 3 — Supervisory Level (SCADA / HMI)**
- Runs on an Industrial PC (IPC).
- Hosts tag database, alarms, trends, and recipes.
- Pushbuttons on the HMI write to PLC memory bits, which trigger ladder rungs.

## 2.2 The Pick and Place Sequence — Engineering Logic

A standard Pick and Place cycle is engineered as a **Finite State Machine (FSM)** with **6 distinct states**:

| State # | State Name | Action Triggered | Exit Condition |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| S0 | **IDLE / HOME** | Robot waits at home coordinates | `START_PB` pressed + Part_Detected = 1 |
| S1 | **PICK_APPROACH** | Move to Z-coordinate *above* pick point | Position reached (servo confirms) |
| S2 | **PICK_GRASP** | Lower Z, close gripper / activate vacuum | Gripper_Closed_Sensor = 1 |
| S3 | **RETRACT** | Move to Z-coordinate *above* pick point | Retract position reached |
| S4 | **PLACE_MOVE** | Joint/linear move to place coordinates | Position reached |
| S5 | **PLACE_RELEASE** | Lower Z, open gripper / vent vacuum | Gripper_Open_Sensor = 1 |
| S6 | **RETURN_HOME** | Move back to home position | Home_Sensor = 1 → S0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a State Machine?** It guarantees that the robot cannot skip a safety step. For example, the system will NOT close the gripper unless the robot has confirmed it is at the pick coordinates. This is the **core principle** examiners test.

## 2.3 SCADA ↔ PLC ↔ Robot I/O Mapping (Address Table)

The SCADA HMI exchanges data with the PLC using a defined **Tag Database**. Below is a typical mapping for a Pick and Place cell:

| SCADA Tag (HMI Display) | PLC Address | Type | Robot / Sensor Reference | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `Robot_At_Home` | %I0.0 | BOOL | Robot Home Limit Switch | Confirmation of home position |
| `Part_Present_Pick` | %I0.1 | BOOL | Proximity Sensor at Pick | Object detection |
| `Gripper_Closed` | %I0.2 | BOOL | Pressure Switch / Reed | Confirms successful grasp |
| `Estop_OK` | %I0.3 | BOOL | E-Stop NC Loop | Safety interlock |
| `Start_PB` | %I0.4 | BOOL | HMI Start Button | Cycle initiation |
| `Conveyor_Run_Cmd` | %Q0.0 | BOOL | VFD Enable to Conveyor | Material handling |
| `Gripper_Close_Cmd` | %Q0.1 | BOOL | Solenoid Valve Y1 | End-effector actuation |
| `Robot_Start_Cycle` | %Q0.2 | BOOL | Robot Controller DI | Triggers robot program |
| `Cycle_Done` | %I0.5 | BOOL | Robot Controller DO | Signals cycle completion |
| `Cycle_Counter` | %MW10 | INT | PLC Counter | Production count |
| `Cycle_Time_s` | %MW12 | REAL | PLC Timer | Performance metric |
| `Fault_Code` | %MW14 | INT | Robot Alarm Word | Diagnostics |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Mathematical Foundations)

Although SCADA programming is largely logic-based, certain kinematic and timing relationships are examinable.

### **A. Cycle Time Calculation**

The total cycle time $T_{cycle}$ of a Pick and Place operation is the sum of the constituent operation times:

$$
T_{cycle} = t_{approach} + t_{grasp} + t_{retract} + t_{traverse} + t_{place} + t_{return}
$$

For constant acceleration motion over a distance $d$, the traverse time is:

$$
t = \frac{v_{max}}{a} + \frac{d - \frac{v_{max}^{2}}{a}}{v_{max}}
$$

where:
- $v_{max}$ = maximum joint velocity (m/s)
- $a$ = acceleration (m/s²)
- $d$ = distance traversed (m)

### **B. Throughput**

$$
\text{Throughput (parts/hour)} = \eta \cdot \frac{3600}{T_{cycle}}
$$

where $\eta$ = **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)**, typically $0.85$ to $0.95$.

### **C. Communication Latency Bound**

$$
t_{latency} \leq 0.1 \cdot T_{cycle}
$$

If latency exceeds 10% of cycle time, the supervisory loop becomes a bottleneck.

### **D. Payload-Acceleration Bound (Robot Dynamics)**

$$
\tau = I \cdot \alpha + m \cdot g \cdot r \cdot \cos(\theta)
$$

- $\tau$ = joint torque required (Nm)
- $I$ = moment of inertia of the link (kg·m²)
- $m$ = payload mass (kg)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration **9.81 m/s²**
- $r$ = moment arm (m)
- $\theta$ = joint angle (rad)

> [!NOTE]
> This torque equation is critical when the SCADA system displays **payload warnings** — the controller must enforce a software torque limit to prevent motor stalling.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Assembly:** SCADA-controlled robots place engine components on moving conveyors (line tracking).
- **Pharmaceutical Packaging:** Pick vials from trays and place into cartoners with 100% traceability (SCADA logs each vial's barcode).
- **Electronics SMT:** Pick SMD components from reels and place on PCBs with micron-level precision.
- **Food & Beverage:** Delta robots pick cookies from a moving belt and place in packaging trays.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Logic Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Safe Pick and Place Sequencing Logic

We will now derive the **logical conditions** for each state transition. This is the type of analytical derivation KTU examiners expect for 14-mark questions.

### **Given Assumptions:**
- `Start_PB` = momentary pushbutton, must be latched.
- `Estop_OK` = TRUE means circuit healthy.
- `Robot_Home` = TRUE means robot is at home position.
- `Part_Present` = TRUE means part is detected at pick station.
- `Gripper_Closed_OK` = TRUE means object is firmly held.
- `Cycle_Done` = TRUE means robot has completed the place sequence.

### **Derivation of S0 → S1 Transition (Start Condition)**

The robot must NOT leave home unless **ALL** of the following are simultaneously satisfied:
1. Operator presses START.
2. No emergency stop is active.
3. Robot is confirmed at HOME.
4. A part is present at the pick location.

In Boolean algebra, "simultaneously satisfied" is the **AND** operation ($\land$).

$$
\text{Start\_Condition} = \text{Start\_PB} \land \text{Estop\_OK} \land \text{Robot\_Home} \land \text{Part\_Present}
$$

### **Derivation of S1 → S2 Transition (Approach Complete)**

The robot moves to the pre-pick position (above the part). The transition occurs when the robot controller pulses back a "Position_Reached" signal. We add a **time delay** to allow mechanical settling:

$$
\text{Approach\_Done} = \text{Robot\_Position\_Reached} \land (t_{settle} \geq 0.5 \text{ s})
$$

### **Derivation of S2 → S3 Transition (Grasp Confirmed)**

The gripper is commanded to close. We must wait for the **pressure confirmation** to ensure the part is actually grasped (not dropped or missed):

$$
\text{Grasp\_Done} = \text{Gripper\_Closed\_Cmd} \land \text{Pressure\_Switch\_OK} \land (t_{grasp} \geq 1.0 \text{ s})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 1-second dwell time** is non-trivial — it compensates for **pneumatic actuation lag**, which is typically **200 to 500 ms** for a standard 5/2 solenoid valve at 6 bar.

### **Derivation of S3 → S4 Transition (Retract Complete)**

The robot retracts to a safe Z-height to clear the part fixture before lateral motion:

$$
\text{Retract\_Done} = \text{Retract\_Position\_Reached} \land \text{Gripper\_Closed\_OK}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note that `Gripper_Closed_OK` is **re-checked** here. This is a **safety re-validation** — if the part was dropped during lift-off, the system should fault and NOT proceed to the place station.

### **Derivation of S4 → S5 Transition (Place Position Reached)**

$$
\text{Place\_Approach\_Done} = \text{Place\_Position\_Reached} \land \text{Robot\_Safe\_to\_Descend}
$$

### **Derivation of S5 → S6 Transition (Release Complete)**

The gripper opens and we wait for the open confirmation:

$$
\text{Release\_Done} = \text{Gripper\_Open\_Cmd} \land \text{Gripper\_Open\_Sensor} \land (t_{release} \geq 0.5 \text{ s})
$$

### **Derivation of S6 → S0 Transition (Home Returned)**

The robot must physically return home for the cycle to be repeatable:

$$
\text{Cycle\_Complete} = \text{Robot\_Home} \land \neg\text{Fault\_Active}
$$

The cycle counter is incremented:

$$
N_{cycles} \leftarrow N_{cycles} + 1
$$

## 3.2 SCADA Tag Update Equations

The SCADA HMI continuously refreshes its display values. The standard SCADA **polling equation** for any tag value $V_{tag}$ at polling time $t_k$ is:

$$
V_{tag}(t_k) = V_{PLC}(t_k - \Delta t_{comms})
$$

where $\Delta t_{comms}$ is the communication round-trip delay between SCADA and PLC. For **Modbus TCP** over Ethernet, this is typically **5–20 ms**.

For derived metrics like **Average Cycle Time**:

$$
\overline{T}_{cycle}(n) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{cycle}(i)
$$

After $n$ cycles, the running average stabilizes as:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \overline{T}_{cycle}(n) \approx T_{steady-state}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Failure Mode Mapping

For KTU, you must know **what happens when each signal fails**. This matrix is a high-yield study tool:

| Failure Signal | Detection Method | SCADA Alarm | PLC Safety Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `Gripper_Closed` = 0 after grasp | Pressure switch timeout (>1.5s) | "GRIPPER FAULT — PART MISSED" | Abort cycle, raise gripper, sound buzzer |
| `Robot_Home` = 0 on power-up | Initial scan | "ROBOT NOT HOMED" | Disable START button on HMI |
| `Part_Present` = 0 when expected | Sensor blocked timeout | "PICK STATION EMPTY" | Halt conveyor, flash yellow lamp |
| `Estop_OK` = 0 | Hardwired NC loop opens | "EMERGENCY STOP ACTIVATED" | Drop all outputs, Category 0 stop |
| `Cycle_Done` = 0 after place | Timeout > $T_{cycle,max}$ | "ROBOT STUCK — CHECK OBSTRUCTION" | Fault latch, requires manual reset |
| Communication loss SCADA↔PLC | Watchdog timer (3 missed polls) | "SCADA LINK LOST" | Process continues locally; HMI shows red banner |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete information flow from the operator's finger on the HMI button down to the robot's gripper fingertips.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator HMI Screen SCADA] -->|Tag Write START_PB| B[SCADA Tag Database]
    B -->|Modbus TCP Ethernet| C[PLC CPU]
    C -->|DI Scan Inputs| D1[Home Limit Switch]
    C -->|DI Scan Inputs| D2[Part Present Sensor]
    C -->|DI Scan Inputs| D3[Gripper Pressure Switch]
    C -->|DO Pulse| E[Robot Controller DI Start Cycle]
    C -->|DO Energize| F[Solenoid Valve Gripper]
    C -->|DO Pulse| G[Conveyor VFD Enable]
    E -->|Trajectory Execution| H[Robot Manipulator 6 Axis]
    H -->|Servo Feedback| I[Encoder Position Data]
    I -->|DO Robot Position Reached| C
    H -->|Physical Action| J[Gripper End Effector]
    J -->|Pneumatic Hose| F
    C -->|Tag Update Cycle Time| B
    B -->|Display Refresh 1Hz| A
    K[EStop Hardware NC Loop] -->|Hardwired to| C
    L[Safety Light Curtain] -->|Hardwired to| C
```

## 4.2 Pick and Place Sequence Flowchart (Mermaid)

This flowchart maps the **6-state FSM** with all safety interlocks explicitly shown.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S0_Home
    S0_Home --> S1_Approach: Start_PB AND Estop_OK AND Part_Present
    S1_Approach --> S2_Grasp: Position_Reached AND t greater or equal 0.5s
    S2_Grasp --> S3_Retract: Gripper_Closed AND Pressure_OK
    S2_Grasp --> S2_Grasp: Pressure timeout FAULT
    S3_Retract --> S4_Place_Move: Retract_Z_Reached AND Gripper_OK rechecked
    S4_Place_Move --> S5_Release: Place_Position_Reached
    S5_Release --> S6_Return: Gripper_Open_Sensor AND t greater or equal 0.5s
    S6_Return --> S0_Home: Robot_Home AND Counter increment
    S0_Home --> [*]: EStop or Power Off
```

## 4.3 SCADA HMI Screen Layout (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Screen1 [Operator HMI Main Screen]
        P1[Header Production Counter 1247 parts]
        P2[Center Graphic Animated Robot with X Y Z coordinates]
        P3[Bottom Status Bar Home: GREEN Cycle: RUNNING]
        P4[Soft Keys START STOP RESET HOME ACK FAULT]
        P5[Alarm Banner EMPTY top yellow]
        P6[Trend Chart Cycle Time last 100 cycles]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "design the SCADA screen", always include **3 mandatory elements**:
> 1. Live mimic / animation of the robot
> 2. Soft-keys for operator commands
> 3. Alarm summary banner

## 4.4 Communication Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SCADA[SCADA Server Industrial PC] -->|Ethernet RJ45| SW[Managed Ethernet Switch]
    SW -->|Cat6| PLC[PLC CPU]
    SW -->|Cat6| ROB[Robot Controller]
    SW -->|Cat6| VFD[Conveyor VFD]
    PLC -->|24V Digital I/O Cable| ROB
    PLC -->|Sensor Cable| S1[Proximity Sensor Pick]
    PLC -->|Sensor Cable| S2[Vacuum Switch]
    PLC -->|Solenoid Cable| SOL[Gripper Valve]
```
