# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define SCADA. List any four functions of a SCADA system in a robotic pick and place cell.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** is a computer-based industrial automation system that centrally monitors, controls, and acquires real-time data from geographically distributed field devices through a graphical HMI.

**Four functions in a robotic pick and place cell:**
1. **Real-time Visualization:** Displays the robot's current coordinates, gripper status, and cycle count on an animated HMI mimic.
2. **Command Dispatch:** Sends operator-initiated commands (Start, Stop, Home, Acknowledge Fault) to the PLC via tag writes.
3. **Alarm Management:** Detects abnormal conditions (gripper fault, E-stop, sensor failure) and displays prioritized alarm banners.
4. **Data Logging & Trending:** Records historical data such as cycle time, throughput, and fault frequency for OEE analysis.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is a Pick and Place robot? State its two main coordinate frames used in programming.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Pick and Place robot** is an automated electro-mechanical manipulator that grasps an object from a defined source location, transports it along a programmed trajectory, and releases it at a target location, typically for repetitive industrial tasks.

**Two main coordinate frames:**
1. **Base (World) Coordinate Frame:** A fixed reference frame attached to the robot's base or the workstation foundation. All pick and place points are programmed in this frame.
2. **Tool (End-Effector) Coordinate Frame:** A moving reference frame attached to the robot's gripper/tool tip. It is used for approach vectors and orientation control during pick/place.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **3-tier architecture** of a SCADA-based robotic pick and place system. List the components at each tier.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Explain the **step-by-step pick and place sequence** as a state machine, including the safety interlocks and sensor feedback at each transition.

---

### **Model Answer for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — 3-Tier Architecture [7 Marks]**

The SCADA-based robotic pick and place system is organized into three hierarchical tiers:

**Tier 1 — Field Level (Sensor & Actuator Layer):**
- **Input sensors:** Inductive proximity sensor (part present), reed switch (gripper position), vacuum pressure switch, home limit switch, E-Stop NC contact, photo-electric sensor.
- **Output actuators:** 5/2 solenoid valve (gripper), conveyor motor contactor/VFD, indicator lamps (Red/Yellow/Green), servo drive enable.
- **Communication:** Hardwired 24V DC digital I/O, 4-20 mA analog for pressure transducers.

**Tier 2 — Control Level (PLC + Robot Controller):**
- **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller):** Executes the ladder logic / state machine. Performs Boolean sequencing of I/O. Holds the tag database mirror.
- **Robot Controller:** Receives high-level move commands (Move to Pick_X, Move to Place_X). Performs inverse kinematics, trajectory interpolation, and servo loop closure.
- **Communication:** Fieldbus protocols such as **EtherCAT, Profinet, or Modbus TCP** between PLC and Robot Controller.

**Tier 3 — Supervisory Level (SCADA / HMI):**
- **Industrial PC** running SCADA software (WinCC, iFIX, Wonderware, Ignition).
- **Tag Database** mirrors PLC memory.
- **HMI Screens:** Mimic diagrams, soft-keys, alarm logs, trends, recipes.
- **Communication:** Ethernet (TCP/IP) using OPC UA or Modbus TCP.

**Block Diagram (Already shown in SECTION 4.1 above — students should redraw with proper labels for full marks).**

#### **Part (b) — Pick and Place State Machine [7 Marks]**

The sequence is implemented as a **6-state Finite State Machine** as derived in SECTION 3.1:

1. **S0 — HOME/IDLE:** Robot at home. Waits for `Start_PB ∧ Estop_OK ∧ Part_Present`.
2. **S1 — APPROACH:** Robot moves to pre-pick coordinate (Z + 50 mm above part). Transition requires `Position_Reached` and 0.5 s settling timer.
3. **S2 — GRASP:** Robot descends, gripper closes. Transition requires `Pressure_Switch_OK` and 1.0 s dwell. **Failure to grasp → FAULT state, not S3.**
4. **S3 — RETRACT:** Robot lifts to safe Z. Gripper status **re-checked** (drop detection).
5. **S4 — PLACE MOVE:** Robot traverses to place coordinates using joint or linear interpolation.
6. **S5 — RELEASE:** Robot descends, gripper opens, 0.5 s dwell.
7. **S6 — RETURN HOME:** Robot returns to home, cycle counter increments, returns to S0.

**Critical Safety Interlocks (each worth 1 mark):**
- `Estop_OK` checked in every state.
- `Gripper_Closed_OK` re-validated after retract.
- `Robot_Home` required to permit START.
- Watchdog timer on every transition (typical 5 s).

---

### **Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the **SCADA HMI screen layout** for a pick and place cell. Explain each section's purpose.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Prepare an **I/O address mapping table** showing the SCADA tag, PLC address, type, and the connected sensor/actuator for a typical pick and place robot.

---

### **Model Answer for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — SCADA HMI Screen Layout [7 Marks]**

A well-engineered SCADA screen is divided into **6 functional zones** (refer to SECTION 4.3):

1. **Header Bar (Top, 10% screen height):** Shows production counter (`Cycle_Counter`), current shift, operator name, and system time. *Purpose: At-a-glance production status.*

2. **Alarm Banner (Below Header, 5%):** Yellow when warnings exist, Red for critical. Displays the highest-priority active alarm. *Purpose: Immediate fault visibility without navigating menus.*

3. **Central Mimic (Middle, 60%):** 2D or 3D animated representation of the robot, conveyor, pick station, and place station. The robot icon moves according to live `X, Y, Z` coordinates. Limit switches and gripper state are color-coded. *Purpose: Spatial situational awareness.*

4. **Soft-Key Panel (Right or Bottom, 15%):** Operator command buttons: **START, STOP, HOME, RESET, ACK (Acknowledge Alarm), JOG, MODE Select (Auto/Manual)**. *Purpose: Command dispatch to PLC.*

5. **Status Bar (Bottom, 5%):** Displays current mode (AUTO/MANUAL), cycle state (S0–S6), and communication link status (PLC link green/red). *Purpose: System health at a glance.*

6. **Trend Chart (Toggle Tab):** Real-time plot of `Cycle_Time_s` over the last 100 cycles, with average and ±3σ lines. *Purpose: Performance monitoring and predictive maintenance.*

**Design Principles:**
- Use **ISA-101 HMI Standards**: high contrast, minimal animation, grey for inactive, green for healthy, red for fault.
- Critical alarms must be visible without any user action.

#### **Part (b) — I/O Address Mapping Table [7 Marks]**

| # | SCADA Tag | PLC Address | Type | Connected To | Purpose |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `Robot_At_Home` | %I0.0 | BOOL DI | Robot Home Limit Switch (NC) | Confirms home position |
| 2 | `Part_Present_Pick` | %I0.1 | BOOL DI | Inductive Proximity Sensor | Detects part at pick |
| 3 | `Part_Present_Place` | %I0.2 | BOOL DI | Photo-electric Sensor | Detects fixture at place |
| 4 | `Gripper_Closed_OK` | %I0.3 | BOOL DI | Pressure Switch (Vacuum/PNE) | Confirms grasp |
| 5 | `Estop_OK` | %I0.4 | BOOL DI | E-Stop NC Loop | Safety interlock |
| 6 | `Start_PB` | %I0.5 | BOOL DI | HMI START Soft-Key | Cycle initiation |
| 7 | `Stop_PB` | %I0.6 | BOOL DI | HMI STOP Soft-Key | Cycle abort |
| 8 | `Robot_Cycle_Done` | %I0.7 | BOOL DI | Robot Controller DO | Cycle complete flag |
| 9 | `Conveyor_Run` | %Q0.0 | BOOL DO | VFD Enable Terminal | Material handling |
| 10 | `Gripper_Close` | %Q0.1 | BOOL DO | 5/2 Solenoid Valve Y1 | End-effector actuate |
| 11 | `Robot_Start` | %Q0.2 | BOOL DO | Robot Controller DI | Trigger robot program |
| 12 | `Tower_Lamp_Green` | %Q0.3 | BOOL DO | Green Lamp | Healthy indication |
| 13 | `Tower_Lamp_Red` | %Q0.4 | BOOL DO | Red Lamp | Fault indication |
| 14 | `Cycle_Counter` | %MW10 | INT Memory | Internal Counter | Production count |
| 15 | `Cycle_Time_s` | %MW12 | REAL Memory | Internal Timer | Performance metric |
| 16 | `Fault_Code` | %MW14 | INT Memory | Robot Alarm Word | Diagnostics |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the E-Stop:** Many students draw elaborate control logic but omit the hardware-based E-Stop loop. KTU awards a **mandatory 2-mark deduction** if E-Stop is not in every safety discussion.
> 2. **Conflating SCADA with PLC:** Writing "SCADA turns on the gripper" is wrong. SCADA writes a **tag**, PLC energizes the **output**. Examiners are strict on this hierarchy.
> 3. **No dwell times:** Students describe a grasp without specifying the **time delay** to wait for pneumatic actuation. Always mention **0.5–1.0 s dwell**.
> 4. **No re-validation of gripper after retract:** This is the most missed safety step. Always mention that after the robot retracts, the gripper sensor is **re-read** to detect part drop.
> 5. **Wrong protocol:** Don't say "SCADA connects to the robot via USB" — use **Modbus TCP / OPC UA / Profinet / EtherCAT**.
> 6. **No ISA-101 compliance:** When drawing HMI screens, mention at least **grey for inactive, green for healthy, red for fault** to score the HMI design mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **SCADA = Supervisor ONLY** — it never directly drives motors. The PLC is the executor.
- ✅ **3-Tier Hierarchy:** **Field I/O → PLC/Robot Controller → SCADA HMI.**
- ✅ **Pick and Place FSM** has **6 states**: HOME → APPROACH → GRASP → RETRACT → PLACE_MOVE → RELEASE → RETURN.
- ✅ **Safety Interlocks** must be checked in **every** state: `Estop_OK`, `Gripper_OK`, `Robot_Home`.
- ✅ **Dwell Times:** **0.5 s** for approach/release, **1.0 s** for grasp (pneumatic lag compensation).
- ✅ **Re-validate gripper status AFTER retract** — this is the most-missed safety step.
- ✅ **Communication Protocols:** **Modbus TCP, Profinet, EtherCAT, OPC UA** — never "USB" or "Wi-Fi" for industrial SCADA.
- ✅ **HMI Design:** follow **ISA-101** — grey inactive, green healthy, red fault, yellow warning.
- ✅ **I/O Mapping:** SCADA tags ↔ PLC addresses (BOOL/INT/REAL). Always include `Cycle_Counter` and `Cycle_Time_s` as REAL tags.
- ✅ **Cycle Time Formula:** $T_{cycle} = t_{approach} + t_{grasp} + t_{retract} + t_{traverse} + t_{place} + t_{return}$.
- ✅ **Throughput Formula:** $\text{Parts/hr} = \eta \cdot \dfrac{3600}{T_{cycle}}$, where $\eta \approx 0.85$.
- ✅ **E-Stop** is **hardwired** (NC loop) directly to the robot controller and PLC — it bypasses SCADA for Category 0 safety.
- ✅ **SCADA Alarms** must be **prioritized** — Critical, High, Medium, Low — and **acknowledged** by the operator.
- ✅ **Watchdog Timer:** every state transition must have a timeout (typically **5 s**); if exceeded, the system faults.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Acquisition Systems - Sampling Theorem - Sampling and Digitizing - Aliasing - Sample and Hold Circuit - Signal Conditioning of Strain Gauge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT604 - PLC & DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Acquisition Systems - Sampling Theorem - Sampling and Digitizing - Aliasing - Sample and Hold Circuit - Signal Conditioning of Strain Gauge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Data Acquisition System (DAS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Term):** A **Data Acquisition System (DAS)** is an integrated assembly of sensors, signal-conditioning circuits, multiplexers, sample-and-hold amplifiers, analog-to-digital converters (ADCs), and digital interfacing logic that converts real-world physical parameters into a digitized form suitable for processing, storage, transmission, or display by a computer or PLC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a doctor checking the heartbeat of a patient. The doctor does not record every single microscopic vibration of the heart — instead, he periodically presses the stethoscope to capture a few representative pulses per second. A **Data Acquisition System does the exact same thing** for industrial signals: it *samples* a continuous (analog) voltage or current from sensors at regular time intervals and converts these *snapshots* into numbers the computer can understand.

---

### 1.2 Sampling Theorem (Nyquist–Shannon Theorem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** The **Sampling Theorem** states that a band-limited analog signal containing no frequency components higher than $f_m$ Hz can be reconstructed completely from its discrete samples **provided that the sampling frequency $f_s$ is at least twice the highest signal frequency**, i.e., $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. The minimum allowable sampling rate, $f_s = 2 f_m$, is called the **Nyquist Rate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of a movie reel. A film that plays 24 still pictures per second looks smooth to the human eye because the eye cannot distinguish motion faster than about 12 changes per second. Similarly, if a signal's highest wiggle rate is 100 Hz, then taking 200 snapshots per second is *just enough* to "fool" the system into reconstructing the original wiggle. Go slower than 200, and the wiggle will look completely different — this distortion is called **aliasing**.

---

### 1.3 Sampling and Digitizing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** **Sampling** is the process of capturing instantaneous amplitude values of a continuous-time signal at discrete, uniformly spaced time instants (governed by a sampling clock). **Digitizing** (or **Quantization & Encoding**) is the subsequent process of assigning a finite-precision binary number to each captured amplitude level so it can be processed by digital systems.

---

### 1.4 Aliasing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** **Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which a high-frequency signal component, after being sampled below the Nyquist rate, masquerades as a lower-frequency signal in the reconstructed output. The aliased frequency is mathematically given by $f_{alias} = \vert f_{signal} - N \cdot f_s \vert$, where $N$ is the integer nearest to the ratio $f_{signal}/f_s$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Have you ever noticed that in films, a spinning car wheel sometimes appears to rotate *backward*? This is **aliasing in vision**. The camera captures 24 frames per second, but the wheel rotates faster than half that rate. Your brain interprets the under-sampled motion as a slower reverse rotation. In DAS, the same physical phenomenon produces *false low-frequency readings* from a *real high-frequency disturbance*.

---

### 1.5 Sample and Hold Circuit (S/H)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit** is an analog memory device that briefly connects its input to a charging capacitor during the *sampling (tracking) phase*, and then disconnects the input, holding the capacitor voltage constant during the *hold phase*, so that the downstream ADC has a stable, unchanging voltage to digitize.

---

### 1.6 Signal Conditioning of Strain Gauge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** **Signal Conditioning for a Strain Gauge** refers to the front-end analog processing required to convert the very small resistance change of a strain gauge (typically a fraction of an ohm) into a usable, noise-immune, scaled, and amplified voltage signal — most commonly implemented using a **Wheatstone Bridge** followed by an **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A strain gauge's resistance changes so slightly (often by less than 0.1 %) when bent that no ordinary voltmeter can detect it. The **Wheatstone Bridge** acts like a finely balanced "see-saw" of four resistors — any tiny disturbance (the strain) tilts the balance, producing a measurable voltage difference. The **instrumentation amplifier** then magnifies that tiny tilt into a strong, clean signal ready for the ADC.

---

### 1.7 Block-Level Summary of a Complete DAS

A standard DAS signal chain follows this physical order:

**Sensor → Signal Conditioner → Multiplexer → Sample & Hold → ADC → Digital Interface → Host (PC/PLC)**

Each block exists to solve a specific engineering problem: noise rejection, channel selection, time-stability, quantization, and communication.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Foundation of the Sampling Theorem

The sampling theorem rests on **three foundational pillars**:

1. **Band-limited assumption:** The input signal $x(t)$ must contain no frequency components above $f_m$ Hz.
2. **Impulse-train multiplication:** Sampling is mathematically modelled as multiplying the analog signal $x(t)$ by a periodic train of unit impulses $\delta_T(t)$ spaced $T_s = 1/f_s$ seconds apart.
3. **Frequency-domain replication:** This multiplication produces an infinite *replicas* (spectral images) of the original spectrum $X(f)$, centred at every integer multiple of $f_s$.

The *non-overlap condition* of these replicas is what guarantees perfect reconstruction. This condition is:

$$f_s - f_m \;\geq\; f_m \quad\Longrightarrow\quad f_s \;\geq\; 2 f_m$$

- If $f_s < 2 f_m$: the spectral replicas **overlap**, and overlap energy cannot be separated — this overlap region is the **aliasing distortion band**.
- If $f_s \geq 2 f_m$: the replicas remain **disjoint**, and a low-pass filter (the reconstruction filter) can perfectly isolate the original baseband.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In real industrial DAS, the sampling rate is always chosen **3× to 10× higher** than the Nyquist minimum, not exactly 2×. The extra margin (called the *oversampling factor* or *guard band*) protects against unexpected high-frequency noise and anti-aliasing filter roll-off imperfections.

---

### 2.2 Quantization and Digitizing

Once a sample is held at amplitude $V_{sample}$, the ADC divides its full input range $[0, V_{ref}]$ into $2^n$ discrete levels (where $n$ = number of bits). The **resolution (LSB size)** is:

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

The quantization error is bounded by $\pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1}}$. This introduces an irreducible noise known as **Quantization Noise**, whose RMS value is $\Delta V / \sqrt{12}$.

---

### 2.3 Aliasing — The "False Frequency" Mechanism

Given a true signal at $f_{true}$ and a sampling frequency $f_s$, the apparent frequency after reconstruction is found by subtracting the largest integer multiple of $f_s$ that brings the result inside $[0, f_s/2]$:

$$f_{apparent} = \left\vert f_{true} - N \cdot f_s \right\vert, \quad N = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{f_{true}}{f_s}\right)$$

**Anti-Aliasing Filter:** A sharp analog low-pass filter placed *before* the sampler restricts the input bandwidth to $f_s/2$. This is the *only* hardware safeguard; no software can remove aliasing once it has occurred.

---

### 2.4 Sample and Hold — Why It Is Mandatory for ADC

An ADC requires a *finite conversion time* (often microseconds to milliseconds). If the input voltage were allowed to fluctuate during this window, the digital output would be ambiguous (different bits would correspond to different instantaneous voltages). The S/H circuit **freezes** the input during conversion so that one stable value is mapped to one unique binary code.

**Two critical timing parameters:**
- **Acquisition Time ($t_{acq}$):** Time taken by the output to settle within the ADC's required accuracy band after switching from hold to sample.
- **Aperture Time ($t_{ap}$):** The small but finite delay between the hold command and the actual disconnection of the input. The *aperture uncertainty* (jitter) directly limits the maximum slew rate the system can faithfully capture.

The maximum input slew rate trackable by an S/H of bandwidth $B$ is:

$$\left.\frac{dV}{dt}\right|_{max} = 2\pi B \cdot V_{FS}$$

---

### 2.5 Strain Gauge and Wheatstone Bridge Signal Conditioning

A strain gauge's resistance changes according to:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$$

where $G_f$ is the **gauge factor** (typically ~2.0 for metallic gauges, ~100+ for semiconductor gauges) and $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ is the strain.

A single active gauge placed in one arm of a **Wheatstone Bridge** excited by $V_{ex}$ produces a differential output:

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$$

For higher sensitivity and **temperature compensation**, four active gauges are used (two in tension, two in compression). The bridge output becomes:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$$

— a factor of **4 improvement** in sensitivity compared to the quarter-bridge configuration.

The bridge output is fed to an **Instrumentation Amplifier** with gain $G_{IA}$, giving a final conditioned signal:

$$V_{cond} = G_{IA} \cdot V_{out}$$

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Condition | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Band-limited signal assumed | Hz |
| 2 | Sampling Period | $T_s = 1 / f_s$ | $T_s < 0.5 / f_m$ | seconds |
| 3 | Oversampling Factor | $K = f_s / (2 f_m)$ | Engineering choice $K = 3$ to $10$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Alias Frequency | $f_a = \vert f - N f_s \vert$ | $N = \text{round}(f/f_s)$ | Hz |
| 5 | ADC Resolution (LSB) | $\Delta V = V_{ref} / 2^n$ | $n$ = number of bits | Volts |
| 6 | Max Quantization Error | $e_q = \pm \Delta V / 2$ | Mid-rise quantizer | Volts |
| 7 | RMS Quantization Noise | $V_{q,rms} = \Delta V / \sqrt{12}$ | Uniform distribution assumption | Volts |
| 8 | SNR of Ideal ADC | $SNR = 6.02 n + 1.76$ dB | Full-scale sine input | dB |
| 9 | S/H Aperture Limit | $dV/dt_{max} = 2\pi B V_{FS}$ | Limited by bandwidth $B$ | V/s |
| 10 | Strain Gauge Equation | $\Delta R / R = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ | $G_f \approx 2$ (metal), $100+$ (semi) | dimensionless |
| 11 | Quarter-Bridge Output | $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ | One active gauge | Volts |
| 12 | Full-Bridge Output | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ | Four active gauges | Volts |
| 13 | Conditioned Output | $V_{cond} = G_{IA} \cdot V_{out}$ | After IA stage | Volts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Utility:** These equations govern everything from a USB-based thermocouple logger in a food-processing plant to the 24-bit sigma-delta ADC inside a weighing scale, to the strain-gauge-based load cells in an aircraft landing gear, to the high-speed vibration DAS used in turbine health monitoring.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Spectral Replication During Sampling

Consider a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ with Fourier Transform $X(f)$. Sampling is modelled as multiplication by an impulse train $\delta_T(t)$:

$$\delta_T(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - k T_s)$$

The sampled signal is therefore:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \delta_T(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k T_s)\, \delta(t - k T_s)$$

Taking the Fourier Transform (using the convolution theorem — multiplication in time ↔ convolution in frequency with $\frac{1}{T_s}\sum \delta(f - k f_s)$):

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

The sampled spectrum is therefore a periodic repetition of $X(f)$ scaled by $1/T_s$ and shifted by every integer multiple of $f_s$. For non-overlap of these replicas, the right edge of the baseband spectrum at $+f_m$ must not cross the left edge of the next replica at $(f_s - f_m)$:

$$f_s - f_m \;\geq\; f_m \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; f_s \;\geq\; 2 f_m$$

This is the rigorous mathematical origin of the Nyquist criterion.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Alias Frequency Formula

Let the true input frequency be $f_{true}$, and let the sampling frequency be $f_s$. We seek the apparent (folded) frequency $f_a$ such that $f_a \in [0, f_s/2]$.

Step 1 — Compute how many half-cycles of $f_s$ the true signal exceeds:

$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{f_{true}}{f_s} + \frac{1}{2} \right\rfloor \quad \text{(nearest integer)}$$

Step 2 — Compute the difference:

$$f_{raw} = f_{true} - N \cdot f_s$$

Step 3 — If $f_{raw}$ is negative, fold it back:

$$f_a = \vert f_{raw} \vert$$

**Numerical Example:** $f_{true} = 230 \text{ Hz}$, $f_s = 100 \text{ Hz}$.

$$N = \text{round}(230 / 100) = 2$$

$$f_{raw} = 230 - 2 \cdot 100 = +30 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\therefore f_a = 30 \text{ Hz}$$

So a 230 Hz signal sampled at 100 Hz **falsely appears as 30 Hz** in the reconstructed output. This is **aliasing**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Wheatstone Bridge Output (Quarter-Bridge)

Let the four arms of the bridge be $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ with excitation $V_{ex}$ applied across the top–bottom nodes. The differential output (between the two midpoints) is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex}\left[ \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} \right]$$

For a **quarter-bridge**, three arms are fixed at $R$ and one arm is the active strain gauge $R + \Delta R$. Substituting $R_1 = R + \Delta R$ and $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex}\left[ \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{R}{2R} \right]$$

The first term expands as:

$$\frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} = \frac{R(1 + \Delta R/R)}{2R(1 + \Delta R/(2R))} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1 + \Delta R/R}{1 + \Delta R/(2R)}$$

Using the binomial approximation $\frac{1+x}{1+y} \approx 1 + (x-y)$ for small $x, y$:

$$\approx \frac{1}{2}\left[ 1 + \frac{\Delta R}{R} - \frac{\Delta R}{2R} \right] = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{\Delta R}{4R}$$

The second term is exactly $\frac{1}{2}$. Subtracting:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

Substituting the strain-gauge relation $\Delta R / R = G_f \varepsilon$:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4}\, G_f\, \varepsilon}$$

This confirms the standard quarter-bridge sensitivity factor of $V_{ex}/4$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Full-Bridge Output (4 Active Gauges)

When all four arms are active and configured such that two gauges elongate (tension) and two compress — a typical bending-beam configuration — the changes are $+\Delta R, +\Delta R, -\Delta R, -\Delta R$. Plugging into the generic bridge equation and re-evaluating without small-signal approximation:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex}\left[ \frac{R+\Delta R}{(R+\Delta R)+(R-\Delta R)} - \frac{R-\Delta R}{(R+\Delta R)+(R-\Delta R)} \right]$$

The denominators simplify to $2R$, giving:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex}\left[ \frac{R+\Delta R}{2R} - \frac{R-\Delta R}{2R} \right] = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{2 \Delta R}{2R} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

Substituting $\Delta R / R = G_f \varepsilon$:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = V_{ex}\, G_f\, \varepsilon}$$

Exactly a 4× gain over the quarter-bridge — and this is **exact**, with no small-signal approximation required.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Maximum Trackable Slew Rate of Sample-and-Hold

The sample-and-hold amplifier has an effective closed-loop bandwidth $B$ (Hz). When tracking a sinusoid $V(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$, the maximum rate of change occurs at the zero crossing:

$$\left.\frac{dV}{dt}\right|_{max} = 2\pi f \cdot V_p$$

For this to remain within the amplifier's linear tracking bandwidth, we must have $f \leq B$. At full-scale $V_p = V_{FS}$, the maximum slew rate trackable is:

$$\left.\frac{dV}{dt}\right|_{max} = 2\pi B \cdot V_{FS}$$

A practical consequence: for a 1 MHz, $\pm 10$ V S/H stage, the maximum slew rate is $2\pi \cdot 10^6 \cdot 10 \approx 62.8$ V/µs. Any signal whose zero-crossing slope exceeds this value will be **distorted even before A/D conversion**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Data Acquisition System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensorA[Physical Sensor] --> condA[Signal Conditioner]
    condA --> muxA[Analog Multiplexer]
    sensorB[Sensor Channel 2] --> condB[Signal Conditioner B]
    condB --> muxA
    sensorC[Sensor Channel 3] --> condC[Signal Conditioner C]
    condC --> muxA
    muxA --> shA[Sample and Hold Circuit]
    shA --> adcA[Analog to Digital Converter]
    adcA --> ifaceA[Digital Interface Logic]
    ifaceA --> hostA[Host Computer or PLC]
    clkA[Master Sampling Clock] --> shA
    clkA --> adcA
    hostA --> ctlA[Channel Select Control]
    ctlA --> muxA
```

**Functional Description of the Flow:**

- **Physical Sensor** converts the physical parameter (temperature, strain, pressure) into an electrical signal (resistance, voltage, current).
- **Signal Conditioner** amplifies, linearises, filters, and isolates the sensor output.
- **Analog Multiplexer** selects one of many input channels under host control.
- **Sample and Hold** captures and stabilizes the voltage for the ADC.
- **ADC** performs quantization and encoding into binary code.
- **Digital Interface** transmits the binary data to the host via parallel bus, serial port, USB, or Ethernet.
- **Master Sampling Clock** orchestrates the S/H and ADC timings.

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Sample and Hold Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vinA[Analog Input Vin] --> swA[Electronic Switch MOSFET]
    swA --> bufA[Buffer Amplifier]
    bufA --> holdA[Holding Capacitor CH]
    holdA --> outA[Output to ADC]
    ctlA[Control Logic Sample or Hold] --> swA
    holdA --> buf2A[Output Buffer High Input Z]
    buf2A --> outA
```

**Operational Phases:**

- **Sample phase:** Switch closed → capacitor charges to track $V_{in}$.
- **Hold phase:** Switch opens → capacitor retains last voltage; high-impedance buffer prevents discharge.

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Strain Gauge Signal Conditioning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    gaugeA[Strain Gauge Rg] --> bridgeA[Wheatstone Bridge]
    refR1[Fixed Resistor R] --> bridgeA
    refR2[Fixed Resistor R] --> bridgeA
    refR3[Fixed Resistor R] --> bridgeA
    excA[Excitation Voltage Vex] --> bridgeA
    bridgeA --> diffA[Differential Output Vdiff]
    diffA --> iaA[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    iaA --> filtA[Low Pass Anti Alias Filter]
    filtA --> outA[Conditioned Output to ADC]
```

**Functional Purpose:** The bridge produces a tiny differential voltage proportional to strain; the instrumentation amplifier (typically a three-op-amp configuration) provides high common-mode rejection (CMRR > 100 dB) and programmable gain (10 to 1000); the low-pass filter blocks any out-of-band interference before sampling.

---

### 4.4 Aliasing Visualization — Frequency Domain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sigA[Original Signal Spectrum centred at 0 Hz bounded by fm] --> sampA[Sampling Operation]
    sampA --> repA[Spectrum Repeated at every integer multiple of fs]
    repA --> okA[Replicas Do NOT Overlap when fs greater than 2fm]
    sampA --> badA[Replicas OVERLAP when fs less than 2fm ALIASING]
    okA --> recoA[Perfect Reconstruction via Low Pass Filter]
    badA --> errA[Irrecoverable Distortion]
```

This is the conceptual reason why **no software filter can remove aliasing** — the energy of overlapping frequencies is mathematically merged and cannot be separated back into the original components.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*State the Sampling Theorem. What is the Nyquist rate?*

**Model Answer:**

> The **Sampling Theorem** (Shannon–Nyquist) states that a band-limited continuous-time signal having no frequency components above $f_m$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its discrete samples if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. The minimum allowable sampling frequency $f_s = 2 f_m$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*What is aliasing? How is it prevented in a Data Acquisition System?*

**Model Answer:**

> **Aliasing** is the distortion phenomenon where a high-frequency signal, when sampled below the Nyquist rate, appears as a false lower frequency in the reconstructed output. It is prevented by placing a sharp **analog low-pass anti-aliasing filter** *before* the sampler, restricting the input bandwidth to $f_s/2$. Once aliasing occurs, it cannot be corrected by digital means.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, Module 4]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical Data Acquisition System and describe the function of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the operation of a Sample-and-Hold circuit. Derive the expression for the maximum trackable input slew rate. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DAS Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 above)

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| Sensor | Converts physical quantity (strain, temperature) into electrical signal |
| Signal Conditioner | Amplification, filtering, linearization, isolation |
| Multiplexer | Sequential selection of multiple input channels |
| Sample and Hold | Captures and stabilises a voltage snapshot |
| ADC | Quantizes and encodes the analog value into binary |
| Digital Interface | Transfers binary data to host via bus/USB/Ethernet |
| Host / PLC | Storage, display, analysis, control |

(Complete block diagram explanation: **7 marks** distributed across architecture, function of each block, and justification of order.)

**(b) Sample-and-Hold Operation:**

The S/H has two phases controlled by an external logic signal:

- **Sample (tracking) phase:** The electronic switch (MOSFET/JFET) closes, connecting the input to the holding capacitor $C_H$ through a buffer amplifier. The capacitor voltage follows the input voltage exactly.

- **Hold phase:** The switch opens. The capacitor is now isolated; its charge is preserved. A high-input-impedance unity-gain output buffer prevents capacitor discharge due to loading.

**Why S/H is essential:** ADCs require a stable input during conversion (microseconds to milliseconds). Without S/H, aperture error arises.

**Maximum Trackable Slew Rate Derivation:**

For a sinusoidal input $V_{in}(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$:

$$\frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = 2\pi f V_p \cos(2\pi f t)$$

The maximum magnitude occurs at the zero crossing:

$$\left.\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}\right|_{max} = 2\pi f V_p$$

The S/H amplifier has a finite closed-loop bandwidth $B$. For faithful tracking, $f \leq B$. At full-scale ($V_p = V_{FS}$):

$$\boxed{\left.\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}\right|_{max} = 2\pi B\, V_{FS}}$$

This sets the upper bound on input signal frequency the S/H can capture without slewing-induced distortion. **(7 marks)** for the operation phases, the necessity justification, and the full derivation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the Wheatstone-bridge-based signal conditioning of a strain gauge. Derive the output voltage for both quarter-bridge and full-bridge configurations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A strain gauge has a nominal resistance of $120\ \Omega$ and gauge factor $2.1$. It is bonded to a steel cantilever whose strain is $800\ \mu\varepsilon$. The gauge is connected in a quarter-bridge with excitation $5\ \text{V}$. The output is fed to an instrumentation amplifier of gain 250. Find:
- (i) The change in gauge resistance $\Delta R$.
- (ii) The bridge output voltage $V_{out}$.
- (iii) The final conditioned output voltage $V_{cond}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to Section 3.3 and Section 3.4 above for the rigorous derivations.

- Quarter-bridge: $\quad V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$
- Full-bridge: $\quad V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ (4× higher sensitivity)

The full-bridge gives exact output without small-signal approximation. **(7 marks)** for diagram, derivation steps, and physical interpretation.

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

Given: $R = 120\ \Omega$, $G_f = 2.1$, $\varepsilon = 800 \times 10^{-6} = 8 \times 10^{-4}$, $V_{ex} = 5\ \text{V}$, $G_{IA} = 250$.

**(i) Change in resistance:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon = 2.1 \times 8 \times 10^{-4} = 1.68 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\Delta R = 120 \times 1.68 \times 10^{-3} = 0.2016\ \Omega \approx 0.202\ \Omega$$

**(ii) Bridge output (quarter-bridge):**

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon = \frac{5}{4} \times 1.68 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$V_{out} = 1.25 \times 1.68 \times 10^{-3} = 2.10 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} = 2.10\ \text{mV}$$

**(iii) Conditioned output:**

$$V_{cond} = G_{IA} \cdot V_{out} = 250 \times 2.10 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$V_{cond} = 0.525\ \text{V} = 525\ \text{mV}$$

**Final Answer:** $\Delta R \approx 0.202\ \Omega$, $V_{out} = 2.10$ mV, $V_{cond} = 525$ mV. **(7 marks)** distributed across the three sub-parts and final interpretation.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 1/4** in the quarter-bridge output — many students write $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ (the full-bridge formula) and lose **2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Confusing Nyquist Rate with Nyquist Frequency:** Nyquist Rate = $2 f_m$ (sampling frequency), Nyquist Frequency = $f_m$ (signal's highest frequency). Examiners check this distinction strictly.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention the anti-aliasing filter** when asked "how to prevent aliasing" — stating "increase sampling rate" alone is *incomplete*. A real-world system has hardware filter + oversampling.
> 4. **Skipping the S/H justification:** Writing only the S/H circuit diagram without explaining *why* an ADC needs a held voltage (aperture error) costs at least 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Unit errors in strain problems:** $\mu\varepsilon$ must be converted to dimensionless $\varepsilon$ (multiply by $10^{-6}$) before plugging into formulas.
> 6. **Oversimplifying the full-bridge derivation:** Full-bridge output is *exact*, not approximated — students who write "approximately $V_{ex} G_f \varepsilon$" lose a step-marker.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **DAS signal chain order:** Sensor → Signal Conditioner → Multiplexer → Sample & Hold → ADC → Digital Interface → Host.
- **Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$; minimum value is the **Nyquist Rate**.
- **Practical sampling rate:** Choose $3 f_m$ to $10 f_m$ (oversampling factor).
- **Aliasing:** Folded frequency $f_a = \vert f - N f_s \vert$; prevented *only* by analog low-pass filter placed before the sampler.
- **Quantization LSB:** $\Delta V = V_{ref} / 2^n$.
- **SNR of ideal N-bit ADC:** $6.02 N + 1.76$ dB.
- **Sample-and-Hold phases:** Sample (switch closed, tracking) and Hold (switch open, frozen); required to give ADC a stable voltage.
- **S/H maximum slew rate:** $2\pi B V_{FS}$.
- **Strain Gauge Law:** $\Delta R / R = G_f \varepsilon$; typical $G_f \approx 2$ (metal), $>100$ (semiconductor).
- **Quarter-bridge output:** $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} G_f \varepsilon$ (approximate, small-strain).
- **Full-bridge output:** $V_{out} = V_{ex} G_f \varepsilon$ (exact, 4× higher sensitivity, with temperature compensation).
- **Signal Conditioning chain:** Wheatstone Bridge → Instrumentation Amplifier → Low-Pass Anti-Aliasing Filter → ADC.
- **Reconstruction of original signal:** Low-pass filter with cut-off $f_m$ applied to the sampled spectrum — only works if Nyquist criterion is satisfied.
- **Engineering rule of thumb:** Always design the **anti-aliasing filter cut-off** slightly below $f_s/2$ to account for non-ideal filter roll-off.
- **In the KTU exam:** Always label axes, units, and formula references; show derivation steps; never write "approximately" without computing the actual value.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LVDT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT604 - PLC & DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** LVDT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

The **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** is a passive inductive transducer that converts **linear mechanical displacement** into a corresponding **electrical voltage signal**. It operates on the principle of mutual induction between a primary winding and two symmetrically placed secondary windings, with a movable soft-iron core acting as the magnetic flux coupler.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim recall expected):**
> "An LVDT is an electromechanical transducer used to convert rectilinear motion of an object into a corresponding electrical signal. It consists of a primary coil and two secondary coils wound on a cylindrical former, with a movable ferromagnetic core separating the secondary windings."

## Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **seesaw balance scale** with two pans, but instead of weights, the pans are connected to magnetic coils. Now picture a **magnet (the core)** sliding along a rod. As the magnet moves right, it pulls energy toward the right coil; as it moves left, energy shifts to the left coil. The difference between the two coils' responses is what tells us **how far** and **in which direction** the magnet moved.

**Real-world analogy:** Think of a **bicycle odometer's cable** — but instead of mechanical rotation, LVDT uses invisible magnetic coupling. There are **no touching parts** (no friction, no wear), making it incredibly durable for measuring piston positions inside heavy industrial machinery.

## Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard excitation frequency:** **1 kHz to 10 kHz** (typically 5 kHz)
- **Operating range:** **±0.1 mm to ±250 mm**
- **Linearity:** **±0.25%** of full scale
- **Resolution:** **Infinite** (theoretically)
- **Sensitivity:** Typically **0.5 V/mm to 5 V/mm**
- **Core material:** Soft iron or **nickel-iron alloy (mu-metal)** for high permeability ($\mu_r \approx 50{,}000$)

> [!NOTE]
> The word **"Differential"** is the heart of LVDT's brilliance — it means the output is the **mathematical difference** between the two secondary voltages, which automatically cancels out common-mode noise and temperature drift.

## Why LVDT Belongs in Data Acquisition Systems (DAS)

In a modern PLC-based automation chain, field sensors like the LVDT act as the **first link** in the signal conditioning pipeline. The voltage output is then:
1. Rectified (AC → DC)
2. Linearized
3. Calibrated
4. Fed to a **PLC analog input module** (e.g., 0–10 V or 4–20 mA)
5. Used for **closed-loop PID control** of position, force, or pressure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION_2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Constructional Anatomy — Structural Breakdown

| Component | Description | Engineering Role |
|-----------|-------------|------------------|
| **Primary Coil (P)** | Wound at the **center** of a hollow cylindrical former | Carries the AC excitation current ($V_p$) |
| **Secondary Coil 1 ($S_1$)** | Wound on the **left** half of the former | Induces voltage $E_{s1}$ proportional to coupling with core |
| **Secondary Coil 2 ($S_2$)** | Wound on the **right** half of the former | Induces voltage $E_{s2}$ proportional to coupling with core |
| **Movable Core** | Soft ferromagnetic cylinder, attached to the measured object | Channels magnetic flux unequally to the two secondaries |
| **Former/Tube** | Non-magnetic (brass/aluminum) hollow cylinder | Holds coils, isolates them from environment |
| **End Shields** | End caps with guide rods | Constrains core motion along a single axis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two secondary coils are **connected in series opposition** (i.e., their polarities are reversed with respect to each other). This is the **differential connection** that gives the LVDT its name.

## Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Working Principle

### Step 1: Null Position (Core Centered)
- Core is geometrically **exactly at the center** of the primary coil.
- Mutual inductance $M_1$ (with $S_1$) **equals** mutual inductance $M_2$ (with $S_2$).
- Induced EMFs: $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$ (equal in magnitude, but **opposite in phase** due to series opposition).
- **Net differential output:** $E_0 = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = 0$ volts.

### Step 2: Core Displaced Towards $S_1$ (Upward Displacement)
- The flux linkage with $S_1$ **increases** (more core inside $S_1$).
- The flux linkage with $S_2$ **decreases** (core has moved away from $S_2$).
- Therefore: $E_{s1} > E_{s2}$.
- **Net output:** $E_0 = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = $ **positive** (in phase with primary).

### Step 3: Core Displaced Towards $S_2$ (Downward Displacement)
- The flux linkage with $S_2$ **increases**.
- The flux linkage with $S_1$ **decreases**.
- Therefore: $E_{s2} > E_{s1}$.
- **Net output:** $E_0 = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = $ **negative** (180° out of phase with primary).

> [!NOTE]
> **Direction detection** is achieved through **phase-sensitive demodulation** (PSD). The phase of $E_0$ relative to the primary excitation voltage reveals the **sign of displacement**.

## KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board Exam Critical)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---------|---------|------|
| 1 | $E_{s1} = E_s\left(1 + \dfrac{x}{X}\right)$ | EMF in $S_1$ at displacement $x$ | Volts |
| 2 | $E_{s2} = E_s\left(1 - \dfrac{x}{X}\right)$ | EMF in $S_2$ at displacement $x$ | Volts |
| 3 | $E_0 = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = \dfrac{2 E_s \, x}{X}$ | Net differential output | Volts |
| 4 | $K = \dfrac{dE_0}{dx} = \dfrac{2 E_s}{X}$ | Sensitivity of LVDT | V/mm or V/cm |
| 5 | $V_p = 4.44 \, f \, N_p \, \phi_m$ | Primary excitation EMF equation | Volts |
| 6 | $E_0 = V_p \cdot \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot k \cdot \dfrac{x}{X}$ | Generalized output in terms of turns ratio | Volts |

Where:
- $E_s$ = Secondary EMF at null position
- $x$ = Instantaneous core displacement from null
- $X$ = Maximum (full-scale) displacement
- $N_p, N_2$ = Number of turns in primary and secondary
- $f$ = Excitation frequency (Hz)
- $k$ = Coupling coefficient
- $\phi_m$ = Maximum magnetic flux (Weber)

## Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|----------|-------------|
| **Aerospace** | Position feedback of **control surfaces** (ailerons, flaps) |
| **Civil** | Deflection monitoring in bridges and dams |
| **Hydraulics** | Piston position in **servo cylinders** |
| **Manufacturing** | **Dimensional metrology** of machined parts |
| **Power Plants** | Valve stem position feedback |
| **Automotive** | Suspension travel sensors |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION_3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Mathematical Model of the LVDT Output Voltage

### Assumption Set
- The two secondary coils are wound with **identical number of turns** $N_2$.
- They are placed **symmetrically** about the primary coil.
- The core is a **uniform soft-iron cylinder** of permeability $\mu$.
- The flux linkage is **linearly proportional** to the core's overlap with the secondary coil (valid over the linear range).

### Analytical Walkthrough

Let the maximum half-stroke of the core be $X$ (i.e., the core can move from $-X$ to $+X$ from the null).

**Step 1: Define the secondary EMFs at null position.**
At null ($x = 0$), the core is centered and induces an equal EMF $E_s$ in each secondary:
$$E_{s1}\big\vert_{x=0} = E_{s2}\big\vert_{x=0} = E_s$$

**Step 2: Express the EMFs as linear functions of displacement.**
Assuming linearity over the operating range, the EMF in $S_1$ increases linearly with positive $x$, while the EMF in $S_2$ decreases linearly:

$$E_{s1} = E_s \left(1 + \frac{x}{X}\right)$$

$$E_{s2} = E_s \left(1 - \frac{x}{X}\right)$$

**Step 3: Apply the differential (series opposition) connection.**
Since the two secondaries are connected in **series opposition**, the net output voltage is the difference:

$$E_0 = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$$

**Step 4: Substitute and simplify.**

$$E_0 = E_s \left(1 + \frac{x}{X}\right) - E_s \left(1 - \frac{x}{X}\right)$$

**Step 5: Expand the brackets.**

$$E_0 = E_s + \frac{E_s \, x}{X} - E_s + \frac{E_s \, x}{X}$$

**Step 6: Cancel the $+E_s$ and $-E_s$ terms.**

$$E_0 = \frac{2 E_s \, x}{X}$$

**Step 7: Final compact form.**

$$\boxed{E_0 = \frac{2 E_s \, x}{X} = K \cdot x}$$

where the sensitivity $K = \dfrac{2 E_s}{X}$ (in V/mm or V/cm).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This linear relationship is precisely why LVDTs are favored in **precision position control** loops. The output voltage is **directly proportional** to displacement, eliminating the need for complex linearization algorithms in the PLC.

## Derivation 2: Sensitivity in Terms of Excitation Frequency

### Use Case
A KTU board problem often asks: *"Calculate the sensitivity if the excitation frequency is changed."*

### Starting Point — Transformer EMF Equation
For the primary winding of the LVDT:
$$V_p = 4.44 \, f \, N_p \, \phi_m$$

The induced EMF in each secondary (at null, assuming full coupling) is:
$$E_s = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \phi_m$$

Therefore, the sensitivity becomes:
$$K = \frac{2 E_s}{X} = \frac{2 \cdot 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \phi_m}{X} = \frac{8.88 \, f \, N_2 \, \phi_m}{X}$$

### Interpretation
$$K \propto f$$

**Doubling the excitation frequency doubles the sensitivity**, which is why modern LVDTs are driven at **5 kHz–10 kHz** to maximize output without resorting to excessive turns-count (which would make the device bulky).

## Derivation 3: Phase Relationship for Direction Sensing

The LVDT's ability to detect **direction** comes from a critical observation:

- When $x > 0$ (core in $S_1$ region): $E_0 = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$ → $E_0$ is **in phase** with $V_p$.
- When $x < 0$ (core in $S_2$ region): $E_0 = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$ → $E_0$ is **180° out of phase** with $V_p$.

A **phase-sensitive demodulator (PSD)** compares $E_0$ with $V_p$ (reference) and outputs a **signed DC voltage**:

$$V_{\text{out,DC}} = \vert E_0 \vert \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

where $\theta$ is the phase angle between $E_0$ and $V_p$ ($\theta = 0°$ for positive $x$, $\theta = 180°$ for negative $x$).

This yields:
$$V_{\text{out,DC}} = \begin{cases} +K \cdot \vert x \vert & \text{if } x > 0 \\ -K \cdot \vert x \vert & \text{if } x < 0 \end{cases}$$

## Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Style)

> **Problem:** An LVDT has a full-scale range of $\pm 20$ mm and an output of **3.5 V** at null frequency. The core is displaced by **+8 mm**. Find the differential output voltage and the sensitivity.

### Given Data
- Full-scale $X = 20$ mm
- $E_s = 3.5$ V
- $x = +8$ mm

### Solution

**Step 1: Compute the secondary EMFs.**

$$E_{s1} = 3.5 \times \left(1 + \frac{8}{20}\right) = 3.5 \times 1.4 = 4.9 \text{ V}$$

$$E_{s2} = 3.5 \times \left(1 - \frac{8}{20}\right) = 3.5 \times 0.6 = 2.1 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Compute the differential output.**

$$E_0 = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = 4.9 - 2.1 = 2.8 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Compute the sensitivity.**

$$K = \frac{2 E_s}{X} = \frac{2 \times 3.5}{20} = 0.35 \text{ V/mm}$$

**Step 4: Verify using the direct formula.**

$$E_0 = \frac{2 \times 3.5 \times 8}{20} = \frac{56}{20} = 2.8 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> The characteristic curve of an LVDT is a **straight line passing through the origin** within the linear range. Outside this range, the curve **flattens** (saturation) — this is why the operating range is strictly limited to within $\pm X$.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION_5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define LVDT. State any two advantages of LVDT over a potentiometric transducer.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** is a passive inductive transducer used to convert linear displacement into a proportional AC voltage output. It consists of a primary coil and two secondary coils wound on a cylindrical former, with a movable soft-iron core that varies the mutual inductance between the primary and the two secondaries.

**Two advantages over a potentiometer:**
1. **Frictionless operation** — the core does not touch the coils, eliminating mechanical wear and ensuring infinite mechanical life.
2. **Infinite resolution** — since output is generated by electromagnetic induction (not by contact resistance), there is no granularity; even sub-micron displacements produce measurable output.

*(Word limit: 80–100 words for full 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Explain how the direction of core displacement is detected in an LVDT.**

**Model Answer:**

The direction of core displacement is detected using a **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (PSD)**. When the core is displaced toward $S_1$ ($x > 0$), the output voltage $E_0$ is **in phase** with the primary excitation voltage. When the core is displaced toward $S_2$ ($x < 0$), the output is **180° out of phase** with the primary. The PSD compares $E_0$ with a reference signal derived from the primary excitation and produces a **signed DC voltage** — positive for one direction, negative for the other. This signed DC is then sent to the PLC's analog input for directional position control.

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working principle of an LVDT**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **output voltage of an LVDT** and define its **sensitivity**. An LVDT has a full-scale range of $\pm 25$ mm and produces 5 V at null. Find the output when the core is displaced by **+12 mm**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
The LVDT consists of a single primary coil (P) wound at the center of a hollow cylindrical former, and two identical secondary coils ($S_1$ and $S_2$) wound symmetrically on either side. The two secondaries are connected in **series opposition** (differential connection). A movable **soft-iron core**, coupled to the object under measurement, slides freely inside the former. The whole assembly is housed in a metallic shield to prevent external magnetic interference.

**Working (3.5 marks):**
The primary is energized by an **AC excitation** (1–10 kHz). When the core is at the **null (central) position**, equal and opposite EMFs are induced in $S_1$ and $S_2$, giving **zero differential output**. When the core moves toward $S_1$, the flux linkage with $S_1$ increases, $E_{s1} > E_{s2}$, and the output is in phase with the primary. When the core moves toward $S_2$, $E_{s2} > E_{s1}$, and the output is 180° out of phase. The **magnitude** of the output indicates displacement, while the **phase** indicates direction — decoded by a phase-sensitive demodulator.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Derivation (3.5 marks):**
At null position, let the EMF in each secondary be $E_s$. For a displacement $x$ (from null, with full-scale $X$):

$$E_{s1} = E_s \left(1 + \frac{x}{X}\right), \quad E_{s2} = E_s \left(1 - \frac{x}{X}\right)$$

The differential output is:

$$E_0 = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = \frac{2 E_s \, x}{X}$$

**Sensitivity** is defined as the rate of change of output voltage with displacement:

$$K = \frac{dE_0}{dx} = \frac{2 E_s}{X} \quad \text{(V/mm)}$$

**Numerical Solution (3.5 marks):**
- Given: $X = 25$ mm, $E_s = 5$ V, $x = +12$ mm
- Sensitivity:

$$K = \frac{2 \times 5}{25} = 0.4 \text{ V/mm}$$

- Output voltage:

$$E_0 = K \cdot x = 0.4 \times 12 = 4.8 \text{ V}$$

(Equivalently: $E_{s1} = 5(1 + 12/25) = 7.4$ V; $E_{s2} = 5(1 - 12/25) = 2.6$ V; $E_0 = 7.4 - 2.6 = 4.8$ V ✓)

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Internal Choice Alternative]

**(a)** Discuss the **characteristics, advantages, and disadvantages** of LVDT. Explain its application in a **PLC-based position control system**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (PSD)** is used with an LVDT. Explain its **working principle** with a block diagram and justify why it is essential. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Characteristics (2 marks):**
- **Linear output:** Within the operating range, $E_0$ is directly proportional to $x$.
- **Infinite resolution:** No mechanical contact → no granularity.
- **Low hysteresis** (< 0.1% FS).
- **High sensitivity:** 0.5–5 V/mm typical.
- **Wide frequency response:** DC to several kHz.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. Frictionless, hence long life and high reliability.
2. Infinite resolution and high sensitivity.
3. Output is electrically isolated from the input.
4. Insensitive to temperature variations (within design range).
5. Robust — can operate in harsh environments.

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
1. Requires AC excitation source.
2. Sensitive to strong external magnetic fields.
3. Larger in size compared to strain gauges for small displacements.

**Application in PLC-based position control (2 marks):**
The LVDT is mounted on a hydraulic cylinder. The core is linked to the piston. The LVDT's AC output is rectified, linearized, and scaled (4–20 mA) into a PLC analog input module. The PLC compares the actual position with the **setpoint** from the SCADA HMI and generates a **PID output** to a proportional valve, closing the loop for precise position control.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
A Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (PSD) is a rectifier whose output polarity depends on the **phase** of the input signal relative to a reference. The block diagram consists of:
1. **Multipliers** (typically analog multipliers or XOR logic) that multiply the LVDT output $E_0$ with a square-wave reference derived from the primary excitation.
2. A **low-pass filter** that extracts the DC component.
3. An **amplifier** that scales the DC to standard levels (0–10 V).

**Working:** When the LVDT output is in phase with the reference, the multiplier output is positive → DC output is positive. When 180° out of phase, the multiplier output is negative → DC output is negative. The magnitude is proportional to displacement.

**Why it is essential (3 marks):**
1. **Direction detection:** A simple rectifier cannot distinguish positive from negative displacement — PSD can.
2. **Noise rejection:** Synchronous detection rejects noise at all frequencies except the excitation frequency.
3. **Linearity preservation:** The DC output is linearly related to displacement, suitable for direct ADC input in a PLC/DAS.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common mistake:** Students often write $E_0 = E_{s1} + E_{s2}$ (additive) instead of $E_0 = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$ (differential). The **series opposition** is the heart of LVDT — getting the sign wrong costs **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Phase confusion:** Forgetting that the output is **in phase** for one direction and **180° out of phase** for the other. The phase-sensitive demodulator is what decodes direction — do **not** skip this in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 3. **Sensitivity units:** Always quote sensitivity with units (V/mm or V/cm). A bare numerical answer loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **Diagram etiquette:** In KTU 2024 scheme, diagrams must be **neatly labeled** with all components (P, $S_1$, $S_2$, core, former). Skipping the **former** label costs marks.
> 5. **Numerical care:** Mixing up $X$ (full-scale) with $x$ (instantaneous) in the formula is the #1 reason for full-mark loss in numerical sub-questions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Full form:** Linear Variable Differential Transformer — a **passive, inductive** transducer.
- **Core principle:** **Mutual induction** between primary and two secondaries; output = **difference** of two induced EMFs.
- **Construction:** One primary (central) + two secondaries (symmetric) + movable soft-iron core on a hollow former.
- **Connection:** Secondaries are in **series opposition** (differential) — **NOT additive**.
- **Excitation:** **AC** (1–10 kHz) to the primary; output is also **AC** initially.
- **Null position:** Core at center → $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$ → $E_0 = 0$ V.
- **Displacement direction:** Detected by **phase of output** (in-phase vs 180° out-of-phase).
- **Displacement magnitude:** Proportional to $|E_0|$, decoded by **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (PSD)**.
- **Master formula:** $E_0 = \dfrac{2 E_s \, x}{X} = K \cdot x$, where $K = \dfrac{2 E_s}{X}$.
- **Sensitivity units:** V/mm or V/cm.
- **Sensitivity ↑ with frequency:** $K \propto f$, hence high-frequency excitation is preferred.
- **Resolution:** Theoretically **infinite** (no contact, no friction).
- **Range:** $\pm 0.1$ mm to $\pm 250$ mm (varies with design).
- **Key advantages:** Frictionless, infinite resolution, high sensitivity, robust, electrically isolated.
- **Key disadvantages:** Needs AC excitation, sensitive to stray magnetic fields, bulkier than strain gauges.
- **Major applications:** Hydraulic piston position, aerospace control surfaces, bridge deflection monitoring, dimensional metrology, valve position feedback in PLC/DAS loops.
- **Signal chain:** LVDT → PSD → LPF → DC signal → PLC analog input → PID control.
- **DAS relevance:** Acts as the **primary sensor** in many PLC-based position/force/pressure control loops, with the PLC serving as the data acquisition and control unit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermocouple
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT604 - PLC & DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thermocouple

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermocouple (TC):** A thermoelectric temperature transducer consisting of **two dissimilar metal conductors joined at one end (measuring/hot junction)** such that the **Seebeck EMF generated** at the open end (reference/cold junction) is a **reproducible function of the temperature difference** between the two junctions, as referenced to standard tables defined by the **International Temperature Scale of 1990 (ITS-90)**.

In the KTU 2024 context of **PLC & Data Acquisition Systems (PBMRT604)**, a thermocouple is classified as a **passive analog transducer** belonging to the family of **thermoelectric sensors**. It converts a thermal quantity (temperature) into a measurable electrical quantity (millivolt-level DC voltage) and is one of the most widely used industrial sensors in the field, owing to its **wide measurement range (-200 °C to +2300 °C)**, robustness, and low cost.

### 1.1.1 The Three Thermoelectric Effects (Foundational Trio)

The operation of a thermocouple relies on three interrelated phenomena:

1. **Seebeck Effect (1821, Thomas Johann Seebeck):** The direct conversion of a temperature gradient into an electromotive force (EMF) at the junction of two dissimilar conductors.
2. **Peltier Effect (1834, Jean Charles Athanase Peltier):** The reverse phenomenon, where passing an electric current through a thermocouple junction causes heating or cooling.
3. **Thomson Effect (1856, William Thomson / Lord Kelvin):** The heating or cooling that occurs in a single homogeneous conductor when an electric current flows through it in the presence of a temperature gradient.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Seebeck Effect** is the *exploited principle* in all thermocouple-based temperature measurement, while the Peltier and Thomson effects are typically considered as **secondary phenomena** that contribute small corrections to the ideal behavior.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure-Cooker Whistle" Intuition

Imagine a **pressure cooker whistle** that blows a musical note whose pitch depends on how hot the stove beneath the cooker is. The whistle is **not a thermometer**, but its pitch (an easily measurable signal) **tracks the temperature** accurately.

A thermocouple works on exactly this same intuitive idea:

| Pressure-Cooker Whistle Analogy | Thermocouple Equivalent |
|---------------------------------|-------------------------|
| Heat from the stove | Temperature at measuring junction ($T_H$) |
| Pitch of the whistle | Generated Seebeck EMF ($E$) in millivolts |
| Cool ambient air around whistle | Temperature at reference junction ($T_C$) |
| Listener's ear measuring pitch | Voltmeter / DAQ analog input module |
| Pitch chart provided by manufacturer | NIST/ITS-90 thermocouple reference tables |

Just as a hot stove produces a high-pitched whistle *only because* the ambient air (the "reference") is cool, a thermocouple produces a voltage *only because* of the **temperature difference** between the hot measuring junction and the cold reference junction. **Heat a thermocouple uniformly — both junctions at 500 °C — and the output voltage collapses to zero.** This is the single most important conceptual point for the KTU examination.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Materials and Calibration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All thermocouple voltages are tabulated with respect to a **reference junction temperature of 0 °C (273.15 K)** held in a **Triple Point of Water (TPW) cell** or an **ice-bath reference**. The reference tables are issued by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** under the **ITS-90 (International Temperature Scale of 1990)** standard.

### 1.4 Industrial-Grade Thermocouple Anatomy

A practical industrial thermocouple consists of:

- **Two thermoelements (legs/wires):** Each made of a precisely alloyed metal.
- **Measuring (Hot) Junction:** Formed by **welding, brazing, or twisting** the two wires. Typical junctions are of types *bead*, *grounded*, *ungrounded*, or *exposed*.
- **Sheath / Probe:** A protective metallic or ceramic tube (often Inconel or SS316) housing the thermoelements, filled with **MgO (Magnesium Oxide)** powder for vibration damping and electrical insulation.
- **Extension / Compensating Wires:** Used to route the millivolt signal from the probe head to the PLC/DAQ terminal block, made of cheaper alloys that approximate the thermoelectric properties of the original thermocouple over a limited range.
- **Termination Head / Cold-End Block:** A junction box containing terminal blocks and (in modern systems) an integrated **PT100 RTD** for cold-junction compensation (CJC).

> [!NOTE]
> **ITS-90 Reference Tables** are required for converting the measured microvolt-level signal to temperature. These tables list the **Seebeck EMF (in μV)** for each thermocouple type at every 1 °C step, with the cold junction held at **exactly 0 °C (32 °F)**.

### 1.5 The Seebeck Coefficient (Material Property)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Seebeck Coefficient ($S$ or $\alpha$):** Defined as the **rate of change of thermoelectric EMF with respect to temperature** at a given operating point, expressed in **microvolts per degree Celsius (μV/°C)**. It is an **intrinsic material property** of the conductor pair.

Mathematically,

$$S_{AB}(T) = \frac{dE_{AB}}{dT}\bigg|_{T}$$

where $E_{AB}$ is the thermoelectric EMF generated by the pair (A, B) and $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin. Typical values range from **5 μV/°C to 80 μV/°C** depending on the thermocouple type.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of the Seebeck Effect

The Seebeck effect arises from the **diffusion of charge carriers** (electrons in N-type materials, holes in P-type materials) along a temperature gradient. Hot carriers at the hot junction possess **higher kinetic energy and higher diffusion velocity** than the cool carriers at the cold junction. This asymmetric diffusion produces a **net charge accumulation** at the cold end, generating a measurable **open-circuit voltage**.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic of EMF Generation

1. **Junction Heating:** The measuring junction absorbs thermal energy, raising the local electron/hole kinetic energy.
2. **Charge Carrier Diffusion:** Higher-energy carriers diffuse *away* from the hot junction toward the cold ends of both legs, leaving behind a charge-imbalance zone.
3. **Electrostatic Equilibrium:** A **back-EMF** is established by the accumulated charge that opposes further diffusion, halting the net charge flow.
4. **Net Open-Circuit Voltage:** The terminal voltage measured at the open ends equals the algebraic sum of the two Seebeck contributions from each leg, *referenced to* the cold junction temperature.
5. **Linearization (first-order):** $E = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ is the small-signal approximation, accurate only over narrow temperature spans.

## 2.2 The Three Fundamental Thermocouple Circuit Laws

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These three laws are **explicitly examinable** in the KTU 2024 scheme. They are the theoretical foundation used to (a) predict thermocouple behavior in complex circuits and (b) explain why extension/compensating wires work.

### 2.2.1 Law of Intermediate Metals (Galvanic Insertion Law)

*Statement:* **Inserting a third metal (C) into a thermocouple circuit — provided both new junctions introduced are at the same temperature — does not alter the net Seebeck EMF of the original pair.**

**Engineering Use:** This law justifies the use of **terminal blocks, solder joints, copper lead-wires inside the PLC, and contact relays** anywhere in the thermocouple loop. As long as the *auxiliary* junctions are isothermal, they are electrically invisible.

### 2.2.2 Law of Intermediate Temperatures (Additivity Law)

*Statement:* **The EMF generated by a thermocouple with its measuring junction at $T_H$ and reference at $T_C$ is equal to the algebraic sum of the EMFs that would be generated with the reference at $T_1$ and the measuring junction at $T_1$ and $T_2$ respectively.**

**Engineering Use:** This law is the **theoretical basis of software-based Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** in the PLC/DAQ module. The instrument only needs to know $T_C$ (measured by an internal RTD) to "shift" the reference to 0 °C mathematically.

### 2.2.3 Law of Inserted Materials (Series-Compensation Law)

*Statement:* **If a section of the thermoelement is replaced by a different material, the resulting EMF error can be corrected by inserting an equal length of the same material in the opposite leg at the same temperature gradient.**

**Engineering Use:** Justifies the design of **extension and compensating cables**, which are constructed from low-cost alloys (e.g., Chromel/Alumel "extension grade" for type K) that mimic the original thermoelements over a limited compensation range (typically 0 °C to 200 °C).

## 2.3 Thermocouple Classification and ISA/ANSI Letter Codes

> [!NOTE]
> The **ISA (Instrument Society of America) / ANSI (American National Standards Institute) letter designation** is the universally accepted thermocouple naming convention. Each letter identifies a specific pair of alloys, with characteristic temperature range, sensitivity, and atmospheric tolerance.

| Type | Positive Leg (TP) | Negative Leg (TN) | Range (°C) | Sensitivity (μV/°C) | Atmosphere / Notes |
|------|-------------------|-------------------|------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **B** | Pt-30%Rh | Pt-6%Rh | 0 to 1820 | 5 to 10 | Oxidizing/Inert; high-temp furnaces |
| **E** | Chromel (Ni-Cr) | Constantan (Cu-Ni) | -270 to +1000 | 60 to 80 | Highest sensitivity; oxidizing |
| **J** | Iron (Fe) | Constantan (Cu-Ni) | -210 to +1200 | 50 to 60 | Reducing/vacuum; cheap; prone to rust |
| **K** | Chromel (Ni-Cr) | Alumel (Ni-Al) | -270 to +1372 | 40 to 50 | General purpose; most popular; oxidizing |
| **N** | Nicrosil (Ni-Cr-Si) | Nisil (Ni-Si-Mg) | -270 to +1300 | 35 to 45 | Improved stability over K; high-temp |
| **R** | Pt-13%Rh | Platinum (Pt) | -50 to +1768 | 8 to 12 | Lab standard; oxidizing |
| **S** | Pt-10%Rh | Platinum (Pt) | -50 to +1768 | 8 to 12 | Lab standard; oxidizing |
| **T** | Copper (Cu) | Constantan (Cu-Ni) | -270 to +400 | 38 to 50 | Cryogenic; food/pharma; reducing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type K (Chromel–Alumel)** is the **de facto industrial default** in PLC/DAQ applications and is the type most frequently referenced in KTU questions because of its wide availability, low cost, and broad range.

## 2.4 Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC) — The Practical Imperative

> [!WARNING]
> A real industrial thermocouple **never** has its cold junction at 0 °C. The cold junction is at the **terminal block inside the PLC or DAQ module**, which may be at 30 °C to 70 °C in a typical industrial cabinet. **Without CJC, the indicated temperature will be in error by exactly the cold-junction temperature offset.** CJC is *not optional*.

### 2.4.1 The CJC Equation (Law of Intermediate Temperatures)

$$E_{TC}(T_H, 0\,°C) = E_{TC}(T_H, T_C) + E_{TC}(T_C, 0\,°C)$$

In words: *the EMF referenced to 0 °C* equals *the measured EMF referenced to $T_C$* plus *the tabulated EMF that would exist if the hot junction were at $T_C$ and the reference at 0 °C*. The PLC/DAQ performs this addition **in firmware** using an internal **PT100 or PT1000 RTD** mounted on the input terminal block.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula set** for thermocouple problems. Memorize the units and boundary conditions precisely.

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Values / Units |
|---|----------|-------------|------------------------|
| 1 | $E = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | First-order Seebeck law (small signal) | $\alpha$ in μV/°C, $\Delta T$ in °C |
| 2 | $S(T) = dE/dT$ | Seebeck coefficient (instantaneous sensitivity) | 5–80 μV/°C |
| 3 | $E_{0 \to T} = E_{0 \to T_H} - E_{0 \to T_C}$ | Reference-shift formula (CJC) | $E$ in μV or mV |
| 4 | $E(T) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} a_i T^i$ | NIST ITS-90 polynomial model | 8th–14th order; $T$ in °C |
| 5 | $\Delta T_{err} = T_C - T_{ref}$ | Cold-junction offset error if CJC is absent | Equals CJC sensor reading |
| 6 | $E_{AB} = -E_{BA}$ | Reversibility (swap leads → invert polarity) | Sign convention |
| 7 | $E_{AC} = E_{AB} + E_{BC}$ | Additivity of Seebeck EMFs (Law of Intermediate Metals) | Vector/tensor sum |

> [!CAUTION]
> **CRITICAL KTU PITFALL:** The vertical pipe `|` is reserved by markdown for table-cell delimiters. When writing **absolute value** or **conditional expressions** in any subsequent calculation row, **use the LaTeX vertical bar `\vert`** instead of a literal pipe.

## 2.6 Integration with PLC and DAQ Systems

In a typical KTU-focused PLC/DAQ architecture, the thermocouple signal flow is:

1. **Field Sensor (Thermocouple Probe):** Generates μV-level DC voltage.
2. **Extension / Compensating Cable:** Routes the millivolt signal to the cabinet, with a transition junction box (TJB) at the field end.
3. **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC) Sensor:** Internal PT100 RTD bonded to the input screw terminal.
4. **Differential Instrumentation Amplifier:** Provides **>80 dB CMRR** to reject 50/60 Hz common-mode noise picked up in long cable runs.
5. **Low-Pass Anti-Aliasing Filter:** Cuts off above the Nyquist frequency of the ADC.
6. **24-bit Sigma-Delta ADC:** Resolves the μV-level signal to digital codes with effective resolution of **~0.025 °C (Type K)**.
7. **Linearization Engine:** Applies the NIST polynomial to convert μV → °C with CJC added.
8. **Engineering Unit Output:** Engineering value (°C, K, or °F) written to the PLC process image.

> [!TIP]
> **Typical PLC Modules for Thermocouple Input:** Siemens SM 1231 TC, Allen-Bradley 5069-IF8, Schneider BMX ART 0414. These modules integrate the amplifier, CJC, ADC, and linearization into a single field-replaceable unit.
